topics.title: Introduction to Biomedical Instrumentation: Overview, Basic Principles and Concepts, Source of Bioelectric Potentials, Resting and Action Potential, Propagation of Action Potentials, Bioelectric Potentials Example (ECG, EMG)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMRT412 - BIOMEDICAL INSTRUMENTATION > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Introduction to Biomedical Instrumentation: Overview, Basic Principles and Concepts, Source of Bioelectric Potentials, Resting and Action Potential, Propagation of Action Potentials, Bioelectric Potentials Example (ECG, EMG) # SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Biomedical Instrumentation: Formal Definition > [!NOTE] > **Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard)** > **Biomedical Instrumentation** is the interdisciplinary branch of engineering that deals with the design, development, measurement, and analysis of instruments, devices, and software used to acquire, process, and interpret physiological signals from living organisms for the purposes of diagnosis, monitoring, therapy, and biomedical research. It is an integration of multiple engineering disciplines (electronics, electrical, mechanical, computer science, chemical) with life sciences (anatomy, physiology, biochemistry) to build **Medical Instruments** and **Biomedical Equipment** that interface with the human body. ### Scope Hierarchy The subject encompasses four primary functional zones: 1. **Measurement / Sensing Zone** — Acquiring biopotentials (ECG, EMG, EEG, EOG, ERG). 2. **Signal Conditioning Zone** — Amplification, filtering, isolation, A/D conversion. 3. **Display / Recording Zone** — Monitors, CRO, recorders, PC-based displays. 4. **Therapeutic / Intervention Zone** — Pacemakers, defibrillators, ventilators, infusion pumps. ## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy > [!IMPORTANT] > **Analogy — "The Car Dashboard System"** > Imagine the **human body as a car** and **biomedical instruments as the dashboard**. Just as a dashboard shows engine temperature, fuel level, and oil pressure through sensors and displays, biomedical instruments translate hidden internal body variables (heart rhythm, muscle activity, brain waves) into readable electrical signals. Without the dashboard, you would not know what is happening inside the engine. Similarly, without biomedical instrumentation, doctors cannot "see" the internal electrical and chemical activity of the body. ## 1.3 Why Biomedical Instrumentation? — The Driving Needs - **Non-invasive diagnosis** without surgical exploration. - **Continuous monitoring** of critically ill patients (ICU, CCU, NICU). - **Quantitative assessment** replacing subjective clinical judgment. - **Long-term recording** for trend analysis (Holter monitors, sleep studies). - **Life-support and life-saving** interventions (defibrillators, ventilators). - **Rehabilitation and prosthetic engineering** (artificial limbs, cochlear implants). ## 1.4 Standard Physiological Signal Metrics (KTU High-Yield Data) > [!NOTE] > **Key Biopotential Signal Metrics** | Signal | Source | Amplitude (Typical) | Frequency Range | |---|---|---|---| | **ECG** (Electrocardiogram) | Heart muscle | **0.5 – 5 mV** | **0.05 – 150 Hz** | | **EMG** (Electromyogram) | Skeletal muscle | **0.1 – 5 mV** | **20 – 5000 Hz** | | **EEG** (Electroencephalogram) | Brain | **5 – 300 µV** | **0.5 – 100 Hz** | | **EOG** (Electro-oculogram) | Eye movement | **0.05 – 3.5 mV** | **DC – 50 Hz** | | **ERG** (Electroretinogram) | Retina | **0.005 – 1 mV** | **0.2 – 200 Hz** | | **PCG** (Phonocardiogram) | Heart sounds | — | **20 – 2000 Hz** | ## 1.5 Basic Principles and Concepts The four foundational principles governing every biomedical instrument are: 1. **Transduction Principle** — Conversion of a physiological variable (non-electrical) into an electrical signal using a transducer. Example: piezoelectric crystal converting mechanical vibration (heart sound) into voltage. 2. **Amplification Principle** — Biopotentials are inherently low-amplitude (µV to mV). High-gain differential amplifiers are used with high **CMRR (Common Mode Rejection Ratio)** preferably **≥ 100 dB** and input impedance **≥ 5 MΩ** to prevent signal loading. 3. **Isolation Principle** — The patient must be electrically isolated from the AC mains using **isolation amplifiers** (typically with **isolation resistance > 10¹² Ω** and **leakage current < 10 µA**) for **patient safety** against microshock and macroshock. 4. **Calibration and Standardization Principle** — Instruments must be calibrated against known physiological or electrical standards (e.g., 1 mV calibration pulse in ECG). # SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Source of Bioelectric Potentials > [!IMPORTANT] > **Core Concept — Origin of Bioelectricity** > All bioelectric potentials originate from the **electrical activity of excitable cells** (nerve, muscle, glandular). These cells maintain a **voltage difference across their semi-permeable plasma membrane** due to the unequal distribution of ions (Na⁺, K⁺, Cl⁻, Ca²⁺) between the **intracellular fluid (ICF)** and **extracellular fluid (ECF)**. ### 2.1.1 The Cellular Membrane — Structure The cell membrane is a **lipid bilayer (≈ 70 Å thick)** with embedded **ion-selective protein channels** and **ion pumps**. The lipid core is highly resistive, giving the membrane a high **resistivity of ~10⁹ Ω·cm**, while the ion channels provide selective conductive pathways. ### 2.1.2 Ionic Distribution Across the Membrane | Ion | Intracellular (mM) | Extracellular (mM) | Equilibrium Potential (Nernst) | |---|---|---|---| | **K⁺** | 150 | 4 | **−90 mV** | | **Na⁺** | 12 | 145 | **+61 mV** | | **Cl⁻** | 4 | 120 | **−89 mV** | | **Ca²⁺** | 10⁻⁴ | 1.2 | **+129 mV** | The inside of the cell is **electrically negative** relative to the outside at rest. ## 2.2 Resting Membrane Potential > [!NOTE] > **Definition** > The **Resting Membrane Potential (RMP)** is the steady-state potential difference (~ **−70 mV** to **−90 mV**) that exists across the plasma membrane of an excitable cell when it is NOT being stimulated. The convention is: outside = 0 V reference, inside = negative. ### Mechanism — Why is the Inside Negative? 1. **Na⁺/K⁺ ATPase Pump** actively pumps **3 Na⁺ out** and **2 K⁺ in** per ATP hydrolyzed. This creates an ionic concentration gradient. 2. At rest, the membrane is **~25 times more permeable to K⁺ than to Na⁺**. 3. K⁺ ions diffuse out through **leak channels** following their concentration gradient. 4. Loss of positive charge from inside → **negative interior**. 5. Equilibrium is reached when electrical force pulling K⁺ back in = chemical force pushing K⁺ out. ## 2.3 The Nernst Equation (Foundation of Biophysics) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Nernst Equation** — Calculates the equilibrium potential for a single ion species across a permeable membrane. $$E_{ion} = \frac{RT}{zF} \ln\left(\frac{[C]_{outside}}{[C]_{inside}}\right) = \frac{2.303 \, RT}{zF} \log_{10}\left(\frac{[C]_{outside}}{[C]_{inside}}\right)$$ **Where:** - $E_{ion}$ = Equilibrium (Nernst) potential in Volts - $R$ = Universal gas constant = **8.314 J/(mol·K)** - $T$ = Absolute temperature in Kelvin (K = °C + 273.15) - $z$ = Valency of the ion - $F$ = Faraday's constant = **96,485 C/mol** - $[C]$ = Concentration (molar) of the ion **At body temperature T = 310 K (37°C):** $$E_{ion} = \frac{61.5 \, \text{mV}}{z} \log_{10}\left(\frac{[C]_{out}}{[C]_{in}}\right)$$ ## 2.4 Goldman-Hodgkin-Katz (GHK) Equation > [!NOTE] > The Nernst equation considers only **one ion**. Real membranes are permeable to multiple ions. The **GHK equation** gives the actual resting membrane potential considering Na⁺, K⁺, and Cl⁻ permeability. $$V_m = \frac{RT}{F} \ln\left(\frac{P_K[K^+]_{out} + P_{Na}[Na^+]_{out} + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_{in}}{P_K[K^+]_{in} + P_{Na}[Na^+]_{in} + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_{out}}\right)$$ Where $P_K$, $P_{Na}$, $P_{Cl}$ are the **permeability coefficients** of the respective ions. ## 2.5 Action Potential — The Electrical Impulse > [!IMPORTANT] > **Definition** > An **Action Potential (AP)** is a rapid, transient, self-propagating depolarization and repolarization of the membrane potential of an excitable cell, lasting typically **1 to 2 milliseconds** in neurons, used for signal transmission. ### The Five Phases of an Action Potential | Phase | Event | Voltage Change | Ion Movement | Channel Type | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Resting** | Polarized | −70 mV | K⁺ leak out | Leak channels | | **Depolarization** | Rising phase | −70 mV → +30 mV | Na⁺ rushes IN | **Voltage-gated Na⁺** opens | | **Overshoot** | Peak | +30 to +40 mV | — | Na⁺ channel inactivates | | **Repolarization** | Falling phase | +30 mV → −80 mV | K⁺ flows OUT | **Voltage-gated K⁺** opens | | **Hyperpolarization** | Undershoot | −80 mV → −70 mV | Continued K⁺ efflux | K⁺ channels slowly close | | **Return to Resting** | Na⁺/K⁺ pump restores ionic gradients back to original | | | Active transport | ### 2.5.1 Threshold Potential A stimulus must depolarize the membrane to the **threshold potential (≈ −55 mV)** to trigger an **all-or-none action potential**. Below threshold → **graded (local) potential**. At or above threshold → **full action potential**. ## 2.6 Propagation of Action Potentials > [!NOTE] > **Definition** > **Propagation** is the unidirectional spread of the action potential along the nerve axon or muscle fiber. Local current loops from the active (depolarized) region depolarize the adjacent resting membrane to threshold, regenerating the AP. ### Velocity of Propagation $$v \propto \sqrt{\frac{d}{R_i \cdot C_m}}$$ Where: - $d$ = axon diameter - $R_i$ = internal axial resistance - $C_m$ = membrane capacitance **Key factors that increase velocity:** - **Larger axon diameter** (giant squid axon) - **Myelination** (saltatory conduction) — increases velocity from **~10 m/s (unmyelinated)** to **~120 m/s (myelinated)** - **Lower membrane capacitance** ### Conduction Modes 1. **Continuous Conduction** — In unmyelinated axons, AP propagates point-to-point smoothly. Velocity = **0.5 to 2 m/s**. 2. **Saltatory Conduction** — In myelinated axons, AP "jumps" between **Nodes of Ranvier** (≈ every 1–2 mm). Velocity = **5 to 120 m/s**. ## 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Mandatory Formulas for KTU Examination (Numerical Problems)** | Formula | Use / Application | Unit / Constant | |---|---|---| | $E_{ion} = \frac{61.5}{z} \log_{10}\left(\frac{[C]_{out}}{[C]_{in}}\right)$ | Single-ion equilibrium potential | Volts, T = 310 K | | $E_{ion} = \frac{RT}{zF}\ln\left(\frac{[C]_{out}}{[C]_{in}}\right)$ | General Nernst equation (any T) | Volts | | $V_m = 61.5 \log_{10}\left(\frac{P_K[K^+]_{out} + P_{Na}[Na^+]_{out} + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_{in}}{P_K[K^+]_{in} + P_{Na}[Na^+]_{in} + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_{out}}\right)$ | Resting potential (multiple ions) | mV | | $v \propto \sqrt{\frac{d}{R_i C_m}}$ | Propagation velocity | m/s | | $v_{myelinated} \approx 6 \times v_{unmyelinated}$ | Saltatory vs continuous | m/s | | $I = \frac{V}{R}$, $V = IR$ | Ohm's law for biopotential | Amps, Ohms, Volts | | $\text{CMRR} = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{A_d}{A_c}\right)$ | Amplifier selectivity | dB | **Standard body constants to memorize:** - Body temperature = **310 K (37°C)** - Faraday constant F = **96,485 C/mol** - Gas constant R = **8.314 J/(mol·K)** - Resting potential = **−70 mV** (standard) - Threshold potential = **−55 mV** - Nernst potential Na⁺ = **+61 mV** - Nernst potential K⁺ = **−90 mV** - Heart rate range = **60–100 bpm** # SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Nernst Equation from First Principles ### Step 1: Define the System Consider a membrane separating two solutions of a monovalent cation (e.g., K⁺) with concentrations $[C]_1$ (inside) and $[C]_2$ (outside). The membrane is selectively permeable to this ion. ### Step 2: Chemical Work (Driving Force) The chemical potential difference (free energy change per mole) for moving the ion from inside to outside against the concentration gradient is: $$\Delta G_{chem} = RT \ln\left(\frac{[C]_2}{[C]_1}\right)$$ ### Step 3: Electrical Work (Opposing Force) The electrical work required to move one mole of ions (charge $zF$) across a potential difference $E$ is: $$\Delta G_{elec} = zFE$$ ### Step 4: Equilibrium Condition At equilibrium, the **net free energy change is zero** (chemical and electrical forces balance): $$\Delta G_{chem} + \Delta G_{elec} = 0$$ $$RT \ln\left(\frac{[C]_2}{[C]_1}\right) + zFE = 0$$ ### Step 5: Solve for E $$zFE = -RT \ln\left(\frac{[C]_2}{[C]_1}\right) = RT \ln\left(\frac{[C]_1}{[C]_2}\right)$$ $$E = \frac{RT}{zF}\ln\left(\frac{[C]_1}{[C]_2}\right)$$ ### Step 6: Convert to Base-10 Logarithm Using the identity $\ln(x) = 2.303 \log_{10}(x)$: $$E = \frac{2.303 \, RT}{zF}\log_{10}\left(\frac{[C]_1}{[C]_2}\right)$$ ### Step 7: Substitute Body Temperature Constants At T = 310 K, evaluate the constant $\frac{2.303 \, RT}{F}$: $$\frac{2.303 \times 8.314 \times 310}{96485} = \frac{5933.7}{96485} \approx 0.0615 \text{ V} = 61.5 \text{ mV}$$ ### Step 8: Final Nernst Equation $$\boxed{E_{ion} = \frac{61.5 \, \text{mV}}{z} \log_{10}\left(\frac{[C]_{out}}{[C]_{in}}\right)}$$ ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Numerical Computation of Potassium Nernst Potential ### Given Data - $[K^+]_{in} = 150$ mM - $[K^+]_{out} = 4$ mM - $z = +1$ - T = 37°C (310 K) ### Step 1: Identify Formula $$E_{K^+} = \frac{61.5}{+1} \log_{10}\left(\frac{[K^+]_{out}}{[K^+]_{in}}\right)$$ ### Step 2: Substitute Values $$E_{K^+} = 61.5 \times \log_{10}\left(\frac{4}{150}\right)$$ ### Step 3: Compute the Logarithm $$\frac{4}{150} = 0.02667$$ $$\log_{10}(0.02667) = \log_{10}(2.667 \times 10^{-2}) = \log_{10}(2.667) + \log_{10}(10^{-2}) = 0.4260 - 2 = -1.574$$ ### Step 4: Multiply $$E_{K^+} = 61.5 \times (-1.574) = -96.8 \text{ mV}$$ ### Step 5: Interpretation The K⁺ equilibrium potential is **−96.8 mV**, close to the standard textbook value of **−90 mV**. This means if the membrane were permeable ONLY to K⁺, the inside would be **96.8 mV more negative** than outside at equilibrium. ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Sodium Equilibrium Potential ### Given Data - $[Na^+]_{in} = 12$ mM - $[Na^+]_{out} = 145$ mM - $z = +1$ ### Step 1: Apply Nernst $$E_{Na^+} = \frac{61.5}{+1} \log_{10}\left(\frac{145}{12}\right)$$ ### Step 2: Compute the Ratio $$\frac{145}{12} = 12.083$$ ### Step 3: Logarithm $$\log_{10}(12.083) = 1.0824$$ ### Step 4: Multiply $$E_{Na^+} = 61.5 \times 1.0824 = +66.6 \text{ mV}$$ ### Step 5: Interpretation The Nernst potential for Na⁺ is **+66.6 mV**, indicating that the equilibrium favors a **positive interior** if the membrane were only permeable to Na⁺. This is the driving force for the rapid depolarization phase. ## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Resting Potential via Goldman-Hodgkin-Katz Equation ### Given Data (typical nerve cell) - $P_K = 1.0$, $P_{Na} = 0.04$, $P_{Cl} = 0.45$ - $[K^+]_{in} = 140$, $[K^+]_{out} = 5$ - $[Na^+]_{in} = 10$, $[Na^+]_{out} = 142$ - $[Cl^-]_{in} = 4$, $[Cl^-]_{out} = 103$ ### Step 1: Numerator (Outside influence) $$N = (1.0)(5) + (0.04)(142) + (0.45)(4) = 5 + 5.68 + 1.8 = 12.48$$ ### Step 2: Denominator (Inside influence) $$D = (1.0)(140) + (0.04)(10) + (0.45)(103) = 140 + 0.4 + 46.35 = 186.75$$ ### Step 3: Apply GHK $$V_m = 61.5 \log_{10}\left(\frac{12.48}{186.75}\right) = 61.5 \log_{10}(0.0668)$$ ### Step 4: Logarithm $$\log_{10}(0.0668) = \log_{10}(6.68 \times 10^{-2}) = 0.8248 - 2 = -1.1752$$ ### Step 5: Multiply $$V_m = 61.5 \times (-1.1752) = -72.27 \text{ mV}$$ ### Step 6: Result The calculated resting potential is **−72.27 mV**, very close to the experimentally measured **−70 mV**. ## 3.5 Derivation 5 — ECG Waveform Timing Analysis ### Given Data A normal ECG paper speed = **25 mm/s**, calibration = **10 mm/mV**. **Standard Waveform Intervals:** | Wave/Interval | Origin / Meaning | Duration (Normal) | |---|---|---| | **P Wave** | Atrial depolarization | 60–100 ms | | **PR Interval** | Atrial depolarization + AV nodal delay | 120–200 ms | | **QRS Complex** | Ventricular depolarization | 80–120 ms | | **ST Segment** | Ventricular plateau (repolarization onset) | 80–120 ms | | **T Wave** | Ventricular repolarization | 160 ms | | **QT Interval** | Total ventricular systole | 300–430 ms | | **RR Interval** | One cardiac cycle | ~ 0.8 s at 75 bpm | ### Step 1: Compute Heart Rate from RR Interval At 25 mm/s, if the RR distance = 20 mm: $$T_{RR} = \frac{20 \, \text{mm}}{25 \, \text{mm/s}} = 0.8 \, \text{s}$$ ### Step 2: Heart Rate Formula $$\text{HR} = \frac{60}{T_{RR} \text{ (in seconds)}} = \frac{60}{0.8} = 75 \, \text{bpm}$$ ## 3.6 ECG Lead Theory — Einthoven's Triangle Einthoven's Law: $II = I + III$ (vector sum of limb leads). $$I = V_L - V_R \quad ; \quad II = V_F - V_R \quad ; \quad III = V_F - V_L$$ Where $V_R$, $V_L$, $V_F$ are potentials at the right arm, left arm, and left leg electrodes. # SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — General Block Diagram of a Biomedical Instrumentation System ```mermaid flowchart LR A[PHYSIOLOGICAL
SOURCE

Resting Potential

Point A on Axon] --> B[Depolarization

DIASTOLE TP SEGMENT] node7 -.->|Next cycle| node1 node4 --> node8[Atrial Repolarization
HIDDEN in QRS] ``` ## 4.5 Mermaid Diagram 5 — EMG Signal Acquisition Chain ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Motor Neuron
Action Potential] --> B[Neuromuscular

Amplifier] F --> G[Bandpass Filter
20 Hz to 5 kHz] G --> H[Rectifier and
