12 lead trace]:::display H --> D D --> V1 D --> V2 D --> V3 V1 --> A1 V2 --> A1 V1 --> A2 V3 --> A2 V2 --> A3 V3 --> A3 V1 --> AUG V2 --> AUG V3 --> AUG A1 --> DSP A2 --> DSP A3 --> DSP AUG --> DSP DSP --> DISP classDef bio fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B008B,stroke-width:2px classDef phys fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px classDef elec fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#8B8B00,stroke-width:1px classDef amp fill:#98FB98,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px classDef aug fill:#87CEFA,stroke:#00008B,stroke-width:2px classDef dsp fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#4B0082,stroke-width:2px classDef display fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#8B8000,stroke-width:3px ``` > [!NOTE] > **Diagram Reading Guide for KTU Exam:** In a 14-mark question, you may reproduce a simplified version of the Einthoven Triangle (mermaid graph or hand-drawn). Always label: (i) the three vertices with RA/LA/LL, (ii) the three lead vectors with their angles, (iii) the heart vector $\vec{H}$ at the centroid, and (iv) Einthoven's Law in the caption. --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: [KTU University Exam – May 2024] **State and explain Einthoven's Law. Why is it clinically significant?** **Model Answer:** Einthoven's Law states that the voltage of Lead II equals the sum of Lead I and Lead III: $$V_{II} = V_I + V_{III}$$ It arises from the geometry of the equilateral triangle formed by RA, LA, and LL electrodes, and the fact that the body is a single volume conductor (Kirchhoff's Voltage Law). **Clinical Significance (3 points):** 1. **Redundancy check:** Any two of the three leads can verify the third. If the measured Lead II differs from $V_I + V_{III}$ by more than ~10%, it indicates lead misplacement, electrode contact error, or instrument fault. 2. **Waveform validation:** Used in ECG machine self-test routines. 3. **Diagnostic value:** Powers the derivation of augmented leads (aVR, aVL, aVF) that complete the 6 frontal-plane axes. --- ### Question 2: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **Define lead vector. What is its direction and magnitude in the Einthoven Triangle?** **Model Answer:** A **lead vector** $\vec{L}$ is an oriented line segment representing a lead's sensitivity to the cardiac vector. It points from the **negative electrode to the positive electrode**, and its magnitude is proportional to the lead's sensitivity (gain). In the standard Einthoven equilateral triangle, all three bipolar lead vectors ($\vec{L}_I, \vec{L}_{II}, \vec{L}_{III}$) have **equal magnitude** (equal to the side length $a$ of the triangle) and are oriented at angles **$0^\circ, +60^\circ,$ and $+120^\circ$** respectively, measured from the Lead I axis (horizontal, pointing leftward from RA to LA). The recorded voltage is given by: $$V_{\text{lead}} = \vec{L} \cdot \vec{H} = L \cdot H \cdot \cos(\theta)$$ --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) ### Question A: [KTU University Exam – May 2024 — 14 Marks] **(a) Derive Einthoven's Law from first principles using the potentials at the three limb electrodes. Explain its significance in ECG instrumentation. (7 marks)** **Model Solution:** Let $V_{RA}$, $V_{LA}$, and $V_{LL}$ be the potentials at the right arm, left arm, and left leg respectively, measured with respect to a common reference. By definition of the bipolar leads: $$V_I = V_{LA} - V_{RA} \quad \text{(Lead I)}$$ $$V_{II} = V_{LL} - V_{RA} \quad \text{(Lead II)}$$ $$V_{III} = V_{LL} - V_{LA} \quad \text{(Lead III)}$$ Adding Leads I and III: $$V_I + V_{III} = (V_{LA} - V_{RA}) + (V_{LL} - V_{LA}) = V_{LL} - V_{RA} = V_{II}$$ Hence: $$\boxed{V_{II} = V_I + V_{III}} \quad \text{— Einthoven's Law}$$ **Significance in ECG instrumentation:** 1. Only **two** of the three bipolar leads are mathematically independent; the third is redundant. 2. Enables **continuous self-calibration** in ECG machines: a discrepancy flags lead-failure or patient-electrode issues. 3. Forms the algebraic foundation for deriving the **augmented leads (aVR, aVL, aVF)** in the standard 12-lead ECG. 4. Confirms the **single-dipole model** of cardiac electrical activity in the frontal plane. --- **(b) With the aid of a neat diagram, explain the Einthoven Triangle. Derive the expressions for the augmented leads $V_{aVR}$, $V_{aVL}$, and $V_{aVF}$ in terms of the standard bipolar lead voltages. (7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Diagram:** (Refer to the Mermaid diagram in Section 4.1, hand-drawn in the answer script.) **Setup:** The Einthoven Triangle is an equilateral triangle with vertices at RA, LA, and LL. The heart is modeled as a current dipole $\vec{H}$ at the centroid. The lead vectors $\vec{L}_I, \vec{L}_{II}, \vec{L}_{III}$ are oriented at $0^\circ, +60^\circ, +120^\circ$ from the horizontal. **Central Terminal (Wilson):** $$V_{CT} = \frac{V_{RA} + V_{LA} + V_{LL}}{3} \approx 0$$ **Deriving expressions for the limb potentials:** Using $V_I = V_{LA} - V_{RA}$ and $V_{II} = V_{LL} - V_{RA}$, plus $V_{CT} = 0$: $$V_{RA} + V_{LA} + V_{LL} = 0$$ Substituting: $$V_{RA} = -\frac{V_I + V_{II}}{3}, \quad V_{LA} = \frac{V_I - V_{III}}{3}, \quad V_{LL} = \frac{V_{II}}{3}$$ **Augmented leads (with 1.5× amplification factor):** $$V_{aVR} = 1.5 (V_{RA} - V_{CT}) = -\frac{V_I + V_{II}}{2}$$ $$V_{aVL} = 1.5 (V_{LA} - V_{CT}) = \frac{V_I - V_{III}}{2}$$ $$V_{aVF} = 1.5 (V_{LL} - V_{CT}) = \frac{V_{II}}{2} = \frac{V_I + V_{III}}{2}$$ These three augmented leads, together with the three bipolar leads, give **six frontal-plane axes** at $30^\circ$ intervals (the **hexaxial reference system**). --- ### Question B: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 — 14 Marks] **(a) Explain the concept of the cardiac vector and the lead vector. Show that the voltage recorded by an ECG lead is the dot product of the lead vector and the cardiac vector. (7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Cardiac Vector $\vec{H}$:** At any instant during the cardiac cycle, the wave of depolarization/repolarization across the myocardium can be represented as a **time-varying equivalent dipole**. This dipole is characterized by a vector $\vec{H}$ with magnitude $H$ (proportional to the net dipole moment) and a direction in 3D space (the mean electrical axis at that instant). **Lead Vector $\vec{L}$:** A lead vector is a property of the **lead system** (not the heart). It represents how effectively a particular lead "sees" the cardiac vector. Its magnitude $L$ is the sensitivity of the lead, and its direction points from the negative electrode to the positive electrode (Einthoven's convention). **Derivation of the dot-product relationship:** Consider the cardiac dipole $\vec{H}$ at the centroid of the equilateral triangle. The dipole creates a potential distribution in the surrounding volume conductor. By the **lead-field theory** (developed by Rush and Driscoll, 1960s, building on Einthoven), the potential difference between two electrodes A (positive) and B (negative) due to a dipole is: $$V_{AB} = \vec{L}_{AB} \cdot \vec{H}$$ where $\vec{L}_{AB} = \vec{r}_A - \vec{r}_B$ for a homogeneous volume conductor and a unit-strength dipole. For Lead I: $\vec{L}_I = \vec{r}_{LA} - \vec{r}_{RA}$, with magnitude $a$ and direction $0^\circ$. Computing the dot product explicitly: $$V_I = \vec{L}_I \cdot \vec{H} = \vert \vec{L}_I \vert \cdot \vert \vec{H} \vert \cdot \cos(\theta) = a \cdot H \cdot \cos(\theta)$$ where $\theta$ is the angle between the cardiac vector and the Lead I axis. Similarly, for Lead II (rotated $+60^\circ$): $$V_{II} = a \cdot H \cdot \cos(\theta - 60^\circ)$$ For Lead III (rotated $+120^\circ$): $$V_{III} = a \cdot H \cdot \cos(\theta - 120^\circ)$$ This proves the dot-product relationship and shows how each lead samples a different "projection" of the same cardiac vector. --- **(b) The cardiac vector makes an angle of $50^\circ$ with the Lead I axis at a certain instant, and its magnitude is 1.5 mV. Assuming the Einthoven Triangle is equilateral with side length equal to the lead vector magnitude, calculate the voltages recorded by Lead I, Lead II, and Lead III. Verify Einthoven's Law numerically. (7 marks)** **Given Data:** - Angle between $\vec{H}$ and $\vec{L}_I$: $\theta = 50^\circ$ - Magnitude of $\vec{H}$: $H = 1.5$ mV - Magnitude of lead vectors: $L = a$ (equilateral, but the value cancels in ratios; for absolute voltages, assume $a = 1$ in normalized units, so voltages equal the projection component) **Step 1 — Calculate $V_I$:** $$V_I = L \cdot H \cdot \cos(50^\circ) = (1)(1.5)(0.6428) = 0.9642 \text{ mV}$$ **Step 2 — Calculate $V_{II}$:** $$V_{II} = L \cdot H \cdot \cos(50^\circ - 60^\circ) = (1)(1.5)\cos(-10^\circ) = (1.5)(0.9848) = 1.4772 \text{ mV}$$ **Step 3 — Calculate $V_{III}$:** $$V_{III} = L \cdot H \cdot \cos(50^\circ - 120^\circ) = (1)(1.5)\cos(-70^\circ) = (1.5)(0.3420) = 0.5130 \text{ mV}$$ **Step 4 — Verify Einthoven's Law:** $$V_I + V_{III} = 0.9642 + 0.5130 = 1.4772 \text{ mV}$$ $$V_{II} = 1.4772 \text{ mV}$$ $$\boxed{V_I + V_{III} = V_{II} \quad \checkmark}$$ **Result Table:** | Lead | Voltage (mV) | Direction | |---|---|---| | Lead I | $+0.96$ | Positive deflection | | Lead II | $+1.48$ | Positive deflection | | Lead III | $+0.51$ | Positive deflection | **Clinical Interpretation:** All three leads show positive QRS deflections because the cardiac vector (at $50^\circ$) lies within the $+30^\circ$ to $+90^\circ$ range (normal axis range $0^\circ$ to $+90^\circ$ in the frontal plane). This is the basis of **electrical axis determination** in clinical ECG interpretation. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Sign error in augmented leads:** Students frequently write $V_{aVR} = +\frac{V_I + V_{II}}{2}$ instead of the correct **negative** sign. The negative sign reflects that aVR's positive electrode is at the right arm — when $\vec{H}$ points *away* from RA, the lead records a *negative* deflection. > 2. **Confusing Lead I and aVL:** Lead I is **bipolar** (LA − RA), while aVL is **augmented unipolar** ($\frac{V_I - V_{III}}{2}$). Mixing them up costs full marks. > 3. **Forgetting the 1.5× amplification:** Augmented leads have a 1.5× gain built into the Goldberger network. Forgetting this gives half the correct magnitude. > 4. **Angle convention mistakes:** Lead II is at $+60^\circ$ from Lead I (NOT $90^\circ$). The cardiac axis convention is counter-clockwise positive in the frontal plane. > 5. **Unit omission:** Always write mV (or mm at standard calibration) — units are mandatory in KTU answer scripts. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist (Einthoven Triangle):** - ✅ **Einthoven Triangle** = equilateral triangle with vertices at **RA, LA, LL** in the frontal plane. - ✅ **Cardiac vector $\vec{H}$** is modeled as a single rotating dipole at the **centroid** of the triangle. - ✅ **Lead vectors** point from the negative to the positive electrode with magnitude equal to triangle side $a$. - ✅ Lead I vector: $0^\circ$; Lead II vector: $+60^\circ$; Lead III vector: $+120^\circ$. - ✅ **Einthoven's Law:** $V_{II} = V_I + V_{III}$ — derived from KVL; holds exactly. - ✅ **Lead voltage formula:** $V_{\text{lead}} = \vec{L} \cdot \vec{H} = L \cdot H \cdot \cos(\theta)$. - ✅ **Augmented leads (Goldberger):** - $V_{aVR} = -\frac{V_I + V_{II}}{2}$ - $V_{aVL} = \frac{V_I - V_{III}}{2}$ - $V_{aVF} = \frac{V_{II}}{2} = \frac{V_I + V_{III}}{2}$ - ✅ **Wilson's Central Terminal** is the reference for augmented leads (assumed $\approx 0$ V). - ✅ The 6 frontal-plane axes (I, II, III, aVR, aVL, aVF) are separated by **$30^\circ$** — the **hexaxial reference system**. - ✅ **Normal cardiac axis range:** $-30^\circ$ to $+90^\circ$. - ✅ **Assumptions:** homogeneous volume conductor, point dipole at center, equilateral geometry, equal limb resistances. - ✅ **Engineering applications:** 12-lead ECG design, vectorcardiography, ICU monitoring, implantable cardiac devices, biomedical signal processing. - ✅ **Redundancy rule:** Only 2 of the 3 bipolar leads are independent; the 3rd is a consistency check. - ✅ **Standard calibration:** 1 mV = 10 mm deflection on standard ECG paper (gain = 1000). - ✅ **Clinical use:** Diagnosis of axis deviation, bundle branch blocks, ventricular hypertrophy, and electrode misplacement. --- *End of Module 2 — Einthoven Triangle Notes* *PEMRT412 — Biomedical Instrumentation | KTU 2024 Scheme*

topics.title: ECG Read Out Devices
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT412 - BIOMEDICAL INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** ECG Read Out Devices

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# ECG Read Out Devices — Core Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

An **ECG Read Out Device** (also called an **Electrocardiograph** or **ECG Recorder/Monitor**) is a bioelectronic instrument used to detect, amplify, condition, and display/record the **small bioelectric potentials (typically 0.5 mV to 5 mV)** generated by the depolarization and repolarization of cardiac muscle fibers. The device converts these surface-level potentials picked up by skin electrodes into a graphical trace called an **electrocardiogram (ECG/EKG)**, which serves as a primary diagnostic indicator of cardiac health.

According to KTU 2024 syllabus nomenclature, the read-out device is treated as the **terminal stage** of the ECG signal chain — the subsystem responsible for converting the processed analog ECG signal into a form perceivable by the clinician (paper trace, CRT/LCD display, or digital storage).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight — Module 2 Focus**
> The Module-2 emphasis on "ECG Read Out Devices" centres on three sub-blocks:
> (a) **Patient cable & lead selector**
> (b) **ECG amplifier (differential pre-amp + main amp)**
> (c) **Display / Recorder section** (ink-jet/thermal array, CRO, or digital LCD)
> All these together constitute the "Read Out" function.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Heart's "Audio Visualizer"

Imagine your heart is a symphony orchestra. Every time it beats, individual musicians (cardiac muscle cells) play tiny electrical "notes." By themselves, you cannot hear them. But if you place highly sensitive microphones (**electrodes**) on the concert hall walls (**skin**), and route that signal through a powerful amplifier and speaker (**ECG amplifier**), you finally "hear" the music. The **ECG Read Out Device** is the **screen-and-speaker combination** at the end — it does not *create* the music, it just lets the cardiologist *see and study* it.

A more technical analogy: think of an ECG machine as a **lie detector for the heart** — it does not generate the signal, it merely reads what is already there and presents it legibly.

## 1.3 Standardised Clinical Metrics

These values are **fixed by international convention** (AHA / IEC 60601-2-25) and are routinely tested in KTU exams.

| Metric | Standard Value | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Paper Speed** | **25 mm/s** | Horizontal axis = time |
| **Vertical Calibration** | **10 mm/mV** | Vertical axis = voltage |
| **Mains Rejection** | > 100 dB | Filters 50/60 Hz interference |
| **Bandwidth** | 0.05 Hz – 150 Hz | Diagnostic-grade |
| **Input Impedance** | > 10 MΩ | Prevents electrode loading |

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic for the exam**: *"TEN at TWENTY-FIVE"* → 10 mm/mV vertical, 25 mm/s horizontal. This single line can fetch 2 marks.

## 1.4 Block-Level Identification of the "Read-Out Stage"

The full ECG instrumentation chain (per KTU Module-1 → Module-2 continuity) is:

1. **Bioelectric Source** — cardiac dipole
2. **Electrodes + Patient Cable** — transducing ionic current to electronic current
3. **Lead Selector Network** — Wilson / Einthoven / Goldberger configurations
4. **ECG Amplifier** (instrumentation amp + gain stages)
5. **Filters** — line frequency (50 Hz notch) + baseline wander (high-pass) + EMG (low-pass)
6. **👉 READ-OUT DEVICE 👈** — galvanometer/thermal array/CRO/LCD + digital memory
7. **Power Supply + Isolation** (patient safety module)

The "Read Out" block is the **focus of this note** — sections 1.4 → 1.6 below.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of an ECG Read-Out Device

The read-out stage is functionally partitioned into three cooperating sub-modules:

### 2.1.1 Galvanometer-Based Direct-Writing Recorders (Older Generation)

- A **d'Arsonval moving-coil galvanometer** drives a heated stylus.
- The stylus physically deflects across heat-sensitive paper.
- **Frequency response:** limited to ~ 40 Hz (low-fidelity, only suitable for resting ECG).
- Deflection equation:

$$ y(t) \;=\; K_g \cdot i(t) $$

  where $K_g$ is the galvanometer constant (m/A) and $i(t)$ is the coil current.

### 2.1.2 Thermal-Array / Ink-Jet Recorder (Modern Clinical Standard)

- A **linear array of 32 to 64 thermal elements** prints on thermosensitive paper.
- Each element is heated briefly to darken a dot → forms the trace.
- **Frequency response:** up to 150 Hz (full diagnostic bandwidth).
- **Advantage:** simultaneous multi-channel tracing using **overlapping stylus trajectories** offset by 1/3 of channel height.

### 2.1.3 CRT / LCD Digital Display

- Used in **patient monitors** (ICUs, CCUs).
- Signal first digitised by an ADC (sampling rate $\geq$ 500 Hz).
- Display persistence via **digital memory refresh**.
- Allows **freeze-frame, scroll-back, and alarm logic**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Hot Topic:** Examiners frequently ask students to *compare* a galvanometer-type recorder with a thermal-array recorder — 7-mark question in ESE.

## 2.2 Heart Rate Calculation — The Most Tested Formula

For a regular rhythm, the heart rate (HR) is computed directly from the **R-R interval** measured on the ECG paper:

$$ \text{HR (beats/min)} \;=\; \frac{60}{T_{RR}} \;=\; \frac{60 \times f_{s}}{N_{boxes}} \times \frac{1}{1} $$

Rewriting in clinically useful form (boxes counted on standard 25 mm/s paper):

$$ \text{HR} \;=\; \frac{1500}{\text{number of small boxes between R-peaks}} $$

or equivalently:

$$ \text{HR} \;=\; \frac{300}{\text{number of large boxes between R-peaks}} $$

where **1 large box = 5 small boxes = 0.20 s** at 25 mm/s.

## 2.3 Calibration Pulse — Why "1 cm = 1 mV"

Before every recording, a **1 mV calibration pulse** is injected:

$$ \Delta y_{cal} \;=\; \frac{1 \text{ mV}}{0.1 \text{ mV/mm}} \;=\; 10 \text{ mm} $$

This 10 mm deflection is the **"normalisation reference"** that lets the cardiologist correctly interpret wave amplitudes.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Typical KTU Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{HR} = \dfrac{1500}{N_{small-box}}$ | Heart rate from R-R boxes | Numerical problems |
| 2 | $T_{box} = 0.04 \text{ s}$ (small), $0.20 \text{ s}$ (large) | Paper time per box | Time-axis questions |
| 3 | $V = \dfrac{h_{mm}}{10}$ (mV) | Voltage from trace height | Amplitude problems |
| 4 | $f_{c} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{R_1 R_2 C_1 C_2}}$ | Notch filter centre freq | Filter design |
| 5 | $A_d = \dfrac{R_f}{R_1}(1 + \tfrac{2R_2}{R_g})$ | Instrumentation-amp gain | Amplifier stage |
| 6 | $\text{CMRR}_{dB} = 20\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{A_d}{A_c}\right)$ | Common-mode rejection ratio | Noise problems |
| 7 | $I_{patient,max} \le 10 \mu\text{A}$ | Patient leakage limit | Safety questions |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Use `\vert` instead of `\vert` pipes in table rows.** When writing absolute value inside a KTU formula cell, write $\vert x \vert$, never `|x|`, to keep markdown tables intact.

## 2.5 Engineering Utility Beyond Medicine

Although "ECG Read Out" sounds clinical, the underlying technology is the **template** for almost every bioelectric recorder in existence:

- **Electroencephalograph (EEG)** — same thermal-array read-out, only different gain and bandwidth.
- **Electromyograph (EMG)** — adds integrating read-out modes (RMS envelope display).
- **Foetal heart-rate monitor** — uses **peak-detection read-out** (autocorrelation of R-peaks).
- **Wearable / Holter recorders** — replace paper with **solid-state memory** and wireless read-out to a phone.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Industry Linkage:** Several previous-year papers asked to *"discuss how the read-out stage of an ECG machine is reused in a Holter monitor."* Mentioning **flash-memory + Bluetooth read-out** earns bonus marks.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Heart-Rate Formula From First Principles

The derivations below are the *complete algebraic chain* expected by KTU examiners. No step is skipped.

**Given:** Two consecutive R-peaks are separated by $N$ small boxes on ECG paper running at standard speed $v_s = 25$ mm/s. Each small box has horizontal width $b = 1$ mm.

**Step 1 — Express the R-R interval in seconds.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Distance between R-peaks} &\;=\; N \cdot b \;\;=\; N \cdot 1 \text{ mm} \;\;=\; N \text{ mm} \\
\text{Time } T_{RR} &\;=\; \frac{\text{Distance}}{\text{Speed}} \;\;=\; \frac{N \text{ mm}}{25 \text{ mm/s}} \;\;=\; \frac{N}{25} \text{ s}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Conversion logic:* Distance divided by speed yields time — a basic kinematic identity applied to paper motion.

**Step 2 — Convert this period into a frequency (beats per second).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_{beat} \;\;=\;\; \frac{1}{T_{RR}} \;\;=\;\; \frac{25}{N} \;\; \text{Hz (beats/s)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert to beats per minute (the clinically meaningful unit).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{HR} \;\;=\;\; f_{beat} \times 60 \;\;=\;\; \frac{25}{N} \times 60 \;\;=\;\; \frac{1500}{N}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final, exam-ready result:**

$$ \boxed{\;\text{HR (beats/min)} \;=\; \dfrac{1500}{N_{small-boxes}}\;} $$

This is the universally cited KTU formula. Memorise the derivation so you can reproduce it under exam pressure.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical — A Full KTU Numerical Template

**Question (typical KTU 2-mark sub-part):** *"On an ECG trace taken at 25 mm/s, two consecutive R-waves are separated by 20 small boxes. Calculate the heart rate in bpm."*

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: Identify the count} \quad N &\;=\; 20 \text{ small boxes} \\
\text{Step 2: Apply the formula} \quad \text{HR} &\;=\; \frac{1500}{N} \;\;=\;\; \frac{1500}{20} \\
\text{Step 3: Simplify} \quad \text{HR} &\;=\; 75 \text{ beats/min}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Answer:** The patient's heart rate is **75 bpm**, well within the normal adult resting range (60–100 bpm).

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical — Voltage Calibration

**Question:** *"A patient ECG shows an R-wave peak that rises 15 mm above baseline on the standard 25 mm/s, 10 mm/mV paper. Calculate the voltage of the R-wave."*

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: Recall calibration} \quad 10 \text{ mm} &\;=\; 1 \text{ mV} \\
\text{Step 2: Set up ratio} \quad V_R \;\;( \text{in mV} ) &\;=\; \frac{h_{R} \text{ (mm)}}{10 \text{ (mm/mV)}} \\
\text{Step 3: Substitute} \quad V_R &\;=\; \frac{15}{10} \;\;=\;\; 1.5 \text{ mV}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Answer:** The R-wave amplitude is **1.5 mV** — within the normal range (1.0 – 2.0 mV).

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Read-Out Technologies

| Property | D'Arsonval Galvanometer | Thermal-Array Recorder | CRT/LCD Digital Monitor |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Frequency response** | ≤ 40 Hz | ≤ 150 Hz | ≥ 500 Hz (post-ADC) |
| **Channels** | 1 (multi-channel via mechanical offset) | Up to 12 simultaneous | Unlimited |
| **Paper cost** | Heat paper (low) | Heat paper (low) | Nil (electronic) |
| **Storage** | Paper only | Paper only | HDD/Flash/Cloud |
| **Diagnostic grade** | Marginal | Full | Full |
| **Modern use** | Obsolete | **Clinical standard** | **ICU standard** |
| **Failure mode** | Stylus friction | Element burnout | Display dead pixels |

---

## 3.5 Derivation of the Notch Filter Centre Frequency

ECG machines include a **twin-T notch filter** to remove 50 Hz mains interference. The classic derivation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_{c} &\;=\; \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{R_1 R_2 C_1 C_2}} \\
\text{For symmetric design } R_1 = R_2 = R,\; C_1 = C_2 = C \;&\Rightarrow\; f_c \;=\; \frac{1}{2\pi RC} \\
\text{For } R = 31.8 \text{ k}\Omega,\; C = 0.1 \mu\text{F} \;&\Rightarrow\; f_c \;=\; \frac{1}{2\pi \cdot 31.8 \times 10^3 \cdot 0.1 \times 10^{-6}} \\
&\;=\; \frac{1}{2\pi \cdot 3.18 \times 10^{-3}} \;=\; 50 \text{ Hz}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Engineering insight:** Note that **changing a single resistor** lets the same board reject 60 Hz interference (US/Asia) instead of 50 Hz (Europe/India). This is a favourite KTU design-trap question.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an ECG Read-Out System

Below is a Mermaid `flowchart` capturing the complete signal flow from skin surface to clinician's eye. Each block represents a functional stage inside the **read-out device** and its supporting electronics.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Heart Bioelectric Source 0.5 to 5 mV] --> B[Surface Electrodes Ag AgCl]
    B --> C[Patient Cable Shielded]
    C --> D[Lead Selector Network Einthoven Wilson Goldberger]
    D --> E[Instrumentation Amplifier Gain 1000 CMRR 100 dB]
    E --> F[Bandpass Filter 0.05 Hz to 150 Hz]
    F --> G[50 Hz Notch Filter Twin T]
    G --> H[Gain Stage Final Gain 5000]
    H --> I[Signal Splitter]
    I --> J[Thermal Array Write Head]
    I --> K[ADC 500 Hz Sampling]
    K --> L[Microcontroller DSP]
    L --> M[LCD TFT Display]
    L --> N[Flash Memory Storage]
    J --> O[ECG Paper Hard Copy]
    M --> P[Clinician Visual Read Out]
    N --> Q[PC or Cloud Upload]
    O --> P
```

**Reading guide (left → right):** Heart signal → electrodes → cable → lead selector → amplifier → filters → splitter → **(read-out branches)** → thermal paper + LCD + memory. All three terminal blocks constitute the **"Read Out" stage** under KTU Module-2 nomenclature.

## 4.2 Mermaid Architecture — Read-Out Sub-Module Internals

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SA[Pre Amplified ECG] --> MP[Microcontroller ARM Cortex M4]
    MP --> RAM[RAM Buffer 64 KB]
    MP --> DSP[DSP Heart Rate Detection]
    DSP --> HR[HR Display Window]
    MP --> LCD[LCD Driver ILI9341]
    MP --> BUZ[Buzzer Alarm if HR out of range]
    MP --> BT[Bluetooth Module HC 05]
    BT --> PHONE[Smartphone Read Out]
```

**Block descriptions:**

- **MP** — processes the digital ECG in real time.
- **RAM** — circular buffer for the last 5 minutes of waveform.
- **DSP** — runs Pan-Tompkins R-peak detection algorithm.
- **HR** — numeric heart-rate read-out.
- **LCD** — graphical trace + numerical vitals.
- **BUZ** — alarms for bradycardia (< 60 bpm) and tachycardia (> 100 bpm).
- **BT** — wireless read-out to a phone, completing the modern Holter paradigm.

## 4.3 Failure-Tree of the Read-Out Stage (Examiner's Favourite)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    F1[Read Out Failure] --> F2[Paper trace blank]
    F1 --> F3[Trace noisy 50 Hz hum]
    F1 --> F4[Trace drifts baseline]
    F1 --> F5[Display freezes]
    F2 --> F21[Thermal head burnout]
    F2 --> F22[No ECG input pre amp dead]
    F3 --> F31[Notch filter open circuit]
    F3 --> F32[Earth loop missing]
    F4 --> F41[Capacitor C1 in HPF leaky]
    F5 --> F51[Watchdog timer not reset]
    F5 --> F52[Flash memory full]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram-fallback policy:** The original KTU Module-2 textbook includes physical drawings of stylus mechanics and thermal-array heating elements. Those physical drawings are reproduced here functionally as the **block-architecture diagrams** above, which convey identical information in Mermaid-compatible form.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 marks each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**Define an ECG read-out device. List its three primary functional blocks.**

**Model Answer:**

An ECG read-out device is the terminal sub-system of an electrocardiograph that converts the conditioned cardiac bioelectric signal into a perceptible form — a paper trace, a CRT/LCD display, or a digital memory record.

The three primary functional blocks are:

1. **Signal conditioning / final amplifier** — sets the trace amplitude to standard 10 mm/mV calibration.
2. **Writing mechanism** — galvanometer, thermal-array head, or LCD/LED matrix.
3. **Display / storage module** — heat-sensitive paper, LCD screen, and/or solid-state memory.

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** Students frequently *omit the storage* block. Examiners deduct 1 mark if digital memory / paper handling is not mentioned.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**Why is the ECG paper standardised at 25 mm/s and 10 mm/mV? What happens if the speed drifts to 20 mm/s?**

**Model Answer:**

- **25 mm/s** and **10 mm/mV** are international standards (AHA / IEC) so that ECG traces recorded in any country are *directly comparable*. The speed defines the **time axis**; the calibration defines the **amplitude axis**.
- If speed drifts to **20 mm/s**, every time-interval read from the trace becomes **25/20 = 1.25 times** too long. The calculated heart rate will appear **20 % lower** than reality, potentially mis-diagnosing a tachycardia as normal.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 marks each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, describe the **functional architecture of an ECG read-out system** based on a **thermal-array recorder**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **principle, construction, and limitations of the d'Arsonval moving-coil galvanometer** as used in older ECG recorders. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

1. **Block diagram (refer Section 4.1 above)** — five marks for diagram, two for explanation.
2. The ECG signal, after amplification and filtering, drives a **linear array of 32 thermal resistors**.
3. Each resistor is **pulse-width modulated** according to the instantaneous ECG voltage.
4. The heated resistors momentarily darken heat-sensitive paper moving at 25 mm/s beneath the array.
5. **Multi-channel operation** is achieved by laterally offsetting each channel's trace by **1/3 of the channel height**, creating the familiar interleaved appearance.
6. **Advantages:** bandwidth up to 150 Hz, simultaneous 12-lead printing, low noise.
7. **Disadvantages:** paper consumable cost, no electronic storage in older models.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

1. A **permanent magnet** creates a uniform radial field between cylindrical pole pieces.
2. A **rectangular coil** suspended on jewelled bearings sits in this field.
3. The amplified ECG current $i(t)$ passes through the coil.
4. The **Lorentz force** $F = N B l i(t)$ produces a torque $\tau = N B A i(t)$, where $A$ is the coil area.
5. The coil rotates against a **spring restoring torque** $k\theta$, giving the balance:
   $$ N B A \, i(t) \;=\; k \, \theta(t) \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \theta(t) \;=\; \frac{NBA}{k}\,i(t) $$
6. A **stylus** attached to the coil writes on heat-sensitive paper.
7. **Limitations:**
   - Mechanical inertia → frequency response limited to ~40 Hz.
   - Stylus friction → distortion of high-frequency QRS details.
   - Single-channel without complex mechanical multiplexing.
   - No electronic storage — paper is the only record.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students forget that the **restoring spring constant k** limits the linear range. If you write only $F = BIl$ without showing the torque balance, you lose 2 marks.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) — [KTU University Exam — May 2023]

**(a)** Derive the **formula for heart-rate calculation** from an ECG strip running at 25 mm/s. Show that **HR = 1500 / N** where N is the number of small boxes between R-peaks. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An ECG recorder uses a **twin-T notch filter** with $R = 31.8 \text{ k}\Omega$ and $C = 0.1 \mu\text{F}$. Calculate the **centre frequency**. Why is this filter included in the read-out path? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

1. Let the horizontal width of one small box be $b = 1$ mm.
2. The paper speed is $v_s = 25$ mm/s.
3. The R-R distance on paper is $d = N b = N$ mm.
4. The R-R time interval is therefore:
   $$ T_{RR} \;=\; \frac{d}{v_s} \;=\; \frac{N}{25}\;\text{s} $$
5. The heart rate in Hz is:
   $$ f_{beat} \;=\; \frac{1}{T_{RR}} \;=\; \frac{25}{N}\;\text{Hz} $$
6. Converting to beats per minute:
   $$ \text{HR} \;=\; f_{beat} \times 60 \;=\; \frac{25 \times 60}{N} \;=\; \frac{1500}{N}\;\text{bpm} $$

**Hence proved.**

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

1. Twin-T notch filter centre frequency formula:
   $$ f_c \;=\; \frac{1}{2\pi RC} $$
2. Substitute $R = 31.8 \text{ k}\Omega = 31.8 \times 10^3 \text{ }\Omega$, $C = 0.1 \mu\text{F} = 0.1 \times 10^{-6}$ F.
   $$ f_c \;=\; \frac{1}{2\pi \cdot 31.8 \times 10^3 \cdot 0.1 \times 10^{-6}} \;=\; \frac{1}{2\pi \cdot 3.18 \times 10^{-3}} $$
   $$ f_c \;\approx\; \frac{1}{0.02} \;=\; 50 \text{ Hz} $$
3. **Purpose:** The 50 Hz notch filter removes **mains power-line interference** that couples into the patient cable through capacitive pick-up. Without it, the ECG trace is contaminated by a 50 Hz sine wave that masks the small ST-segment changes critical for diagnosing myocardial ischaemia.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often compute $1 / (2\pi RC)$ but forget to **convert units** (kΩ, μF → Ω, F). A common error is writing $31.8 \times 0.1 = 3.18$ directly in the denominator, which is wrong by a factor of $10^7$. Always explicitly state the unit conversion step.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **ECG Read Out Device** = terminal stage of an ECG machine; converts processed bioelectric signal into human-perceptible form (paper, screen, or memory).
- **Standard paper speed** = **25 mm/s**; **standard vertical calibration** = **10 mm/mV** — known as *"TEN at TWENTY-FIVE."*
- **1 small box = 0.04 s, 1 large box = 0.20 s.**
- **Heart rate formula:** $HR = \dfrac{1500}{N_{small-boxes}}$ or $\dfrac{300}{N_{large-boxes}}$ — derivation must be reproducible.
- **Three read-out technologies** to compare in exams:
  1. D'Arsonval galvanometer — bandwidth ≤ 40 Hz, obsolete.
  2. Thermal-array recorder — bandwidth ≤ 150 Hz, **clinical standard**.
  3. Digital LCD + memory — bandwidth ≥ 500 Hz, **ICU/Holter standard**.
- **Twin-T notch filter** is centred at the **mains frequency** (50 Hz in India); its centre frequency follows $f_c = 1/(2\pi RC)$.
- **Patient leakage limit** = **10 μA** (IEC 60601-1) — a frequent 1-mark safety question.
- Always state **CMRR** in dB, computed as $20 \log_{10}(A_d/A_c)$, never as a raw ratio.
- **Modern read-outs** are *digital*: ADC → DSP (Pan-Tompkins for HR) → LCD + flash + Bluetooth.
- **Examiner's golden rule:** Always draw a **neat block diagram** in 7-mark questions; 2 marks are reserved for diagram alone.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: ECG Machine
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT412 - BIOMEDICAL INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** ECG Machine

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Electrocardiograph (ECG Machine)** is a biomedical electronic instrument designed to non-invasively record the electrical activity of the human heart over a period of time using surface electrodes placed on the limbs and chest. The resulting trace — the **Electrocardiogram (ECG/EKG)** — represents the **summated action potentials** of millions of cardiac muscle cells (myocytes) as detected from the body surface, providing diagnostic information about cardiac rhythm, conduction, ischemia, infarction, and structural abnormalities.

**Standard Operational Definition (KTU Board Expectation):**
An ECG machine is a high-gain, low-noise bioelectric amplifier system that acquires the **cardiac electrical vector** through a defined lead configuration, processes it through signal-conditioning stages (amplification, filtering, isolation), and presents the waveform either on a thermal-array recorder, oscilloscope, or digital display.

### Key Operating Parameters (Standard ECG Industry Norms)

| Parameter | Standard Value |
|---|---|
| Input signal amplitude | **0.5 mV to 5 mV** |
| Frequency band | **0.05 Hz to 150 Hz** |
| Standard sensitivity | **10 mm/mV** |
| Paper speed | **25 mm/s** (standard), 50 mm/s (double) |
| Common Mode Rejection Ratio (CMRR) | **> 100 dB** (≥ 80 dB minimum clinical) |
| Input impedance | **> 5 MΩ** (typically 10 MΩ) |
| Patient leakage current | **< 10 μA** (Type CF, isolated) |

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Heart as a Battery and the Body as a Wire"

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy (Plain English):**
> Imagine the heart as a **weak AA battery (1.5 V)** sitting inside a tub of conductive saline (the body's electrolytic tissue). Every time it "fires" (depolarization), it generates a tiny voltage that spreads through the saline. Now, if you dip **10 electrodes** at specific points on the tub's rim, you can measure the voltage difference between any two of them. The ECG machine is essentially a **very sensitive, very picky voltmeter** that:
>
> 1. Picks up the **tiny ripples (mV level)** of this bioelectric source.
> 2. **Rejects the huge 50/60 Hz line noise** that the body also picks up like an antenna.
> 3. **Amplifies and charts** the voltage over time — and the chart is the ECG.
>
> Different electrode pairings (called *leads*) give you different "camera angles" of the same 3-D electrical event in the heart — that's why 12 leads give 12 different but related views.

### The Cardiac Cycle — Why an ECG Has Waves

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The heart's natural pacemaker (the **SA node**) fires an electrical wave that sweeps across the atria (**P wave**), pauses at the **AV node**, then rushes down the bundle of His and Purkinje fibers to depolarize the ventricles (**QRS complex**), and finally the ventricles repolarize (**T wave**). The ECG is the **summed surface projection** of these vectorial events.

## 1.3 Historical Context & Clinical Significance

- Invented by **Willem Einthoven** (1903) — awarded the **Nobel Prize in 1924** for his "string galvanometer" ECG.
- The **12-lead ECG** remains the gold-standard, non-invasive, inexpensive first-line cardiac diagnostic test worldwide.
- Applications: arrhythmia detection, myocardial infarction (MI) localization, electrolyte imbalance, drug effects, pacemaker function.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Functional Block Architecture of an ECG Machine

The ECG machine is a **multi-stage bioelectric amplifier** with strict patient-safety isolation. Below is the canonical block decomposition:

### Stage 1 — Electrode Interface
- **Surface electrodes** (Ag/AgCl disposable) convert ionic skin current → electronic current.
- **Electrode-skin impedance** must be minimized using electrolyte gel (NaCl paste). Typical impedance **< 5 kΩ** per electrode pair.
- **Defibrillation protection** (zener/avalanche diodes + current-limiting resistors) is mandatory — the patient may be defibrillated while connected.

### Stage 2 — Lead Selector Network
- A **rotary switch / multiplexer** that selects any pair from the 10 electrodes (4 limb + 6 chest).
- Modern machines use **electronic switching** (relay / CMOS analog MUX) for computer control.

### Stage 3 — Preamplifier (Instrumentation Amplifier)
- **Three-Op-Amp Instrumentation Amplifier (IA)** is the universal choice.
- **Reasons:** differential gain (high), common-mode rejection (CMRR > 100 dB), extremely high input impedance (> 10⁹ Ω), low noise.
- Gain here is moderate (×10) to avoid saturation by DC offset.

### Stage 4 — Right-Leg Drive (RLD) Circuit
- A **negative-feedback active loop** that re-injects the common-mode voltage back into the patient's right leg through a high-value resistor.
- **Purpose:** Drastically reduces 50/60 Hz interference by *nulling* the common-mode pickup.
- Improves effective CMRR by **20–40 dB**.

### Stage 5 — DC Isolation Barrier
- **Isolation amplifier** (transformer, optocoupler, or capacitive isolation) separates the patient-connected side from the mains/receiving side.
- Protects patient from **microshock** (currents < 10 μA can fibrillate the heart directly).
- Meets **IEC 60601-1** patient-leakage limits.

### Stage 6 — Main Amplifier & Filtering
- Provides final gain (total system gain ≈ 1000–5000).
- **Band-pass filter:** **0.05 Hz (high-pass)** to remove DC baseline drift, **150 Hz (low-pass)** to remove EMG/muscle noise and RF.
- **50/60 Hz notch filter** for line interference (Q ≈ 2–4, tunable).

### Stage 7 — Output / Display / Recorder
- **Thermal-array writer**, LCD, or digital storage.
- **Calibration pulse** (1 mV) injected at start of every trace.

## 2.2 The 12-Lead ECG System

> [!NOTE]
> The "12 leads" are not 12 wires — they are **12 different voltage "viewpoints"** derived from just **10 physical electrodes** (4 limb + 6 precordial).

### 2.2.1 Limb Leads (Bipolar — Einthoven's Triangle)

- **Lead I:** LA (+) − RA (−) → measures vector along **0°** (horizontal left arm).
- **Lead II:** LL (+) − RA (−) → measures vector along **+60°** (down the left side).
- **Lead III:** LL (+) − LA (−) → measures vector along **+120°** (down the right side).
- All three lie in the **frontal plane**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Einthoven's Law (The Triangle Rule):**
>
> $$II = I + III$$
>
> This must hold at every instant in time. It is the KTU-board-favourite equation and is used as a validity check on electrode placement.

### 2.2.2 Augmented Limb Leads (Unipolar — Goldberger)

Derived by removing the exploring electrode's connection from the central terminal (Wilson) and using a modified reference:

- **aVR** (augmented Vector Right): explores right shoulder, vector **−150°**.
- **aVL** (augmented Vector Left): explores left shoulder, vector **−30°**.
- **aVF** (augmented Vector Foot): explores left leg, vector **+90°**.

### 2.2.3 Precordial / Chest Leads (Unipolar — Wilson's Central Terminal)

- **V1:** 4th ICS, right sternal border.
- **V2:** 4th ICS, left sternal border.
- **V3:** midway between V2 and V4.
- **V4:** 5th ICS, mid-clavicular line.
- **V5:** anterior axillary line, same level as V4.
- **V6:** mid-axillary line, same level as V4.
- Explore the **horizontal plane**.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!TIP]
> The following equations are tested repeatedly in KTU Module 2 examinations. Memorize the symbols and boundary conditions.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Einthoven's Law** | $II = I + III$ | mV, instantaneous |
| 2 | **Heart Rate (small-box method, 25 mm/s)** | $HR = \dfrac{1500}{N}$ | N = number of small boxes between R–R |
| 3 | **Heart Rate (large-box method, 25 mm/s)** | $HR = \dfrac{300}{N_{L}}$ | $N_L$ = large boxes between R–R |
| 4 | **Heart Rate (seconds method)** | $HR = \dfrac{60}{R\!-\!R\text{(seconds)}}$ | beats per minute (bpm) |
| 5 | **ECG paper time per small box** | $t_{sb} = \dfrac{25\,\text{mm/s} \times 1\,\text{mm}}{1} = 0.04$ s | At 25 mm/s, 1 small box = 1 mm = 0.04 s |
| 6 | **ECG paper time per large box** | $t_{lb} = 5 \times t_{sb} = 0.20$ s | 1 large box = 5 mm |
| 7 | **Calibration sensitivity** | $S = \dfrac{\text{deflection (mm)}}{1\,\text{mV}} = 10$ mm/mV | Standard |
| 8 | **Amplitude conversion** | $A_{mV} = \dfrac{A_{mm}}{10}$ | From mm deflection on paper |
| 9 | **Total ECG voltage gain** | $G_{total} = G_{pre} \times G_{iso} \times G_{main}$ | Typically 1000–5000 |
| 10 | **CMRR (in dB)** | $CMRR_{dB} = 20\,\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{A_{d}}{A_{cm}}\right)$ | $A_d$ differential, $A_{cm}$ common-mode gain |
| 11 | **Interference reduction by RLD** | $\Delta CMRR \approx 20\log_{10}\!\left(1 + \dfrac{Z_{loop}}{R_{RLD}}\right)$ | dB improvement; $Z_{loop}$ body-to-electrode |
| 12 | **Electrode-skin impedance (Ag/AgCl)** | $Z_e \approx 1\,k\Omega$ at 10 Hz | After gel preparation |
| 13 | **QRS duration (normal)** | $0.06 \le QRS \le 0.10$ s | 1.5–2.5 small boxes |
| 14 | **Normal PR interval** | $0.12 \le PR \le 0.20$ s | 3–5 small boxes |
| 15 | **Normal QTc (Bazett)** | $QTc = \dfrac{QT}{\sqrt{R\!-\!R}}$ | ≤ 0.44 s (men), ≤ 0.46 s (women) |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Clinical decision support:** automated QRS detection (Pan-Tompkins algorithm) in modern digital ECG machines.
- **Holter monitors** extend ECG recording to 24–48 hours using the same amplifier chain on a portable platform.
- **Telemetry / wearable ECG** (Apple Watch, AliveCor) uses a single modified bipolar lead.
- **Defibrillators** share the same electrode interface and use ECG for **R-wave synchronized shock (cardioversion)**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Einthoven's Law from Kirchhoff's Voltage Law

**Statement to prove:** $II = I + III$

We define the three limb leads as voltage differences between the limb electrodes (RA, LA, LL):

$$I = V_{LA} - V_{RA}$$
$$II = V_{LL} - V_{RA}$$
$$III = V_{LL} - V_{LA}$$

**Step 1.** Add the equations for Lead I and Lead III:

$$I + III = (V_{LA} - V_{RA}) + (V_{LL} - V_{LA})$$

**Step 2.** Cancel the common term $V_{LA}$:

$$I + III = V_{LL} - V_{RA}$$

**Step 3.** Recognize the right-hand side as the definition of Lead II:

$$I + III = V_{LL} - V_{RA} = II$$

$$\boxed{\,II = I + III\,}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Einthoven's Law is not empirical — it is a direct consequence of **Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL)** applied to the closed loop of the three limb electrodes. It is used clinically to verify electrode integrity (a broken wire makes the equality fail).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Heart Rate from R–R Interval

The standard ECG paper speed is **25 mm/s**. The paper is gridded such that:
- 1 **small box** = 1 mm wide.
- 1 **large box** = 5 mm wide (5 small boxes).

### Step-by-Step Time Derivation

**Step 1.** Time per small box:

$$t_{sb} = \frac{\text{distance}}{\text{speed}} = \frac{1\,\text{mm}}{25\,\text{mm/s}} = 0.04\,\text{s}$$

**Step 2.** Time per large box:

$$t_{lb} = 5 \times t_{sb} = 5 \times 0.04 = 0.20\,\text{s}$$

**Step 3.** Let $N$ = number of small boxes between two consecutive R-peaks. Then the R–R interval in seconds is:

$$R\!-\!R = N \times 0.04\,\text{s}$$

**Step 4.** Heart rate (beats per minute) is the inverse of the period (in minutes):

$$HR = \frac{1}{R\!-\!R\,\text{(min)}} = \frac{1}{N \times 0.04 / 60}$$

**Step 5.** Simplify:

$$HR = \frac{60}{N \times 0.04} = \frac{1500}{N}\,\text{bpm}$$

**Step 6.** Equivalent large-box form (let $N_L = N/5$):

$$HR = \frac{300}{N_L}\,\text{bpm}$$

**Worked Numerical Example:**

Suppose $N = 20$ small boxes between R–R peaks.

$$HR = \frac{1500}{20} = 75\,\text{bpm}$$

$$\boxed{\,HR = 75\,\text{bpm (normal sinus rhythm)}\,}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Required Amplifier Gain of ECG Front-End

The ECG signal at the skin is typically **0.5 mV to 5 mV peak-to-peak**. The recorder expects a **1 V** full-scale input (or 10 mm deflection for 1 mV at standard sensitivity).

**Step 1.** Required output amplitude (for full-scale deflection):

$$V_{out} = 1\,\text{V (full-scale)}$$

**Step 2.** Required gain for the smallest expected input:

$$G_{req} = \frac{V_{out}}{V_{in,min}} = \frac{1\,\text{V}}{0.5\,\text{mV}} = \frac{1}{0.5 \times 10^{-3}} = 2000$$

**Step 3.** For the largest expected input (5 mV), the same gain gives:

$$V_{out,max} = 2000 \times 5\,\text{mV} = 10\,\text{V}$$

This exceeds 1 V — therefore a **gain switch (×1, ×2)** or **auto-ranging ADC** is provided in modern machines.

**Step 4.** Practical split across stages:

$$G_{total} = G_{pre} \times G_{iso} \times G_{main} = 10 \times 1 \times 200 = 2000$$

**Step 5.** In dB:

$$G_{dB} = 20\log_{10}(2000) = 20 \times 3.301 = 66\,\text{dB}$$

$$\boxed{\,G_{req} \approx 2000 \;(66\,\text{dB})\text{ for 1 V full-scale from 0.5 mV minimum input}\,}$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — CMRR Numerical Requirement

A 50 Hz mains interference of **V_cm = 5 V** (typical body pickup) must be suppressed to below the ECG signal of **V_d = 1 mV**.

**Step 1.** Required suppression factor:

$$S_{req} = \frac{V_{cm}}{V_{d}} = \frac{5}{0.001} = 5000$$

**Step 2.** Convert to dB:

$$CMRR_{dB} = 20\log_{10}(5000) = 20 \times 3.699 = 73.98 \approx 74\,\text{dB}$$

**Step 3.** A practical safety margin (×10) requires:

$$CMRR_{req} = 20\log_{10}(50000) = 93.98 \approx 94\,\text{dB}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU boards accept **CMRR ≥ 80 dB** as the minimum clinical standard; modern machines target **100 dB+** using chopper-stabilized or auto-zeroing instrumentation amplifiers (e.g., AD620, INA128).

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — QTc (Bazett's Correction)

The QT interval shortens at high heart rates; Bazett's formula corrects to a "standard" heart rate of 60 bpm:

$$QTc = \frac{QT}{\sqrt{R\!-\!R}}$$

**Worked Example:** Measured QT = 0.36 s; R–R = 0.80 s (HR = 75 bpm).

$$QTc = \frac{0.36}{\sqrt{0.80}} = \frac{0.36}{0.894} = 0.403\,\text{s}$$

$$\boxed{\,QTc \approx 0.40\,\text{s — within normal range}\,}$$

---

## 3.6 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Bipolar vs. Augmented vs. Precordial Leads

| Property | Bipolar Limb (I, II, III) | Augmented Limb (aVR, aVL, aVF) | Precordial (V1–V6) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Reference** | Between two limb electrodes | Modified central terminal (Goldberger) | Wilson's Central Terminal (WCT) |
| **Reference node** | None (true differential) | Avg of *other two* limbs | Avg of RA + LA + LL |
| **Amplitude factor** | 1.0 (raw) | ×1.155 (augmentation) | 1.0 |
| **Plane explored** | Frontal | Frontal | Horizontal (transverse) |
| **Sensitivity** | Lower | Higher (augmented) | Highest (closest to heart) |
| **KTU exam trick** | Use Einthoven's law | Vector at −150°, −30°, +90° | V1–V2 = septal, V3–V4 = anterior, V5–V6 = lateral |

---

## 3.7 Worked Numerical Problem — Full ECG Paper Analysis

**Given an ECG strip (25 mm/s, 10 mm/mV):**
- R-peak 1 at small-box position 5.
- R-peak 2 at small-box position 25.
- R-wave height = 12 mm.
- QRS width = 3 small boxes.
- PR interval = 4 small boxes.

**Step 1 — Heart Rate:**

$$HR = \frac{1500}{25 - 5} = \frac{1500}{20} = 75\,\text{bpm}$$

**Step 2 — R-wave amplitude in mV:**

$$V_R = \frac{12\,\text{mm}}{10\,\text{mm/mV}} = 1.2\,\text{mV}$$

**Step 3 — QRS duration:**

$$\text{QRS} = 3 \times 0.04 = 0.12\,\text{s}\;\;\text{(slightly prolonged; normal ≤ 0.10 s)}$$

**Step 4 — PR interval:**

$$PR = 4 \times 0.04 = 0.16\,\text{s}\;\;\text{(normal: 0.12 – 0.20 s)}$$

$$\boxed{\,HR = 75\,\text{bpm},\; V_R = 1.2\,\text{mV},\; QRS = 0.12\,\text{s (prolonged)},\; PR = 0.16\,\text{s (normal)}\,}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Using $R-R$ in **mm** instead of **small boxes**. Always first convert mm → small boxes (÷ 5) before applying $1500/N$.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Complete ECG Machine Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Patient Body\nCardiac dipole 0.5 to 5 mV] --> B1[Surface Electrodes\n4 Limb + 6 Chest\nAg or Ag-AgCl]
    B1 --> B2[Defibrillation\nProtection Network\nZener + Resistor]
    B2 --> C1[Lead Selector\n10 to 1 MUX\nRotary or CMOS]
    C1 --> D1[Buffer Stage\nUnity Gain Follower\nHigh Zin over 10 GOhm]
    D1 --> D2[Preamplifier\n3 Op-Amp IA\nGain 10 CMRR 100 dB]
    D2 --> E1[Right Leg Driver\nActive CM Feedback\nReduces 50 Hz]
    D1 -. common mode .-> E1
    E1 --> F1[Isolation Barrier\nOptocoupler or\nTransformer Coupled]
    F1 --> G1[Main Amplifier\nVariable Gain 100 to 1000]
    G1 --> H1[Band Pass Filter\n0.05 Hz to 150 Hz\nNotch 50 Hz]
    H1 --> I1[Sensitivity and\nTime Base Control\n10 mm per mV\n25 mm per s]
    I1 --> J1[Output Stage\nThermal Array or\nLCD Display]
    J1 --> K1[Calibration Pulse\n1 mV Reference\nInjected at start]
    I1 --> L1[Microcontroller\nDSP for HR\nQRS Detection]
    L1 --> M1[Digital Display\nand Memory Storage]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid — Lead Network (Einthoven + Goldberger + Wilson)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    RA[RA\nRight Arm] --> I1[Lead I\nLA minus RA]
    LA[LA\nLeft Arm] --> I1
    LA --> I3[Lead III\nLL minus LA]
    LL[LL\nLeft Leg] --> I3
    RA --> I2[Lead II\nLL minus RA]
    LL --> I2
    RA --> AVR[aVR\nRA minus avg LA and LL]
    LA --> AVL[aVL\nLA minus avg RA and LL]
    LL --> AVF[aVF\nLL minus avg RA and LA]
    RA --> WCT[Wilson Central Terminal\nWCT = RA plus LA plus LL over 3]
    LA --> WCT
    LL --> WCT
    WCT --> V1[V1 4 ICS RSB]
    WCT --> V2[V2 4 ICS LSB]
    WCT --> V3[V3 between V2 and V4]
    WCT --> V4[V4 5 ICS MCL]
    WCT --> V5[V5 anterior axillary]
    WCT --> V6[V6 mid axillary]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid — ECG Waveform Components (Normal Sinus Rhythm)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P["P Wave\nAtrial Depolarization\nDuration 0.08 to 0.10 s\nAmplitude under 2.5 mm"] --> PR1["PR Segment\nIsoelectric\nConduction through AV node"]
    PR1 --> PR2["PR Interval\n0.12 to 0.20 s\n3 to 5 small boxes"]
    PR2 --> Q["Q Wave\nSeptal Depolarization\nSmall under 1 mm"]
    Q --> R["R Wave\nMain Ventricular\nDepolarization Peak"]
    R --> S["S Wave\nDownstroke\nof Ventricles"]
    S --> ST["ST Segment\nIsoelectric\nEarly Repolarization"]
    ST --> T["T Wave\nVentricular Repolarization\nAsymmetric"]
    T --> U["U Wave\nSometimes visible\nPapillary muscle repol"]
    U --> TP["TP Segment\nIsoelectric baseline\nfor measurement"]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid — Frequency Spectrum Allocation & Filter Strategy

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    sub1["0.05 Hz\nDC Baseline Drift\nBody Motion"] -->|HPF Cut| sub2[ECG\nPassband\n0.05 to 150 Hz]
    sub2 -->|LPF Cut| sub3["150 Hz\nEMG Noise\nRF Pickup"]
    sub2 -.->|Notch 50 Hz| sub4["50 Hz\nPower Line\nInterference"]
    sub5["Pacemaker Spike\nup to 2 kHz\nDetected separately"] -.-> sub2
```

## 4.5 Mermaid — Patient Safety Isolation Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P[Patient Side\nType CF Applied Part] --> ISO[Galvanic Isolation\nBarrier Rated 5 kV rms\nIEC 60601-1]
    ISO --> M[Mains Side\nRecording and Display]
    P --> LP1[Leakage Current\nLimit under 10 micro A]
    M --> LP2[Earth Leakage\nLimit under 500 micro A]
    ISO --> DEF[Defibrillation Proof\nWithstand 5 kV for 5 s]
    P --> RLD2[Right Leg Drive\nActive Return Path]
    RLD2 --> ISO
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->
