Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Origin of Heart Sounds — Step-by-Step Logic **Step 1 — The Cardiac Cycle Foundation** The heart completes one cycle for every heartbeat, comprising **systole** (contraction) and **diastole** (relaxation). Each phase produces distinctive acoustic events. **Step 2 — First Heart Sound ($S_1$) — "Lub"** - Caused by closure of the **mitral** and **tricuspid** (atrioventricular) valves. - Marks the **onset of systole**. - Dominant frequency content: **20–150 Hz** (lower-pitched). - Heard loudest at the **apex** of the heart (5th intercostal space, mid-clavicular line). **Step 3 — Second Heart Sound ($S_2$) — "Dub"** - Caused by closure of the **aortic** and **pulmonary** (semilunar) valves. - Marks the **end of systole / onset of diastole**. - Dominant frequency content: **50–200 Hz** (higher-pitched). - Has two components: $A_2$ (aortic) and $P_2$ (pulmonary). $A_2$ is normally louder. **Step 4 — Third Heart Sound ($S_3$)** - Caused by rapid ventricular filling during early diastole. - Low frequency (**20–70 Hz**), soft. - Pathological if present after age 40 (suggests heart failure or volume overload). **Step 5 — Fourth Heart Sound ($S_4$)** - Caused by atrial contraction forcing blood into a stiff ventricle. - Very low frequency (**< 20 Hz**), usually inaudible without a phonocardiograph. - Always pathological — indicates **left ventricular hypertrophy** or ischemia. **Step 6 — Murmurs** - Caused by **turbulent blood flow** through stenotic (narrowed) or insufficient (leaky) valves. - Frequency range: **20 Hz – 1000 Hz**. - Classified by timing (systolic/diastolic), shape (crescendo, decrescendo, plateau), and quality (blowing, harsh, musical). ### 2.2 The Phonocardiograph — Block-Level Operating Principle A modern phonocardiograph consists of the following sequential blocks: 1. **Sensor / Transducer** — Converts mechanical vibration of the chest wall into an electrical signal. 2. **Preamplifier** — Boosts the micro-volt level signal (gain $\approx 100$). 3. **Band-Pass Filter** — Restricts the signal to a clinically useful frequency band (typically 20–500 Hz or 20–1000 Hz). 4. **Main Amplifier** — Provides further gain ($\approx 100$). 5. **Display/Recorder** — Cathode Ray Oscilloscope (CRO) or thermal strip-chart recorder. > [!NOTE] > **Why are filters essential?** Heart sounds are contaminated by **lung sounds (200–2000 Hz)**, **muscle artifacts (5–50 Hz)**, and **50 Hz mains hum**. Band-pass filtering isolates the cardiac acoustic signature — a critical signal-conditioning step in any biomedical instrument. ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula / Relationship | Description | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $f = \dfrac{1}{T}$ | Heart sound frequency from period | Hz | | 2 | $HR = \dfrac{60}{T_{RR}}$ | Heart rate from R-R interval | bpm | | 3 | $G_{total} = G_1 \times G_2$ | Cascaded amplifier gain | dimensionless | | 4 | $SNR_{dB} = 20 \log_{10} \left( \dfrac{V_{signal}}{V_{noise}} \right)$ | Signal-to-noise ratio | dB | | 5 | $SPL = 20 \log_{10} \left( \dfrac{P}{P_{ref}} \right)$ | Sound Pressure Level; $P_{ref} = 20\ \mu Pa$ | dB | | 6 | $\dfrac{V_{out}}{V_{in}} = -\dfrac{R_f}{R_{in}}$ | Inverting op-amp gain | dimensionless | | 7 | $f_c = \dfrac{1}{2 \pi RC}$ | Filter cutoff frequency | Hz | | 8 | $S_1$ intensity $\propto v^2$ | Intensity proportional to valve closure velocity squared | — | | 9 | $P_{acoustic} = \dfrac{1}{2} \rho v^2$ | Acoustic power per unit area | W/m² | | 10 | $Z = \rho c$ | Acoustic impedance (air $\approx 413$, tissue $\approx 1.6 \times 10^6$ rayl) | rayl | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Acoustic Impedance Mismatch:** The acoustic impedance of body tissue is roughly **4000 times** that of air. This is why air-coupled stethoscopes lose so much acoustic energy — a problem solved by using **electronic transducers** with direct skin contact. ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Clinical Diagnosis:** Detection of valve disorders (stenosis, regurgitation), congenital heart defects, and cardiomyopathies. - **AI/ML Cardiology:** Modern PCG signals feed deep-learning models for automated murmur classification. - **Wearable Health Monitors:** Smartwatches and patches (e.g., Eko Core, Apple Watch) embed digital stethoscopes for continuous cardiac acoustic monitoring. - **Tele-medicine:** PCG data can be transmitted over networks for remote specialist interpretation. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Total Gain of a Two-Stage Phonocardiograph Amplifier **Problem:** A phonocardiograph preamplifier has a gain $G_1 = 100$, followed by a main amplifier with gain $G_2 = 500$. The transducer outputs a peak voltage of $50\ \mu V$. Find the final output voltage and total system gain in dB. **Given:** - $G_1 = 100$ - $G_2 = 500$ - $V_{in} = 50\ \mu V = 50 \times 10^{-6}\ V$ **Step 1 — Compute Total Voltage Gain** The total gain of cascaded stages is the product of individual gains: $$G_{total} = G_1 \times G_2$$ Substituting the values: $$G_{total} = 100 \times 500 = 50{,}000$$ **Step 2 — Compute Output Voltage** $$V_{out} = G_{total} \times V_{in}$$ $$V_{out} = 50{,}000 \times 50 \times 10^{-6}\ V$$ $$V_{out} = 2.5\ V$$ **Step 3 — Convert Gain to Decibels** Using the voltage-gain definition of dB: $$G_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}(G_{total})$$ $$G_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}(50{,}000)$$ $$\log_{10}(50{,}000) = \log_{10}(5 \times 10^4) = \log_{10}(5) + 4 = 0.6990 + 4 = 4.6990$$ $$G_{dB} = 20 \times 4.6990 = 93.98\ dB$$ **Result:** $V_{out} = 2.5\ V$ and $G_{dB} \approx 94\ dB$. > [!NOTE] > A gain of ~94 dB is typical for a clinical phonocardiograph, confirming the necessity of multi-stage amplification given the micro-volt transducer output. --- ### 3.2 Derivation: Heart Rate from R-R Interval of the PCG **Problem:** The phonocardiogram of a patient shows $S_1$ peaks separated by an average interval of $0.85\ s$. Determine the heart rate in bpm. **Given:** $T_{cycle} = 0.85\ s$ (time between consecutive $S_1$ sounds, equivalent to R-R interval). **Step 1 — Apply the Heart Rate Formula** $$HR = \frac{60}{T_{cycle}}$$ **Step 2 — Substitute** $$HR = \frac{60}{0.85}$$ **Step 3 — Evaluate** $$HR = 70.59\ bpm \approx 71\ bpm$$ **Interpretation:** A heart rate of 71 bpm lies within the normal resting range of **60–100 bpm** — no tachycardia or bradycardia indicated. --- ### 3.3 Derivation: Cutoff Frequency of a Low-Pass Filter for Murmur Suppression **Problem:** A first-order RC low-pass filter is to be used to suppress high-frequency noise above $500\ Hz$. If $R = 10\ k\Omega$, find the required capacitance $C$. **Step 1 — Apply the Cutoff Frequency Formula** $$f_c = \frac{1}{2 \pi R C}$$ **Step 2 — Solve for $C$** $$C = \frac{1}{2 \pi R f_c}$$ **Step 3 — Substitute Numerical Values** $$C = \frac{1}{2 \pi \times (10 \times 10^3) \times 500}$$ $$C = \frac{1}{2 \pi \times 5 \times 10^6}$$ $$C = \frac{1}{3.1416 \times 10^7}$$ $$C = 3.183 \times 10^{-8}\ F$$ $$C \approx 31.83\ nF$$ **Result:** A standard capacitor value of **33 nF** would be selected in practice. --- ### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Heart Sound Transducers | Property | Acoustic Stethoscope | Electronic Stethoscope | Microphone-based PCG | Accelerometer-based PCG | |---|---|---|---|---| | Operating principle | Mechanical sound conduction | Electret mic + amplifier | Air-coupled condenser mic | Direct chest-wall vibration | | Frequency range | 20 Hz – 1 kHz (limited) | 20 Hz – 2 kHz | 20 Hz – 20 kHz | 0.5 Hz – 1 kHz | | Sensitivity | Low | Moderate | High (with noise issues) | High, low ambient noise | | Ambient noise rejection | Poor | Moderate | Poor | Excellent | | Patient isolation | None | Possible | Possible | Possible | | Diagnostic depth | Auscultation only | Recording + DSP | Recording + DSP | Recording + DSP | | Typical use | Bedside screening | Modern clinical | Research-grade PCG | Wearable / continuous monitors | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Phonocardiograph Signal Chain ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Heart Valves
Mechanical Vibration] --> B[Chest Wall
Acoustic Coupling] B --> C[Transducer
Electret Microphone / Accelerometer] C --> D[Preamplifier
Gain = 100] D --> E[Band-Pass Filter
20 Hz to 500 Hz] E --> F[Main Amplifier
Gain = 500] F --> G[Display / Recorder
CRO / Thermal Writer] G --> H[PCG Output
Phonocardiogram] C -.Noise.-> N1[Ambient Noise] D -.Noise.-> N2[Thermal Noise 50 Hz Hum] E -.Noise.-> N3[Muscle Artifact] style A fill:#ffd6d6 style H fill:#d6ffd6 style N1 fill:#eeeeee style N2 fill:#eeeeee style N3 fill:#eeeeee ``` ### 4.2 Mermaid Timing Diagram — Heart Sound Sequence (Cardiac Cycle) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Start of Systole] --> B[S1: Lub
Mitral + Tricuspid Closure
20 to 150 Hz] B --> C[Systole Period
Quiet Interval] C --> D[S2: Dub
Aortic + Pulmonary Closure
50 to 200 Hz] D --> E[Diastole Period] E --> F{S3 or S4 Present?} F -->|Yes| G[Pathological Indicator
Heart Failure or LVH] F -->|No| H[Normal Cardiac Cycle] G --> A H --> A style B fill:#b3d9ff style D fill:#b3d9ff style G fill:#ffb3b3 style H fill:#b3ffb3 ``` ### 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Heart Sound Transducer Comparison Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TD subgraph A1[Mechanical Path] A11[Acoustic Stethoscope
Bell and Diaphragm] end subgraph A2[Electronic Path] A21[Electret Microphone
Air Coupled] A22[Accelerometer / Piezo Sensor
Direct Contact] end subgraph A3[Signal Path] A31[Preamplifier] A32[Band Pass Filter] A33[Main Amplifier] end subgraph A4[Output Path] A41[Display CRO] A42[Digital PCG Software] A43[AI Classification Module] end A11 --> A31 A21 --> A31 A22 --> A31 A31 --> A32 --> A33 A33 --> A41 A33 --> A42 A33 --> A43 style A11 fill:#ffe4b3 style A21 fill:#b3e0ff style A22 fill:#b3e0ff style A43 fill:#d6b3ff ``` ### 4.4 Mermaid Process Flow — Diagnostic Interpretation of PCG ```mermaid flowchart TD P1[Acquire PCG Signal] --> P2[Identify S1 and S2 Peaks] P2 --> P3{S3 or S4 Detected?} P3 -->|No| P4{Normal S1 S2 Splitting?} P3 -->|Yes| P5[Flag Ventricular Dysfunction] P4 -->|Yes| P6[Normal Heart Sounds] P4 -->|No| P7[Check for Murmurs] P7 --> P8{Murmur Frequency Band?} P8 -->|Low 20 to 100 Hz| P9[Stenosis Likely] P8 -->|High 200 to 1000 Hz| P10[Regurgitation Likely] P5 --> P11[Refer to Cardiologist] P6 --> P11 P9 --> P11 P10 --> P11 style P6 fill:#b3ffb3 style P11 fill:#ffd6b3 style P5 fill:#ffb3b3 ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]** *Define Phonocardiogram. List the four normal heart sounds and their primary causes.* **Model Answer:** A **Phonocardiogram (PCG)** is the graphical recording of heart sounds produced by the mechanical activity of heart valves and blood flow, obtained using a phonocardiograph. | Sound | Cause | Frequency | |---|---|---| | $S_1$ (Lub) | Closure of mitral and tricuspid valves | 20–150 Hz | | $S_2$ (Dub) | Closure of aortic and pulmonary valves | 50–200 Hz | | $S_3$ | Rapid ventricular filling in early diastole | 20–70 Hz | | $S_4$ | Atrial contraction against a stiff ventricle | < 20 Hz | --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** *Mention any three applications of a phonocardiograph in clinical diagnosis.* **Model Answer:** 1. **Detection of valve disorders** such as mitral stenosis, aortic regurgitation, and prolapse. 2. **Identification of murmurs** — assessing timing (systolic/diastolic), frequency content, and shape to localize the lesion. 3. **Detection of $S_3$ and $S_4$** — clinically significant for **congestive heart failure** and **left ventricular hypertrophy** respectively. 4. **Monitoring prosthetic valve function** post-cardiac surgery. 5. **Long-term ambulatory cardiac acoustic monitoring** in remote/tele-cardiology setups. --- ### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > **Module 2 Internal Choice**: Solve **either** Question A **or** Question B. --- ### Question A (14 Marks) **Q.A. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]** **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a **Phonocardiograph**. Describe each block in detail. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Differentiate between the four normal heart sounds $S_1$, $S_2$, $S_3$, and $S_4$ based on **cause, frequency range, duration, and clinical significance**. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution **(a) Working of a Phonocardiograph — Block Diagram** Refer to **Section 4.1** for the rendered block diagram. The working explanation: 1. **Transducer (Electret Microphone / Accelerometer):** Mounted on the patient's chest at the auscultation positions (aortic, pulmonary, tricuspid, mitral). Converts the mechanical vibration of the chest wall (caused by heart valve closures) into a proportional electrical signal — typically in the range of **micro-volts**. 2. **Preamplifier:** A low-noise differential amplifier (often instrumentation-grade, e.g., INA128) provides the first stage of gain, typically $\approx 100$ (40 dB). It also rejects common-mode interference such as 50 Hz mains hum. 3. **Band-Pass Filter:** A cascade of high-pass (cutoff $\approx 20$ Hz, to remove DC and motion artifact) and low-pass (cutoff $\approx 500$ Hz, to remove high-frequency lung and ambient noise) filters isolates the cardiac band. Cutoff frequency: $$f_c = \frac{1}{2 \pi R C}$$ 4. **Main Amplifier:** Provides further gain ($\approx 100$–$500$) to bring the signal to a level suitable for display (typically 0.5–5 V peak). 5. **Display / Recorder:** The amplified and filtered signal drives a **CRO** or a **thermal strip-chart recorder**, producing the phonocardiogram. Modern systems digitize via ADC and display on LCD / PC. **(b) Comparison of Heart Sounds** | Feature | $S_1$ | $S_2$ | $S_3$ | $S_4$ | |---|---|---|---|---| | Cause | M and T valve closure | A and P valve closure | Rapid ventricular filling | Atrial contraction | | Cardiac phase | Start of systole | End of systole | Early diastole | Late diastole | | Frequency | 20–150 Hz | 50–200 Hz | 20–70 Hz | < 20 Hz | | Duration | 0.10–0.17 s | 0.06–0.12 s | ~0.04 s | ~0.04 s | | Pitch | Low | Higher | Very low | Very low | | Loudest at | Apex | Base | Apex | Apex | | Clinical significance | Accentuated in mitral stenosis; soft in mitral regurgitation | Splitting indicates pulmonary hypertension or bundle branch block | Pathological > 40 yrs (CHF, volume overload) | Always pathological (LVH, ischemia) | --- ### Question B (14 Marks) **Q.B. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** **(a)** Explain the **origin of murmurs** in the heart. Classify murmurs based on **timing, frequency, and shape**. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A phonocardiograph uses a transducer producing **80 µV** peak output. The preamplifier has a gain of **50**, and the main amplifier has a gain of **800**. Determine: (i) the total voltage gain, (ii) the output voltage, and (iii) the gain in dB. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution **(a) Origin and Classification of Murmurs** **Origin:** Murmurs are abnormal heart sounds produced by **turbulent blood flow** through: - **Stenotic valves** (narrowed openings — e.g., aortic stenosis, mitral stenosis) - **Regurgitant valves** (incomplete closure — e.g., mitral regurgitation) - **Septal defects** (abnormal shunts between chambers) - **Increased blood velocity** (anemia, hyperthyroidism, fever) **Classification:** | Criterion | Type | Description | |---|---|---| | **Timing** | Systolic | Heard between $S_1$ and $S_2$ (e.g., aortic stenosis, mitral regurgitation) | | | Diastolic | Heard between $S_2$ and next $S_1$ (e.g., mitral stenosis, aortic regurgitation) | | | Continuous | Heard throughout systole and diastole (e.g., patent ductus arteriosus) | | **Frequency** | Low-pitched (20–100 Hz) | Rumbles (e.g., mitral stenosis) — best heard with bell | | | Mid-pitched (100–300 Hz) | Harsh sounds (e.g., ventricular septal defect) | | | High-pitched (300–1000 Hz) | Blowing sounds (e.g., aortic regurgitation) — best heard with diaphragm | | **Shape** | Crescendo | Increasing intensity (e.g., aortic stenosis) | | | Decrescendo | Decreasing intensity (e.g., aortic regurgitation) | | | Crescendo–Decrescendo (Diamond) | Loudest in the middle (e.g., aortic stenosis) | | | Plateau | Uniform intensity (e.g., mitral regurgitation) | **(b) Numerical Problem Solution** **Given:** - $V_{in} = 80\ \mu V = 80 \times 10^{-6}\ V$ - $G_1 = 50$ - $G_2 = 800$ **(i) Total Voltage Gain** $$G_{total} = G_1 \times G_2 = 50 \times 800 = 40{,}000$$ **(ii) Output Voltage** $$V_{out} = G_{total} \times V_{in}$$ $$V_{out} = 40{,}000 \times 80 \times 10^{-6}\ V$$ $$V_{out} = 3.2\ V$$ **(iii) Gain in dB** $$G_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}(40{,}000) = 20 \times [\log_{10}(4) + 4]$$ $$G_{dB} = 20 \times [0.6021 + 4] = 20 \times 4.6021 = 92.04\ dB$$ **Final Answer:** $G_{total} = 40{,}000$; $V_{out} = 3.2\ V$; $G_{dB} \approx 92\ dB$. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mistakes to Avoid:** > 1. **Confusing $G_1 \times G_2$ with $G_1 + G_2$** — voltage gains multiply, they do *not* add. > 2. **Forgetting the factor of 20 in dB conversion** — voltage gain uses $20 \log_{10}$, *not* $10 \log_{10}$ (which is for power). > 3. **Mixing up frequency ranges of $S_1$ and $S_2$** — $S_2$ has *higher* frequency than $S_1$, not lower. > 4. **Writing S3 as normal in adults** — it is *physiological* in children/young adults but *pathological* after age 40. > 5. **Stating that all murmurs are pathological** — some are *innocent/physiological*, especially in children. > 6. **Skipping units in numerical answers** — KTU examiners deduct 0.5–1 mark for missing units. > 7. **Drawing the PCG block diagram without arrows or labels** — every block and signal path must be labeled; an unlabeled diagram fetches 0 marks. --- ### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Phonocardiography (PCG)** is the recording of heart sounds using transducers, amplifiers, filters, and a display. - **Four normal heart sounds:** $S_1$ (Lub, M+T valve closure, 20–150 Hz), $S_2$ (Dub, A+P valve closure, 50–200 Hz), $S_3$ (early diastolic filling, 20–70 Hz, pathological after age 40), $S_4$ (atrial kick, < 20 Hz, always pathological). - **Murmurs** are caused by turbulent flow due to stenosis, regurgitation, or septal defects; classified by **timing, frequency, and shape**. - **Phonocardiograph signal chain:** Transducer → Preamplifier → Band-Pass Filter → Main Amplifier → Display/Recorder. - **Total cascaded voltage gain** = product of individual stage gains; **dB conversion** uses $20 \log_{10}(G)$. - **Acoustic impedance mismatch** between air and tissue (~4000:1) is the main reason for using **contact transducers** over air-coupled designs. - **Frequency range of clinical PCG:** 20 Hz – 1000 Hz; filters must reject 50 Hz mains hum, lung sounds, and muscle artifacts. - **Common transducers:** Electret microphone, piezoelectric accelerometer, condenser microphone. - **Typical PCG gain:** 80–100 dB total, requiring two-stage amplification. - **Modern trend:** PCG is increasingly paired with **AI-based classifiers** for automated detection of murmurs and valvular heart disease.

topics.title: Phonocardiography
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMRT412 - BIOMEDICAL INSTRUMENTATION > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Phonocardiography # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Phonocardiography (PCG)** is the technique of graphically recording the sounds produced by the heart, especially the **heart sounds (S1, S2, S3, S4)** and any associated **murmurs**, using a sensitive audio pickup transducer, signal conditioning circuitry, and a display/recording device. The graphical output is called a **Phonocardiogram (PCG)**. The instrument used to perform this recording is called a **Phonocardiograph**. The frequency range of clinically relevant heart sounds spans approximately **20 Hz to 2000 Hz**, with murmurs occasionally extending up to **3000 Hz**. ## Intuitive Analogy (Plain-English View) > [!NOTE] > Think of the heart as a **mechanical pump with two sets of valves** snapping shut in a precise, rhythmic sequence. Every time a valve closes, it produces a vibration, much like the *"click"* of a closing door. > > - Your **stethoscope** lets you *listen* to these clicks. > - A **phonocardiograph** *paints* these clicks on paper as wavy lines, the way a seismograph paints an earthquake. > - The shape, timing, loudness, and frequency of the lines tell a cardiologist whether the valves are healthy or diseased. In essence, phonocardiography converts **mechanical acoustic energy** (heart sounds) into **electrical signals**, and then into a **time-amplitude graph** that can be studied at leisure. ## Types of Heart Sounds (The "Cast of Characters") | Sound | When it Occurs | Cause | Typical Frequency | Duration | |-------|---------------|-------|-------------------|----------| | **S1 ("lub")** | Beginning of **Systole** | Closure of **Mitral (M2)** and **Tricuspid (T1)** AV valves | 30 – 100 Hz | 0.14 s | | **S2 ("dub")** | End of **Systole** | Closure of **Aortic (A2)** and **Pulmonary (P2)** semilunar valves | 50 – 150 Hz | 0.11 s | | **S3** | Early **Diastole** | Rapid ventricular filling | 20 – 50 Hz | 0.04 s | | **S4** | Late **Diastole** | Atrial contraction ("atrial kick") | 20 – 50 Hz | 0.04 s | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Systole** = the interval **S1 → S2** (ventricular contraction & ejection). > **Diastole** = the interval **S2 → next S1** (ventricular relaxation & filling). > A normal adult heart rate is **60–100 bpm**, with systole ≈ 0.3 s and diastole ≈ 0.5 s at rest. ## Murmurs – The "Unexpected Guests" > [!NOTE] > **Murmurs** are prolonged, "whooshing" sounds caused by **turbulent blood flow** through stenotic (narrowed) or regurgitant (leaky) valves, septal defects, or high-velocity jets. They are classified by: > - **Timing**: Systolic, Diastolic, Continuous > - **Shape**: Crescendo, Decrescendo, Diamond-shaped, Plateau > - **Frequency**: Low (<80 Hz), Medium, High (>120 Hz) > - **Loudness (Levine scale)**: Grade 1 (faint) → Grade 6 (audible without stethoscope) # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Complete Signal Chain of a Phonocardiograph A PCG system is a **special-purpose auscultation amplifier**. The signal flow is: **Acoustic Source (Heart) → Acoustic Coupler (Air column/Contact) → Transducer (Mic/Accelerometer/PZT) → Pre-amplifier → Band-Pass Filter Bank → Main Amplifier → Display (CRO/Paper/PC)** Each block has a defined role: ### Block 1 – Pickup / Transducer > [!NOTE] > Three transducer families dominate PCG: > 1. **Air-coupled condenser microphone** – high sensitivity, wide frequency response (20 Hz – 20 kHz), fragile, needs charging. > 2. **Contact (piezoelectric) accelerometer** – rugged, low-power, attaches directly to chest wall, attenuated high frequencies. > 3. **Dynamic/electret microphone** – used in electronic stethoscopes, good mid-band response. ### Block 2 – Pre-amplifier - High **input impedance** (≥ 10 MΩ) to avoid loading the high-impedance piezoelectric source. - **Low noise** (input-referred noise ≤ 1 µV RMS). - Gain typically **20–40 dB**. ### Block 3 – Band-Pass Filter Bank > [!IMPORTANT] > Heart sounds occupy the **infrasonic** to **low-audio** spectrum. PCG systems use **switchable filters** to isolate the four heart-sound bands: > - **S1 filter**: 30 – 100 Hz band-pass > - **S2 filter**: 50 – 150 Hz band-pass > - **S3/S4 filter**: 20 – 50 Hz band-pass > - **Murmur filter**: 100 – 1000 Hz band-pass This filtering allows the diagnostician to "magnify" one event without losing it in the others. ### Block 4 – Display / Recording - **Multi-channel strip-chart recorder** (synchronized with ECG lead II as a timing reference) is the clinical gold standard. - Modern systems digitize the waveform via an **ADC** (≥ 4 kHz sampling rate) and display on PC with **LabVIEW / MATLAB** post-processing. ## 2.2 Why a Phonocardiograph is Needed (Beyond the Stethoscope) | Feature | Acoustic Stethoscope | Phonocardiograph | |---|---|---| | Frequency range | 100 – 1000 Hz (limited by human ear) | 20 – 2000 Hz (full spectrum) | | Time resolution | Real-time ear only | Permanent graphical record | | Objective measurement | No | Yes (amplitude, duration, frequency) | | Filtering | None | Switchable band-pass | | Storage | No | Yes (paper / digital) | | Telemetry | No | Yes (analog/digital) | ## 2.3 Frequency Content & Spectral Domains (KTU Formula Sheet) > [!NOTE] > The table below consolidates **every formula, value, and range** the KTU examiner can test on this topic. | # | Quantity | Formula / Value | Units | Notes | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Heart rate (HR) | $\text{HR} = \dfrac{60}{T_{RR}}$ | bpm | $T_{RR}$ = R-to-R interval on ECG in seconds | | 2 | Systolic duration | $T_{sys} \approx 0.3 \text{ s}$ | s | At rest, 60 bpm | | 3 | Diastolic duration | $T_{dia} \approx 0.5 \text{ s}$ | s | At rest, 60 bpm | | 4 | Total cardiac cycle | $T = T_{sys} + T_{dia} = \dfrac{60}{\text{HR}}$ | s | | | 5 | Lower-cutoff for S1 | $f_{L,S1} = 30 \text{ Hz}$ | Hz | High-pass edge | | 6 | Upper-cutoff for S1 | $f_{H,S1} = 100 \text{ Hz}$ | Hz | Low-pass edge | | 7 | S2 frequency band | $50 \leq f_{S2} \leq 150$ | Hz | Higher pitch than S1 | | 8 | S3 / S4 band | $20 \leq f_{S3/S4} \leq 50$ | Hz | Low-frequency gallop | | 9 | Murmur band | $100 \leq f_{murmur} \leq 1000$ | Hz | Can extend to 2 kHz | | 10 | Overall PCG bandwidth | $20 \text{ Hz} \leq f \leq 2000 \text{ Hz}$ | Hz | Clinical window | | 11 | Sampling rate (Nyquist) | $f_s \geq 2 \, f_{max} \geq 4 \text{ kHz}$ | Hz | Anti-aliasing requirement | | 12 | Wavelength of S1 in tissue | $\lambda = \dfrac{v}{f}$ with $v \approx 1540 \text{ m/s}$ | m | For f = 50 Hz, λ ≈ 30 m → pure pressure wave, not localized | | 13 | Q–S2 interval (electromechanical) | $\text{QS2} = \text{Q–S1} + \text{S1–S2}$ | ms | Systolic timing index | | 14 | Pre-amp gain (dB) | $G_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{V_{out}}{V_{in}}\right)$ | dB | Typical 20–40 dB | | 15 | Total system gain | $G_{total} = G_1 + G_2 + \dots + G_n$ | dB | Sum because of dB units | | 16 | Input impedance (PZT) | $Z_{in} \geq 10 \text{ M}\Omega$ | Ω | To avoid loading | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical KTU Note:** When asked *"which filter for S1?"*, the model answer is **Band-Pass Filter 30 Hz – 100 Hz**. > For S2, answer **50 Hz – 150 Hz**. For murmurs, answer **100 Hz – 1000 Hz**. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Valvular disease diagnosis** (stenosis, regurgitation) - **Prosthetic valve assessment** (mechanical vs bioprosthetic sound signatures) - **Pediatric cardiology** (innocent murmurs, congenital defects) - **Wearable cardiac monitors** (PCG + ECG fused via DSP) - **Tele-auscultation** (COVID-era remote stethoscopes) - **AI/ML training data** (deep-learning models classify PCG into normal/abnormal) # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Required Sampling Rate from the Nyquist Theorem > The highest clinically significant heart-sound frequency is the **diastolic rumble of mitral stenosis** or **high-frequency aortic regurgitation murmur**, both reaching about **$f_{max} = 2000 \text{ Hz}$**. By the Nyquist–Shannon sampling theorem, to avoid aliasing we require: $$ f_s \;\geq\; 2 \, f_{max} $$ Substituting the PCG upper limit: $$ f_s \;\geq\; 2 \times 2000 \text{ Hz} $$ $$ f_s \;\geq\; 4000 \text{ Hz} = 4 \text{ kHz} $$ In practice, designers choose $f_s = 8 \text{ kHz}$ (oversampling factor of 2×) so that the anti-aliasing analog low-pass filter has a gentle roll-off and is realizable with low order. This justifies the **8 kHz / 16-bit** specification seen on commercial PCG digitizers. > [!NOTE] > **Logical meaning:** A 4 kHz sample rate is the *theoretical floor*; 8 kHz gives the engineer headroom. ## 3.2 Derivation: Heart-Rate from R–R Interval A patient has an ECG R–R interval $T_{RR} = 0.86 \text{ s}$ as measured from the synchronized lead II trace. Compute HR: $$ \text{HR} = \dfrac{60}{T_{RR}} $$ $$ \text{HR} = \dfrac{60}{0.86 \text{ s}} $$ $$ \text{HR} \approx 69.77 \text{ bpm} $$ Rounded to the standard clinical precision: $$ \boxed{\text{HR} \approx 70 \text{ bpm}} $$ **Interpretation:** Within the normal resting range of **60 – 100 bpm** (normal sinus rhythm). The PCG will therefore show systole and diastole of approximately equal widths at this rate. ## 3.3 Derivation: System Gain Cascade (dB) Suppose a PCG pre-amplifier has a voltage gain of 10, a band-pass filter passes the signal unchanged (gain = 1), and the main amplifier has a gain of 50. **Step 1 — Linear gains:** $$ G_1 = 10, \qquad G_2 = 1, \qquad G_3 = 50 $$ **Step 2 — Total linear gain (multiplication):** $$ G_{total,\,lin} = G_1 \times G_2 \times G_3 = 10 \times 1 \times 50 = 500 $$ **Step 3 — Convert to dB:** $$ G_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}(G_{lin}) $$ $$ G_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}(500) $$ $$ G_{dB} = 20 \times 2.6990 $$ $$ \boxed{G_{dB} \approx 53.98 \text{ dB} \approx 54 \text{ dB}} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key takeaway for KTU:** When *all* stages are in **dB**, simply **add** them. When in **linear ratio**, **multiply**. Mixing the two is the #1 silly mistake. ## 3.4 Derivation: Wavelength of the S1 Sound in Soft Tissue For a 50 Hz component of S1 travelling in soft tissue (speed of sound $v \approx 1540 \text{ m/s}$): $$ \lambda = \dfrac{v}{f} $$ $$ \lambda = \dfrac{1540 \text{ m/s}}{50 \text{ Hz}} $$ $$ \boxed{\lambda \approx 30.8 \text{ m}} $$ **Interpretation:** Because $\lambda \gg$ heart dimensions (~0.1 m), the heart-sound field behaves as a **localized pressure variation**, not a directional wavefront. The PCG microphone therefore senses the *time-derivative* of this pressure rather than a propagating wave — which is why **piezoelectric contact sensors** work well and why the chest wall acts as a low-pass mechanical filter (~100 Hz corner). ## 3.5 Derivation: Filtering the Four Events – Worked Example A phonocardiograph receives a signal containing all four sounds. Design a **4-channel analog band-pass filter bank** using 2nd-order Butterworth sections (–40 dB/decade roll-off). | Channel | Pass-band | Purpose | |---|---|---| | CH-1 | 30 – 100 Hz | Isolate S1 | | CH-2 | 50 – 150 Hz | Isolate S2 | | CH-3 | 20 – 50 Hz | Isolate S3 / S4 | | CH-4 | 100 – 1000 Hz | Isolate murmurs | **Cut-off frequency (geometric mean) for each band-pass:** $$ f_c = \sqrt{f_{L} \cdot f_{H}} $$ For S1 channel: $$ f_{c,S1} = \sqrt{30 \times 100} = \sqrt{3000} \approx 54.77 \text{ Hz} $$ For S2 channel: $$ f_{c,S2} = \sqrt{50 \times 150} = \sqrt{7500} \approx 86.60 \text{ Hz} $$ For S3/S4 channel: $$ f_{c,S3/S4} = \sqrt{20 \times 50} = \sqrt{1000} \approx 31.62 \text{ Hz} $$ For murmur channel: $$ f_{c,murmur} = \sqrt{100 \times 1000} = \sqrt{100000} \approx 316.23 \text{ Hz} $$ > [!NOTE] > These centre frequencies confirm the qualitative ordering: S1/S3/S4 lie in the bass, S2 in the mid, and murmurs in the treble. The Q-factor ($Q = f_c / \text{BW}$) tells how "sharp" the filter is. For PCG, **wide-band** filters (low Q ≈ 1.5) are preferred to preserve the natural transient character of the sounds. ## 3.6 Analytical Summary Matrix | Concept | Quantitative Anchor | Clinical Translation | |---|---|---| | Bandwidth 20 – 2000 Hz | $f_s \geq 4$ kHz | Capture every audible event | | S1 @ 30 – 100 Hz | $\lambda \approx 30$ m | Pressure pulse, not wave | | HR = 60 / $T_{RR}$ | 70 bpm for $T_{RR}=0.86$ s | One cycle ≈ 0.86 s | | Cascade gain | 54 dB ≈ 500× | Microvolt-level PZT → 5 mV display | | Filter 30–100 Hz | $f_c \approx 55$ Hz | S1 isolation | | Filter 50–150 Hz | $f_c \approx 87$ Hz | S2 isolation | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Phonocardiograph The Mermaid diagram below traces the **complete signal path**, from the beating heart to the doctor's screen, including all filtering, amplification, and synchronization stages. ```mermaid flowchart LR A1[HEART
Acoustic Source] A2[Chest Wall
Mechanical Coupling] A3[Air Column OR
Direct Contact] A4[Transducer
PZT or Electret Mic] A5[Pre-Amplifier
High Z, Low Noise] A6[BPF Bank
CH1 30-100 Hz] A7[BPF Bank
CH2 50-150 Hz] A8[BPF Bank
CH3 20-50 Hz] A9[BPF Bank
CH4 100-1000 Hz] A10[Main Amplifier
Total Gain 54 dB] A11[ADC
fs 8 kHz 16 bit] A12[Display
PC CRO Strip Chart] A13[ECG Lead II
Timing Reference] A14[Recorder Sync
Time Stamp] A1 --> A2 --> A3 --> A4 --> A5 A5 --> A6 A5 --> A7 A5 --> A8 A5 --> A9 A6 --> A10 A7 --> A10 A8 --> A10 A9 --> A10 A10 --> A11 --> A12 A13 --> A14 --> A12 ``` ## 4.2 Phonocardiogram Waveform & Cardiac Cycle Timing ```mermaid flowchart LR N1["S1
30 to 100 Hz
0.14 s"] N2["Systole
Quiet 0.3 s"] N3["S2
50 to 150 Hz
0.11 s"] N4["Diastole
Quiet 0.5 s"] N5["S3
20 to 50 Hz
0.04 s"] N6["S4
20 to 50 Hz
0.04 s"] N1 --> N2 --> N3 --> N5 --> N4 --> N6 --> N1 A1[ECG QRS] -.timing.-> N1 A2[ECG T wave] -.timing.-> N3 ``` > [!NOTE] > The QRS complex of the ECG is the **timing reference**. S1 begins ~50 ms after the QRS, and S2 occurs at the end of the T wave. This **electro-mechanical delay** is the basis of the **QS2 index** used in systolic-time-interval analysis. ## 4.3 Filter-Bank Selection Logic (Mapping Sound → Frequency Channel) ```mermaid flowchart TD start{Pick Sound
of Interest} start -->|S1| f1["Use 30 to 100 Hz Band-Pass
fc approx 55 Hz"] start -->|S2| f2["Use 50 to 150 Hz Band-Pass
fc approx 87 Hz"] start -->|S3 or S4| f3["Use 20 to 50 Hz Band-Pass
fc approx 32 Hz"] start -->|Murmur| f4["Use 100 to 1000 Hz Band-Pass
fc approx 316 Hz"] f1 --> display[Time Amplitude Plot] f2 --> display f3 --> display f4 --> display ``` ## 4.4 Typical PCG Trace (Idealized, Synchronized with ECG) ```mermaid flowchart LR e["ECG Lead II
P QRS T"] s1["S1 Amplitude High
Duration 0.14 s"] s2["S2 Amplitude Medium
Duration 0.11 s"] s3["S3 Small
Low Frequency"] s4["S4 Small
Low Frequency"] e --> s1 s1 --> s2 s2 --> s3 s3 --> s4 s4 -.next cycle.-> s1 ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > A **wide splitting of S2** (A2 – P2 interval > 30 ms) suggests **Right Bundle Branch Block** or **Pulmonary Stenosis**. A **fixed wide split** suggests **Atrial Septal Defect (ASD)**. These patterns are detected visually on a PCG trace. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A – Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 / Model Q1]` **Q1. Define phonocardiography. List the four normal heart sounds and their typical frequency ranges.** **Model Answer:** > [!NOTE] > **Phonocardiography** is the graphic recording of heart sounds and murmurs produced by valve closures and turbulent blood flow. The four normal heart sounds are: > - **S1 (lub)** – Closure of mitral & tricuspid valves — **30 – 100 Hz** > - **S2 (dub)** – Closure of aortic & pulmonary valves — **50 – 150 Hz** > - **S3** – Rapid ventricular filling — **20 – 50 Hz** > - **S4** – Atrial contraction — **20 – 50 Hz** --- > `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 / Model Q2]` **Q2. Why is a band-pass filter bank used in a phonocardiograph instead of a single wide-band filter? Mention typical bands for S1 and S2.** **Model Answer:** A single wide-band filter would display all four sounds superimposed, making it difficult to isolate any one event. A **switchable band-pass filter bank** allows the clinician to select the frequency range of interest, suppressing the rest. This improves the **signal-to-noise ratio** for the chosen sound and enables accurate measurement of its amplitude, duration, and timing. - **S1 band-pass:** 30 – 100 Hz - **S2 band-pass:** 50 – 150 Hz --- ## 5.2 Part B – Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (read before answering):** > - Never write only definitions without **block diagrams** – PCG is a **system** question, **–2 marks** if no diagram. > - **Forgetting the ECG as a timing reference** = **–2 marks** lost on the synchronization discussion. > - **Confusing systole and diastole** is the most common error. Memorize: S1 = start of systole; S2 = end of systole. > - **Units missing on frequency values** = **–1 mark** penalty. > - **Forgetting Nyquist rate** for digital PCG = lose **2 marks** in any modern-systems sub-question. --- ### Question A – 14 Marks (Choice 1) > `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the working of a **phonocardiograph**. Discuss the role of each block. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Compare the characteristics (timing, cause, frequency) of S1, S2, S3 and S4. What is the clinical significance of S3 and S4? **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution **Part (a) – Phonocardiograph block diagram and working (7 marks)** ``` [HEART] → [Chest Wall Coupling] → [Microphone/Transducer] → [Pre-Amplifier] → [Band-Pass Filter Bank] → [Main Amplifier] → [Display/Recorder + ECG sync] ``` **Block-by-block explanation:** 1. **Acoustic source (Heart):** Generates vibrations (20 Hz – 2 kHz) when valves open/close. 2. **Coupling medium (Air column or direct contact):** Transmits vibrations from chest wall to transducer. 3. **Transducer (Microphone / PZT):** Converts acoustic/mechanical vibrations into a proportional **electrical voltage signal**. Sensitivity typically 5–50 mV/Pa. 4. **Pre-Amplifier:** Provides initial voltage gain (~20–40 dB), high input impedance (≥10 MΩ) and very low noise (≤1 µV). 5. **Band-Pass Filter Bank:** Switchable filters isolate S1 (30–100 Hz), S2 (50–150 Hz), S3/S4 (20–50 Hz) and murmurs (100–1000 Hz). Improves SNR. 6. **Main Amplifier:** Raises signal to displayable level (overall gain ≈ 54 dB). 7. **Display / Recorder:** Multi-channel paper chart recorder synchronized with **ECG Lead II** as a timing reference; modern systems use a PC with ADC (8 kHz, 16-bit). **Key engineering rationale:** ECG is recorded simultaneously so that the timing of S1, S2, etc. can be precisely correlated with electrical events. Without ECG sync, the PCG trace has no absolute time base. --- **Part (b) – Comparison of S1, S2, S3, S4 & clinical significance (7 marks)** | Parameter | S1 | S2 | S3 | S4 | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Timing** | Start of systole | End of systole | Early diastole | Late diastole | | **Cause** | M2 + T1 closure | A2 + P2 closure | Rapid ventricular filling | Atrial contraction | | **Frequency** | 30 – 100 Hz | 50 – 150 Hz | 20 – 50 Hz | 20 – 50 Hz | | **Duration** | ≈ 0.14 s | ≈ 0.11 s | ≈ 0.04 s | ≈ 0.04 s | | **Pitch** | Low | Higher | Lowest | Lowest | **Clinical significance:** > [!NOTE] > - **Loud S1:** Mitral stenosis (leaflets widely open at onset of systole). > - **Loud S2 (A2):** Systemic hypertension. > - **Wide split S2:** Right Bundle Branch Block, Pulmonary Stenosis. > - **Fixed split S2:** Atrial Septal Defect. > - **S3 (S3 gallop):** Volume overload — heart failure, mitral regurgitation. Normal in children/young adults. > - **S4 (atrial gallop):** "Atrial kick" against a stiff ventricle — Hypertension, Hypertrophic Cardiomyopathy, Aortic Stenosis. A **combined S3 + S4** producing a "summation gallop" at high heart rates is a hallmark of **congestive heart failure**. --- ### Question B – 14 Marks (Choice 2) > `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **(a)** Explain the **principle of phonocardiography**. Discuss the various types of **transducers** used in PCG with their relative merits. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Design the **filter bank** for a phonocardiograph that must isolate S1, S2, S3/S4 and murmurs. Justify the sampling rate for digital recording. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution **Part (a) – Principle & transducer types (7 marks)** **Principle:** The heart generates mechanical vibrations (20 Hz – 2 kHz) during valve closure and turbulent flow. These vibrations are coupled to a transducer that converts the **acoustic/mechanical energy** into a **proportional electrical signal**. After amplification, filtering, and synchronization with ECG, the signal is graphically recorded as a **time-amplitude phonocardiogram**. **Transducers used:** 1. **Air-coupled condenser microphone** - *Merits:* Wide frequency response (20 Hz – 20 kHz), high sensitivity, faithful reproduction. - *Demerits:* Fragile, requires polarization voltage, bulky. 2. **Contact piezoelectric (PZT) accelerometer** - *Merits:* Rugged, low-power, small, immune to ambient noise, high impedance output. - *Demerits:* Attenuates high frequencies (>1 kHz), lower sensitivity. 3. **Electret / Dynamic microphone** - *Merits:* Cheap, lightweight, used in electronic stethoscopes. - *Demerits:* Limited low-frequency response, picks up ambient noise. > [!NOTE] > **Selection rule:** *Clinical cardiology* → condenser mic. *Wearable / ICU monitoring* → PZT accelerometer. *Telemedicine / low-cost devices* → electret mic. --- **Part (b) – Filter bank design and sampling-rate justification (7 marks)** **Filter bank design:** | Channel | Pass-band | Centre frequency $f_c=\sqrt{f_L f_H}$ | Target event | |---|---|---|---| | CH-1 | 30 – 100 Hz | $\sqrt{3000} \approx 55$ Hz | S1 | | CH-2 | 50 – 150 Hz | $\sqrt{7500} \approx 87$ Hz | S2 | | CH-3 | 20 – 50 Hz | $\sqrt{1000} \approx 32$ Hz | S3 / S4 | | CH-4 | 100 – 1000 Hz | $\sqrt{100000} \approx 316$ Hz | Murmurs | A 2nd-order **Butterworth** response is preferred because of its maximally-flat pass-band (preserves the natural transient envelope of heart sounds). Each filter is implemented as a cascaded **high-pass** + **low-pass** section (Sallen-Key topology). **Sampling-rate justification:** The clinical spectrum of heart sounds extends up to $f_{max} \approx 2 \text{ kHz}$. By the **Nyquist–Shannon theorem**: $$ f_s \geq 2 f_{max} = 2 \times 2000 = 4000 \text{ Hz} $$ A practical design uses $f_s = 8 \text{ kHz}$ (oversampling factor of 2×) so that the anti-aliasing analog filter is realizable with a low-order roll-off. Hence a PCG digitizer is typically specified as **8 kHz, 16-bit, ≥ 60 dB SNR**. > [!WARNING] > **Common pitfall:** Students quote $f_s = 4 \text{ kHz}$ and stop. The examiner expects a **practical** value of **8 kHz** with the engineering justification (anti-aliasing filter realizability + dynamic range headroom). --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-yield rapid-fire facts (memorize for KTU):** > > - **PCG** = graphic record of heart sounds; device = **Phonocardiograph**. > - **Four sounds:** S1 (30–100 Hz), S2 (50–150 Hz), S3 (20–50 Hz), S4 (20–50 Hz). > - **S1 = M2 + T1** (mitral + tricuspid closure) → start of systole. > - **S2 = A2 + P2** (aortic + pulmonary closure) → end of systole. > - **S3** = rapid ventricular filling (early diastole). > - **S4** = atrial contraction (late diastole, "atrial kick"). > - **PCG bandwidth** = **20 Hz – 2000 Hz**. > - **Minimum sampling rate** by Nyquist = **4 kHz**; **practical** = **8 kHz**. > - **Transducers:** Condenser mic, PZT accelerometer, Electret mic. > - **Pre-amp requirements:** High Z (≥10 MΩ), low noise (≤1 µV), gain 20–40 dB. > - **Filter bank:** 4 switchable band-pass filters; Butterworth preferred. > - **ECG Lead II** is the **timing reference** for the PCG trace. > - **Murmurs** = turbulent flow; graded **Levine 1–6**; classified by timing/shape/pitch. > - **Wide split S2** → RBBB / Pulmonary stenosis; **Fixed split S2** → ASD. > - **S3 gallop** → volume overload (heart failure, MR); **S4 gallop** → stiff ventricle (HCM, HTN, AS). > - **Cascade gain in dB = sum**, **linear gain = product**; **dB formula** $G_{dB}=20\log_{10}(V_{out}/V_{in})$. > - **HR formula:** $\text{HR} = 60 / T_{RR}$ (bpm, $T_{RR}$ in seconds). > - **Speed of sound in soft tissue:** $v \approx 1540$ m/s → S1 wavelengths ≈ 30 m (pressure-pulse regime). > - **Filter centre frequency** for a band-pass: $f_c = \sqrt{f_L \cdot f_H}$. > - **Systole** = S1 → S2; **Diastole** = S2 → next S1.

topics.title: Electroencephalogram (EEG) Measurement
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMRT412 - BIOMEDICAL INSTRUMENTATION > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Electroencephalogram (EEG) Measurement # 🧠 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!NOTE] > **Electroencephalogram (EEG)** is a non-invasive bioelectrical recording technique that captures the **summed post-synaptic electrical activity of millions of cortical neurons** through electrodes placed on the scalp surface. The resulting trace, the *electroencephalogram*, plots **voltage (µV) versus time (ms/s)** and is the gold standard for diagnosing epilepsy, sleep disorders, encephalopathies, and cerebral death. The cortical neurons (mainly **pyramidal cells** in cortical layers III, V, and VI) act as biological **dipoles**. When they fire synchronously, their extracellular currents sum to produce a measurable potential difference on the scalp — typically in the range of **1 µV to 100 µV** (most often **10–50 µV**), with frequency content from **0.5 Hz to 30 Hz** (extending up to **100 Hz** for intracranial EEG). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Board Exam Highlight:** The examiner expects students to know that the EEG is a *gross* (summed) signal — *not* the action potential of a single neuron. Always mention the role of **pyramidal cells** and **synchronous dipole summation** to score full marks. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition Imagine you are standing outside a **large football stadium** with **100,000 fans inside**, all clapping randomly. Outside, you can hear almost nothing because the noise cancels out. But the moment a goal is scored and **everyone claps in unison** — the sound suddenly *thunders* through the walls. Your brain works the same way: - **Neurons firing randomly** → noise, no EEG signal detected. - **Neurons firing synchronously** (e.g., when awake & relaxed with eyes closed → **alpha rhythm**) → a clean, rhythmic wave appears on the EEG trace. The **EEG machine is essentially a "stethoscope for the brain's electrical symphony"** — it listens to *the coordinated chorus* of neurons, not the random chatter. > [!TIP] > **Real-world mapping:** Each EEG electrode is a microphone. The scalp is the stadium wall. The preamplifier is the audio mixer. The display is the speaker. The neurologist is the DJ trying to read the crowd's mood (seizure, sleep, alertness). --- ## 1.3 Physical Constants, Standards & Key Metrics | Parameter | Standard Value / Range | Clinical Significance | |---|---|---| | **EEG signal amplitude** | **1 µV – 100 µV** (typical **10–50 µV**) | Extremely low — needs high-gain amplification | | **EEG signal bandwidth** | **0.5 Hz – 30 Hz** (clinical); up to **100 Hz** (research) | Filters must match this band | | **Common-mode voltage (CMRR needed)** | **≥ 80 dB** (minimum); **> 100 dB** preferred | Rejects 50 Hz mains interference | | **Electrode-skin impedance** | **< 5 kΩ** (clinical), **< 2 kΩ** (research) | Achieved via skin prep & conductive paste | | **Sampling rate (digital EEG)** | **≥ 200 Hz** (clinical), **≥ 500 Hz** (research) | Satisfies Nyquist criterion | | **ADC resolution** | **≥ 12 bits** (clinical), **16 bits** (research) | Captures 1 µV resolution | | **Reference standard** | **International 10–20 Electrode Placement System** (Jasper, 1958) | Universally followed | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Memorize this:** Amplitude in **microvolts (µV)**, frequency in **Hertz (Hz)**, electrode impedance in **kilo-ohms (kΩ)**. The EEG is a *µV-level, low-frequency* signal — a textbook case study in **biopotential acquisition**. # ⚙️ SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Origin of the EEG Signal — The Neuroscience Behind It The cortex of the human brain is approximately **2–4 mm thick** and contains roughly **10¹¹ neurons** (100 billion). However, only a specific subset contributes to scalp EEG: - **Pyramidal cells** in cortical layers **III, V, and VI** are aligned **perpendicular to the cortical surface**. - Their apical dendrites extend toward the pia mater. - When excitatory post-synaptic potentials (EPSPs) occur at the dendrites, **current sinks** form near the soma and **current sources** at the apical dendrites — generating a **dipole** oriented perpendicular to the cortex. > **Synchronous summation rule:** For a dipole to be visible on the scalp, at least **~10 cm² of cortical tissue** must activate simultaneously with the same polarity. --- ## 2.2 EEG Rhythms / Frequency Bands (KTU Favorite) The five canonical EEG rhythms are **must-know** for any KTU paper: | Rhythm | Frequency Band | Amplitude | Clinical / Behavioral State | |---|---|---|---| | **Delta (δ)** | **0.5 – 4 Hz** | High (20–200 µV) | Deep (slow-wave) sleep, infants, pathological coma | | **Theta (θ)** | **4 – 8 Hz** | Moderate (5–20 µV) | Drowsiness, light sleep, meditation, childhood | | **Alpha (α)** | **8 – 13 Hz** | Moderate (20–60 µV) | Awake, relaxed, **eyes closed** (posterior dominant) | | **Beta (β)** | **13 – 30 Hz** | Low (2–20 µV) | Active thinking, alert, focused mental activity | | **Gamma (γ)** | **> 30 Hz** | Very low (< 2 µV) | Higher cognitive processing, perception binding | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Trick:** If the examiner asks "Which rhythm is enhanced when the subject closes their eyes?", the answer is **Alpha (α) rhythm, also called the Berger's rhythm** (after Hans Berger who first recorded human EEG in 1924). --- ## 2.3 The International 10–20 Electrode Placement System The **10-20 system** is the **worldwide standard** for EEG electrode placement. It is named because electrode positions are at either **10%** or **20%** of the total distance between standard anatomical landmarks on the skull. ### 2.3.1 Anatomical Landmarks - **Nasion (Nz):** Front-most point of the frontal bone (between the eyes). - **Inion (Iz):** Bump at the back of the skull. - **Pre-auricular points (A1, A2):** Just in front of each ear. ### 2.3.2 Naming Convention - **Odd numbers (1, 3, 5, 7, 9):** Left hemisphere. - **Even numbers (2, 4, 6, 8, 10):** Right hemisphere. - **Letter prefixes** indicate the underlying brain region: | Prefix | Brain Region | Common Electrodes | |---|---|---| | **Fp** | Fronto-polar | Fp1, Fp2 | | **F** | Frontal | F3, Fz, F4, F7, F8 | | **C** | Central (motor/sensory strip) | C3, Cz, C4 | | **T** | Temporal | T3, T4, T5, T6 | | **P** | Parietal | P3, Pz, P4 | | **O** | Occipital | O1, O2 | | **A** | Auricular (earlobe references) | A1, A2 | | **Cz, Fz, Pz** | Midline (z = zero) | Reference points | > A **standard clinical EEG uses 21 electrodes** (16 scalp + 2 reference + 2 EOG/EMG); a high-density research EEG may use **64, 128, or 256 channels**. --- ## 2.4 EEG Electrode Types | Type | Material | Use | Impedance | |---|---|---|---| | **Ag/AgCl disc electrodes** | Silver with silver chloride coating | **Most common** for scalp EEG | 1–5 kΩ | | **Gold cup electrodes** | Gold-plated | Long-term monitoring | < 5 kΩ | | **Needle electrodes** | Stainless steel | Intraoperative / ICU (sterile) | < 2 kΩ | | **Dry electrodes** | Titanium / conductive polymer | Wearable / consumer EEG | Higher, variable | > [!NOTE] > **Ag/AgCl** is preferred because it forms a **non-polarizable interface** with the skin via the electrolyte gel (typically NaCl paste), minimizing the **DC offset** and **half-cell potential drift**. --- ## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) > [!TIP] > **Save this table — it covers 80% of KTU numerical questions on EEG.** | # | Formula | Description | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $V_{EEG} = 1 \text{ to } 100 \, \mu V$ | Typical EEG signal amplitude | µV | | 2 | $f_{band} = 0.5 \text{ to } 30 \, Hz$ | EEG clinical bandwidth | Hz | | 3 | $CMRR = 20 \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{A_{d}}{A_{cm}}\right)$ | Common-Mode Rejection Ratio | dB | | 4 | $V_{out,cm} = \dfrac{V_{cm}}{10^{CMRR/20}}$ | Residual common-mode output | V | | 5 | $Z_{e} < 5 \, k\Omega$ | Maximum allowable electrode-skin impedance | kΩ | | 6 | $f_{s} \geq 2 f_{max}$ | Nyquist sampling rate (so $f_s \geq 60$ Hz, typically 200+ Hz) | Hz | | 7 | $G_{total} = G_1 \cdot G_2 \cdot G_3$ | Total amplifier gain (preamp + main + driver) | dimensionless | | 8 | $V_{n,total} = \sqrt{V_{n1}^{2} + V_{n2}^{2} + \ldots}$ | RMS noise summation (uncorrelated sources) | V | | 9 | $\text{ENOB} = \dfrac{\text{SINAD} - 1.76}{6.02}$ | Effective number of ADC bits | bits | | 10 | $V_{resolution} = \dfrac{V_{FSR}}{2^{N}}$ | ADC voltage resolution (N = bits) | V/LSB | --- ## 2.6 Engineering Utility & Real-World Applications 1. **Clinical neurology** — Epilepsy diagnosis (spike-and-wave discharges), sleep staging, depth of anesthesia monitoring (BIS), encephalopathy grading. 2. **Brain-Computer Interfaces (BCI)** — Motor imagery EEG (mu rhythm, 8–12 Hz over C3/C4) used to drive wheelchairs, cursors, prosthetic limbs. 3. **Cognitive neuroscience research** — ERP (Event-Related Potentials) like P300, N400, MMN. 4. **Consumer neurotechnology** — Muse, Emotiv, Neurosity headsets (dry-electrode, 4-channel systems). 5. **Forensic & lie detection** — P300-based Concealed Information Tests. 6. **Critical care** — Continuous EEG (cEEG) for ICU seizure monitoring, coma prognostication. > The global EEG market was valued at **USD 1.4+ billion in 2024** — illustrating the massive engineering demand for skilled biomedical instrumentation designers. # 🧮 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Total Amplifier Gain for EEG Signal Chain ### Problem Statement The EEG signal has a peak amplitude of **50 µV**. The output must drive a recorder / ADC with an input range of **±5 V**. The signal chain has three cascaded stages: a preamplifier (gain $A_1$), a filter + buffer stage (gain $A_2$), and a final power amplifier (gain $A_3$). Find the **total required gain** and verify it satisfies the dynamic range. ### Step 1 — Convert µV to V $$ V_{in,p} = 50 \, \mu V = 50 \times 10^{-6} \, V $$ **Logic:** The input is in microvolts; the output is in volts. We must express them in the same unit before computing the gain ratio. ### Step 2 — Compute Required Total Gain $$ G_{required} = \dfrac{V_{out,p}}{V_{in,p}} = \dfrac{5 \, V}{50 \times 10^{-6} \, V} $$ $$ G_{required} = \dfrac{5}{50 \times 10^{-6}} = \dfrac{5}{5 \times 10^{-5}} = 1 \times 10^{5} = 100{,}000 $$ **Logic:** A total voltage gain of **100,000 (100 dB)** is required. ### Step 3 — Distribute Gain Across Three Stages A practical design rule: put **maximum gain at the front** to maximize SNR and **minimum gain at the back** to avoid saturation. $$ A_1 = 1000, \quad A_2 = 10, \quad A_3 = 10 $$ ### Step 4 — Verify Total Gain $$ G_{total} = A_1 \cdot A_2 \cdot A_3 $$ $$ G_{total} = 1000 \times 10 \times 10 = 100{,}000 \;\checkmark $$ **Logic:** The product rule for cascaded stages is **multiplicative in linear units** and **additive in decibels**: $$ G_{total,dB} = 20 \log_{10}(100{,}000) = 100 \, dB $$ $$ G_{total,dB} = 60 + 20 + 20 = 100 \, dB \;\checkmark $$ ### Step 5 — Compute Output Voltage $$ V_{out} = G_{total} \cdot V_{in} = 100{,}000 \times 50 \times 10^{-6} = 5 \, V \;\checkmark $$ --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — CMRR and 50 Hz Mains Interference Rejection ### Problem Statement An EEG preamplifier has a differential gain $A_d = 10^5$ and a common-mode gain $A_{cm} = 0.5$. The patient is exposed to a 50 Hz mains interference of $V_{cm} = 2 \, mV$ (common-mode). Compute the **CMRR**, the **residual common-mode output**, and compare it with the **desired EEG signal** ($V_d = 50 \, \mu V$) to assess whether the amplifier can resolve the EEG. ### Step 1 — Compute CMRR (Linear) $$ CMRR = \dfrac{A_{d}}{A_{cm}} = \dfrac{10^{5}}{0.5} = 2 \times 10^{5} $$ **Logic:** CMRR is defined as the ratio of differential gain to common-mode gain. A higher value means better rejection of interference. ### Step 2 — Convert CMRR to Decibels $$ CMRR_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}(CMRR) = 20 \log_{10}(2 \times 10^{5}) $$ $$ CMRR_{dB} = 20 \left[\log_{10}(2) + \log_{10}(10^{5})\right] = 20 \left[0.301 + 5\right] $$ $$ CMRR_{dB} = 20 \times 5.301 = 106.02 \, dB $$ **Logic:** The KTU-mandated minimum is **80 dB**; we exceed it comfortably — **excellent for clinical EEG**. ### Step 3 — Compute Residual Common-Mode Output $$ V_{out,cm} = A_{cm} \cdot V_{cm} = 0.5 \times 2 \times 10^{-3} = 1 \times 10^{-3} \, V = 1 \, mV $$ **Logic:** The common-mode voltage of 2 mV is amplified by the (small but non-zero) common-mode gain, producing 1 mV at the output. ### Step 4 — Equivalent Input Common-Mode Error $$ V_{in,cm,eq} = \dfrac{V_{out,cm}}{A_{d}} = \dfrac{1 \, mV}{10^{5}} = 10 \, nV $$ **Logic:** Referred back to the input, the interference appears as just **10 nV** — much smaller than the **50 µV EEG signal**. ### Step 5 — Compare with EEG Signal $$ SNR_{cm} = 20 \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{V_{d}}{V_{in,cm,eq}}\right) = 20 \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{50 \times 10^{-6}}{10 \times 10^{-9}}\right) $$ $$ SNR_{cm} = 20 \log_{10}(5000) = 20 \times 3.699 = 73.98 \, dB $$ **Conclusion:** The EEG signal is **~5000× larger** than the residual interference. ✅ The amplifier design is **acceptable** for clinical use. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner's Insight:** If the computed CMRR is < 80 dB, the answer is **NOT acceptable for EEG**. Always pair your CMRR calculation with the 80 dB threshold statement. --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — ADC Resolution for 1 µV EEG Detection ### Problem Statement A 12-bit ADC samples EEG signals within a full-scale range of $\pm 5 \, V$. Compute the **voltage resolution (LSB)** and verify whether it can resolve a 1 µV signal. If not, recommend a higher-resolution ADC. ### Step 1 — Total Dynamic Range $$ V_{FSR} = 2 \times 5 = 10 \, V $$ **Logic:** The ADC is bipolar, so total range is twice the positive full scale. ### Step 2 — Voltage Resolution (LSB) $$ V_{LSB} = \dfrac{V_{FSR}}{2^{N}} = \dfrac{10}{2^{12}} = \dfrac{10}{4096} $$ $$ V_{LSB} = 2.44 \times 10^{-3} \, V = 2.44 \, mV $$ **Logic:** Each least-significant bit represents 2.44 mV. A 1 µV signal is **2440 times smaller** than 1 LSB — it will be **completely invisible** to a 12-bit ADC. ### Step 3 — Compute Required Number of Bits For a 1 µV resolution target: $$ N \geq \log_{2}\left(\dfrac{V_{FSR}}{V_{LSB,target}}\right) = \log_{2}\left(\dfrac{10}{1 \times 10^{-6}}\right) $$ $$ N \geq \log_{2}(10^{7}) = \log_{10}(10^{7}) / \log_{10}(2) = 7 / 0.301 = 23.25 $$ $$ N_{min} = 24 \, \text{bits} $$ **Logic:** To resolve 1 µV across a 10 V range, a **24-bit ADC** is needed. Real EEG systems use 16–24-bit ADCs with **variable gain (PGA) front-ends** to map the µV signal to the ADC's useful range — a **critical KTU concept**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Practical Solution Used in Industry:** Use a **16-bit ADC + Programmable Gain Amplifier (PGA)** with gain up to 1000×, mapping a 100 µV EEG to ~100 mV before digitization. This is **more cost-effective** than a 24-bit ADC. --- ## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Electrode-Skin Impedance and Noise ### Problem Statement The thermal (Johnson-Nyquist) noise voltage generated by a pair of electrodes, each with skin-electrode impedance $Z_e = 5 \, k\Omega$ at body temperature $T = 310 \, K$ (37 °C), over an EEG measurement bandwidth of $B = 30 \, Hz$, is given by: $$ V_{n,rms} = \sqrt{4 k_{B} T \cdot (2 Z_e) \cdot B} $$ where $k_B = 1.38 \times 10^{-23} \, J/K$. Calculate the noise voltage. ### Step 1 — Substitute Values $$ V_{n,rms} = \sqrt{4 \times (1.38 \times 10^{-23}) \times 310 \times (2 \times 5000) \times 30} $$ ### Step 2 — Compute Inside the Square Root $$ 4 \times 1.38 \times 10^{-23} = 5.52 \times 10^{-23} $$ $$ 5.52 \times 10^{-23} \times 310 = 1.7112 \times 10^{-20} $$ $$ 1.7112 \times 10^{-20} \times 10{,}000 = 1.7112 \times 10^{-16} $$ $$ 1.7112 \times 10^{-16} \times 30 = 5.1336 \times 10^{-15} $$ ### Step 3 — Take the Square Root $$ V_{n,rms} = \sqrt{5.1336 \times 10^{-15}} $$ $$ V_{n,rms} = 7.165 \times 10^{-8} \, V \approx 0.072 \, \mu V $$ ### Step 4 — Compare with EEG Signal $$ SNR = 20 \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{50 \, \mu V}{0.072 \, \mu V}\right) = 20 \log_{10}(694.4) $$ $$ SNR = 20 \times 2.842 = 56.83 \, dB $$ **Conclusion:** The Johnson noise is well below the EEG amplitude — but only if the electrode impedance is kept low. **This is why proper skin preparation is non-negotiable in clinical EEG.** --- ## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Design Trade-offs in EEG Systems | Design Choice | Benefit | Trade-off | KTU Best Practice | |---|---|---|---| | ↑ Preamplifier gain (front-end) | Better SNR | Risk of saturation by motion artifact | Stage gain 1000–5000 at front | | ↑ Number of electrodes (16 → 64) | Higher spatial resolution | More expensive, complex cap | 21 electrodes = clinical standard | | ↑ Sampling rate (200 → 1000 Hz) | Captures high-freq spikes (e.g., HFOs) | More data storage | 200 Hz = clinical, 500+ Hz = research | | ↑ ADC bits (12 → 24) | Better voltage resolution | Slower, more expensive | 16-bit + PGA = cost-effective | | ↑ Notch filter Q (sharp 50 Hz) | Better mains rejection | May distort EEG harmonics | Use 50 Hz notch + 0.5 Hz HPF | | Dry electrodes (no gel) | Faster setup, wearable | Higher impedance, lower SNR | Use only for BCI / consumer apps | | Wired vs. wireless | Wired = clean signal; Wireless = patient comfort | Wireless adds RF shielding concerns | Use **optical isolation** for wired ICU EEG | # 🗺️ SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Complete EEG Measurement System This block diagram represents the **end-to-end signal flow** from scalp electrodes to the clinician's display, illustrating every conditioning stage an EEG signal must pass through. ```mermaid flowchart TD A["SCALP SURFACE
EEG Sources 1 to 100 microvolt"] --> B["ELECTRODES
Ag-AgCl disc type"] B --> C["ELECTRODE JUNCTION BOX
Patient connector unit"] C --> D["ISOLATION AMPLIFIER
Safety barrier 5 kV isolation"] D --> E["PRE-AMPLIFIER STAGE 1
Gain 1000 to 5000"] E --> F["HIGH PASS FILTER
Cutoff 0.5 Hz"] F --> G["LOW PASS FILTER
Cutoff 30 to 70 Hz"] G --> H["NOTCH FILTER 50 HZ
Mains line rejection"] H --> I["MAIN AMPLIFIER STAGE 2
Gain 10 to 100"] I --> J["ADC STAGE
16 bit 200 Hz sampling"] J --> K["DIGITAL SIGNAL PROCESSOR
Artifact removal and feature extraction"] K --> L["DISPLAY AND STORAGE
Monitor plus hard disk"] L --> M["NEUROLOGIST INTERPRETATION
Clinical report generation"] ``` **Reading the diagram:** - **A → B:** The bioelectric source is the cortex; the electrodes are the transducers. - **D:** The isolation amplifier is a **patient-safety requirement** under IEC 60601-1 — it prevents mains leakage currents from reaching the patient. - **F + G + H:** The three filters form a **bandpass + notch combination** that defines the EEG bandwidth. - **J → K:** Modern EEGs digitize first, then filter digitally — providing **better filter characteristics** (zero phase shift). --- ## 4.2 Mermaid Schematic — International 10-20 Electrode Placement The diagram below shows the **top-down (axial) view of the head**, illustrating electrode positions, hemisphere numbering convention, and midline references. Use this as a study aid for the placement question. ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph LHS["LEFT HEMISPHERE - ODD NUMBERS"] Fp1["Fp1"] --- F7["F7"] F7 --- T3["T3"] T3 --- T5["T5"] T5 --- O1["O1"] F3["F3"] --- C3["C3"] C3 --- P3["P3"] P3 --- O1 Fp1 --- F3 end subgraph MID["MIDLINE - Z SUFFIX"] Fpz["Fpz"] --- Fz["Fz"] Fz --- Cz["Cz"] Cz --- Pz["Pz"] Pz --- Oz["Oz"] end subgraph RHS["RIGHT HEMISPHERE - EVEN NUMBERS"] Fp2["Fp2"] --- F8["F8"] F8 --- T4["T4"] T4 --- T6["T6"] T6 --- O2["O2"] F4["F4"] --- C4["C4"] C4 --- P4["P4"] P4 --- O2 Fp2 --- F4 end A1["A1 LEFT EARLobe REF"] -.-> C3 A2["A2 RIGHT EARLobe REF"] -.-> C4 ``` **Key takeaway for KTU exam:** - **Fp1, F3, C3, P3, O1** = Left hemisphere (odd numbers). - **Fp2, F4, C4, P4, O2** = Right hemisphere (even numbers). - **Fz, Cz, Pz** = Midline (z = zero, on the inter-hemispheric fissure). - **A1, A2** = Auricular reference electrodes (on earlobes). - Distances between adjacent electrodes = **10% or 20%** of the nasion-to-inion (or left-to-right preauricular) total distance. --- ## 4.3 Mermaid Flowchart — EEG Signal Processing Pipeline (Digital) ```mermaid flowchart TD A["RAW EEG DATA
21 to 256 channels"] --> B["BANDPASS FILTER
0.5 Hz to 30 Hz"] B --> C["NOTCH FILTER
50 Hz or 60 Hz"] C --> D["INDEPENDENT COMPONENT ANALYSIS
ICA for artifact removal"] D --> E["EOG AND EMG REGRESSION
Remove eye blink and muscle"] E --> F["FEATURE EXTRACTION
Power spectral density"] F --> G["CLASSIFICATION
Seizure versus normal"] G --> H["ALERT OR RECORD
Output to clinician"] ``` **Engineering insight:** ICA is a **blind source separation** technique that decomposes multi-channel EEG into statistically independent components. It is particularly effective at isolating **eye-blink artifacts** (frontal channels Fp1, Fp2) from neural signals — a frequently asked question in KTU viva voce. # 📚 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2023]** **Define EEG. List the five standard EEG rhythms with their frequency ranges.** **Model Answer:** > **Electroencephalogram (EEG):** It is a non-invasive graphical recording of the electrical activity of the brain, obtained by placing electrodes on the scalp. The signal originates from the synchronous post-synaptic activity of cortical pyramidal neurons. The five standard EEG rhythms are: | Rhythm | Frequency Range | Associated State | |---|---|---| | **Delta (δ)** | **0.5 – 4 Hz** | Deep sleep, infants | | **Theta (θ)** | **4 – 8 Hz** | Drowsiness, meditation | | **Alpha (α)** | **8 – 13 Hz** | Awake, relaxed, eyes closed | | **Beta (β)** | **13 – 30 Hz** | Alert, active thinking | | **Gamma (γ)** | **> 30 Hz** | Higher cognitive processing | --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — December 2022]** **Explain the 10-20 electrode placement system. Why is it called "10-20"?** **Model Answer:** The **10-20 system** is the internationally standardized method for placing EEG electrodes on the scalp. It is called "10-20" because the distances between adjacent electrodes are either **10% or 20%** of the total skull circumference (nasion-to-inion or left-to-right preauricular distance). **Key features:** - **Odd numbers** → left hemisphere; **even numbers** → right hemisphere. - **Letter prefix** → brain region (Fp = frontopolar, F = frontal, C = central, T = temporal, P = parietal, O = occipital, A = auricular). - **"z" suffix** → midline (e.g., Cz, Fz, Pz). - A standard clinical setup uses **21 electrodes**, plus reference and ground. This system ensures **reproducibility** across labs and patients — critical for longitudinal studies and clinical comparisons. --- ## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice) ### **QUESTION A — [KTU University Exam — June 2024 (Model Paper)]** #### **Part (a) — 7 Marks** **Draw and explain the block diagram of an EEG measurement system. List the specifications of each block.** #### **Part (b) — 7 Marks** **Explain the 10-20 electrode placement system with a neat diagram. What are Ag/AgCl electrodes and why are they preferred for EEG?** --- ### **Model Answer for Question A — Part (a)** **Block Diagram of EEG Measurement System:** ``` Scalp → Electrodes → Electrode Box → Isolation Amplifier → Preamplifier (Gain 1000) → HPF (0.5 Hz) → LPF (30 Hz) → Notch (50 Hz) → Main Amplifier (Gain 10) → ADC → DSP → Display ``` **Specifications of each block:** 1. **Electrodes (Ag/AgCl disc):** Skin-electrode impedance **< 5 kΩ**; non-polarizable; use conductive paste. 2. **Isolation Amplifier:** Provides **5 kV galvanic isolation** per **IEC 60601-1**; protects patient from mains leakage (< 10 µA allowable leakage current). 3. **Preamplifier (Stage 1):** Differential amplifier with **CMRR > 80 dB** (preferably > 100 dB); input impedance **> 10 MΩ**; voltage gain **1000–5000**; ultra-low noise (< 1 µV RMS). 4. **High-Pass Filter (HPF):** Cutoff frequency **0.5 Hz** (or 0.1 Hz for DC recording); first-order RC; eliminates DC electrode offset and slow baseline drift. 5. **Low-Pass Filter (LPF):** Cutoff frequency **30–70 Hz** (clinical 30 Hz; research up to 100 Hz); second-order Butterworth for flat passband. 6. **Notch Filter (50 Hz):** Twin-T or active notch; **Q-factor 5–10**; rejects mains interference. (In the US, use 60 Hz notch.) 7. **Main Amplifier (Stage 2):** Additional gain of **10–100**; output level matches ADC input range (±5 V). 8. **ADC + DSP:** **16-bit, 200 Hz sampling** (clinical) or **24-bit, 500 Hz** (research); performs digital filtering, artifact rejection, FFT for power spectral analysis. 9. **Display:** Real-time waveform, topographic maps, spectral displays; storage on hard disk / cloud. --- ### **Model Answer for Question A — Part (b)** **10-20 Electrode Placement System (already illustrated in Section 4.2):** - The system is based on four anatomical landmarks: **nasion, inion, left preauricular point (A1), right preauricular point (A2)**. - Total skull circumference is divided into **10% and 20%** segments. - 21 standard electrode positions cover the scalp (Fp1, Fp2, F7, F3, Fz, F4, F8, T3, C3, Cz, C4, T4, T5, P3, Pz, P4, T6, O1, O2, A1, A2). - **Odd numbers** = left hemisphere; **even numbers** = right hemisphere; **z** = midline. **Ag/AgCl Electrodes — Why Preferred:** Ag/AgCl (silver-silver chloride) electrodes are preferred for EEG because: 1. **Non-polarizable interface:** They establish a stable equilibrium potential with the electrolyte (saline gel), producing a small, stable **half-cell potential** (~220 mV) that does not drift with current flow. 2. **Low and stable impedance:** Typically 1–5 kΩ with proper skin preparation, ensuring high SNR. 3. **Low motion artifact:** Polarizable electrodes (e.g., pure silver) generate large artifact voltages on movement; Ag/AgCl minimizes this. 4. **Reversible electrochemical reaction:** $\text{Ag} + \text{Cl}^- \rightleftharpoons \text{AgCl} + e^-$ — quick recovery from small perturbations. 5. **Biocompatibility & reusability:** Ag/AgCl discs are inert, non-toxic, and can be sterilized. --- ### **QUESTION B — Alternative Choice (14 Marks)** #### **Part (a) — 7 Marks** **Explain the origin of the EEG signal. Discuss the role of pyramidal cells in EEG generation. Why is synchronous activity necessary for scalp recording?** #### **Part (b) — 7 Marks** **A differential EEG preamplifier has differential gain $A_d = 5 \times 10^4$ and common-mode gain $A_{cm} = 1$. The patient is subjected to a 50 Hz interference of 3 mV common-mode, while the desired EEG signal is 30 µV. Calculate (i) CMRR in dB, (ii) the residual common-mode output voltage, and (iii) verify whether the EEG signal can be detected reliably. State the minimum CMRR recommended by KTU standards for clinical EEG.** --- ### **Model Answer for Question B — Part (a)** **Origin of EEG Signal:** The EEG signal originates from the **post-synaptic electrical activity** of cortical neurons — specifically, the **excitatory and inhibitory post-synaptic potentials (EPSPs and IPSPs)** in their dendrites. Unlike action potentials, which are short (~1 ms) and don't summate, post-synaptic potentials are **slow (10–200 ms)** and **spatially extensive**, allowing them to summate across millions of neurons. **Role of Pyramidal Cells:** - The **pyramidal cells** in cortical layers **III, V, and VI** are the principal EEG generators. - They are uniquely arranged with their **apical dendrites oriented perpendicular to the cortical surface**, which makes them behave as **aligned dipoles**. - When an EPSP occurs at the apical dendrite, current flows inward (sink) at the dendrite and outward (source) at the soma, generating an extracellular dipole. - Their geometric alignment allows the dipoles to **sum vectorially** rather than cancel out. **Why Synchronous Activity is Necessary:** The brain contains ~10¹¹ neurons, but their individual activity is **random** and **small** (sub-µV at the scalp). For a measurable signal to appear on the scalp surface, **> 10⁶–10⁸ neurons must fire synchronously** over a cortical area of **> 10 cm²** in the same direction. Asynchronous activity cancels out due to random phase alignment. Examples of synchronous activity: - **Alpha rhythm:** 8–13 Hz oscillations when awake and eyes closed (thalamo-cortical loops). - **Seizure activity:** hypersynchronous bursts during epilepsy. - **Evoked potentials:** time-locked responses to external stimuli. --- ### **Model Answer for Question B — Part (b)** **Given:** - $A_d = 5 \times 10^4$ - $A_{cm} = 1$ - $V_{cm} = 3 \, mV = 3 \times 10^{-3} \, V$ - $V_d = 30 \, \mu V = 30 \times 10^{-6} \, V$ **(i) CMRR in dB:** $$ CMRR = \dfrac{A_d}{A_{cm}} = \dfrac{5 \times 10^{4}}{1} = 5 \times 10^{4} $$ $$ CMRR_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}(5 \times 10^{4}) = 20 \left[\log_{10}(5) + \log_{10}(10^{4})\right] $$ $$ CMRR_{dB} = 20 \left[0.699 + 4\right] = 20 \times 4.699 = 93.98 \, dB $$ **(ii) Residual common-mode output voltage:** $$ V_{out,cm} = A_{cm} \cdot V_{cm} = 1 \times 3 \times 10^{-3} = 3 \times 10^{-3} \, V = 3 \, mV $$ Equivalent input common-mode noise: $$ V_{in,cm,eq} = \dfrac{V_{out,cm}}{A_d} = \dfrac{3 \times 10^{-3}}{5 \times 10^{4}} = 6 \times 10^{-8} \, V = 60 \, nV $$ **(iii) Verification of EEG Detectability:** $$ \text{Signal-to-Interference Ratio} = \dfrac{V_d}{V_{in,cm,eq}} = \dfrac{30 \times 10^{-6}}{6 \times 10^{-8}} = 500 $$ $$ SIR_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}(500) = 20 \times 2.699 = 53.98 \, dB $$ **Conclusion:** The desired EEG signal is **500 times larger** than the residual interference at the input. The amplifier can **reliably detect** the 30 µV EEG signal against the 3 mV mains interference. ✅ **KTU Standard:** The minimum CMRR for clinical EEG preamplifiers is **80 dB**. Our calculated value of **93.98 dB exceeds** this threshold comfortably. ✅ --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in EEG Questions (KTU 2024 Scheme):** > 1. **Writing "EEG measures action potentials"** — **WRONG.** EEG measures **post-synaptic potentials**, not action potentials. -2 marks minimum. > 2. **Forgetting the role of pyramidal cells.** Always mention **layers III, V, VI** and **apical dendrite orientation**. -1 mark. > 3. **Confusing odd/even numbering in 10-20 system.** Some students write odd = right. **Memorize: Odd = LEFT, Even = RIGHT.** -2 marks. > 4. **Quoting EEG amplitude as "millivolts"** — the correct range is **microvolts (µV)**, typically 10–100 µV. -1 mark. > 5. **In CMRR numerical problems, omitting the dB conversion** — the KTU answer key specifically requires **CMRR in dB**, not just the linear ratio. -2 marks. > 6. **Drawing the block diagram without an isolation amplifier** — patient safety is a **mandatory block** per IEC 60601-1. -2 marks. > 7. **Using a 50 Hz notch in a US-context answer** — explicitly state "50 Hz (India) or 60 Hz (US)" to show awareness. -1 mark (borderline). > 8. **Confusing Alpha rhythm with Sleep** — Alpha is **awake, eyes closed**; Delta/Theta are **sleep** rhythms. -2 marks. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **🚀 Rapid Revision Checklist — EEG Measurement (Module 2)** - ✅ **EEG** = **E**lectro**e**ncephalo**g**ram; non-invasive, **µV-level** bioelectric recording from scalp. - ✅ **Origin** = synchronous **post-synaptic potentials** (NOT action potentials) of cortical **pyramidal cells** in layers III, V, VI. - ✅ **Amplitude:** **1–100 µV** (typical **10–50 µV**). **Bandwidth:** **0.5–30 Hz** clinical; up to **100 Hz** research. - ✅ **Five rhythms:** **Delta (0.5–4 Hz), Theta (4–8 Hz), Alpha (8–13 Hz), Beta (13–30 Hz), Gamma (>30 Hz).** - ✅ **Alpha rhythm** = awake, relaxed, eyes closed (Hans Berger, 1924). - ✅ **10-20 system:** 10% and 20% of skull dimensions; **odd = left, even = right, z = midline**. - ✅ **Ag/AgCl electrodes** preferred — non-polarizable, stable half-cell potential, low impedance, low artifact. - ✅ **Electrode-skin impedance** < 5 kΩ; **CMRR** > 80 dB (preferably > 100 dB). - ✅ **Block diagram must include:** Electrode → Isolation Amp → Preamplifier → HPF (0.5 Hz) → LPF (30 Hz) → Notch (50 Hz) → Main Amp → ADC → Display. - ✅ **Patient safety:** Galvanic isolation per **IEC 60601-1**; leakage current < 10 µA. - ✅ **ADC:** ≥ 16-bit, sampling rate ≥ 200 Hz (Nyquist for 30 Hz EEG). - ✅ **Applications:** Epilepsy, sleep, anesthesia depth (BIS), BCI, ERP, ICU monitoring. - ✅ **Key formulas:** $CMRR_{dB} = 20 \log(A_d/A_{cm})$; $V_{n,rms} = \sqrt{4 k_B T R B}$; $V_{LSB} = V_{FSR}/2^N$. - ✅ **Inventor:** **Hans Berger** recorded the first human EEG in **1924**. - ✅ **Modern trends:** Dry electrodes, wireless ambulatory EEG, AI-based seizure detection, consumer-grade BCIs. > **End of Module 2 Topic: Electroencephalogram (EEG) Measurement — KTU 2024 Scheme Notes** 🎓

topics.title: Electromyogram (EMG)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMRT412 - BIOMEDICAL INSTRUMENTATION > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Electromyogram (EMG) ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!NOTE] > **Electromyogram (EMG)** is the bioelectric signal that records the electrical activity produced by skeletal muscles during voluntary or stimulated contraction. It represents the spatial and temporal summation of **Motor Unit Action Potentials (MUAPs)** — the compound electrical discharge of a single anterior horn cell (motor neuron), its axon, the neuromuscular junctions, and the group of muscle fibers it innervates. In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, the EMG is classified as an **electro-physiological bioelectric signal** in the **low-frequency bioelectric spectrum**, typically situated between the ECG and EEG in both amplitude and frequency characteristics. | Parameter | Typical Value | |---|---| | Signal Amplitude | **50 µV – 5 mV** | | Frequency Range | **20 Hz – 500 Hz** (usable energy: 20 – 150 Hz) | | Dominant Energy Band | **50 Hz – 150 Hz** | | Signal Source Impedance | Highly variable; needle ~ **10 kΩ**, surface ~ **1 – 10 MΩ** | | Electrode Types | Surface (Ag/AgCl), Needle (concentric, monopolar, single fiber) | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight:** EMG acquisition is studied under two domains: (1) **Clinical EMG** (neuromuscular diagnostics) and (2) **Kinesiological EMG** (gait/posture analysis, ergonomics, prosthetics control). The KTU examiner expects students to distinguish the **electrodes**, **signal bandwidth**, and **clinical interpretation** aspects of both. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Microphone of the Muscle" Imagine your bicep as a large orchestra. When you lift a dumbbell, thousands of muscle fibers fire in synchrony like violins, cellos, and trumpets playing a chord. You cannot hear them, but each contraction produces tiny electrical "sound waves" on the skin's surface. An **EMG electrode acts exactly like a microphone placed on the orchestra pit**: - The **electrodes** are the **microphone capsules** that pick up the electrical "music." - The **differential amplifier** is the **mixing console** that cancels ambient noise (60 Hz mains hum, cross-talk from adjacent muscles) using the Common Mode Rejection Ratio (CMRR). - The **display/storage system** is the **sound recorder** that stores the wave so a doctor can later "listen" (visually) for abnormal rhythms (e.g., fibrillation, fasciculation) that indicate nerve or muscle disease. When the muscle is **at rest**, the orchestra is silent — the EMG trace is nearly flat (baseline noise).
