## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper]**
*Define the term "Linear Attenuation Coefficient" ($\mu$) in the context of X-ray imaging. List any three factors on which $\mu$ depends.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The **linear attenuation coefficient** ($\mu$) is defined as the fraction of X-ray photons removed per unit thickness of the absorbing material. Its SI unit is **cm⁻¹** and it is mathematically expressed in the Beer-Lambert Law as $I = I_0 e^{-\mu x}$.

**Three factors on which $\mu$ depends:**
1. **Atomic number (Z)** of the absorbing material — $\mu \propto Z^3$ in the photoelectric range.
2. **Physical density ($\rho$)** of the material — denser tissues attenuate more.
3. **Photon energy (E)** — at higher energies, $\mu$ decreases.

*(Award 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for listing the three factors with brief explanation.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Supplementary]**
*What is a Hounsfield Unit (HU)? Why is water assigned a value of 0 HU in CT imaging?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The **Hounsfield Unit (HU)** or **CT Number** is a quantitative scale used in Computed Tomography to express the linear attenuation coefficient of a tissue relative to that of water.

**Formula:**
$$\text{HU} = 1000 \cdot \frac{\mu_{\text{tissue}} - \mu_{\text{water}}}{\mu_{\text{water}}}$$

**Why water = 0 HU:** Water is the most abundant component of the human body (~60%) and its attenuation coefficient is very close to that of soft tissue. Setting water as the reference (with $K = 1000$) makes the scale symmetric and intuitive:
- Air = **−1000 HU** (no attenuation)
- Water = **0 HU** (reference)
- Bone = **+1000 HU** (high attenuation)

This scale allows radiologists to identify tissues by absolute quantitative values rather than subjective gray levels.

*(Award 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for formula, 1 mark for water = 0 HU justification.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — July 2024, Module 3]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **principle and operation of a diagnostic X-ray radiography system** for producing a still picture. Discuss the role of each block in detail. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **Beer-Lambert Law of Attenuation** from first principles. A monoenergetic X-ray beam of intensity 1000 photons/cm²·s passes through 5 cm of muscle tissue ($\mu = 0.2$ cm⁻¹) followed by 2 cm of bone ($\mu = 0.8$ cm⁻¹). Calculate the transmitted intensity. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

The diagnostic X-ray radiography system produces a still picture by recording the spatial pattern of X-ray photons transmitted through the patient. The block diagram is:

**Block 1: High Voltage Generator (1 Mark)**
Provides a stable, controllable DC potential of **30–150 kVp** to the X-ray tube. The kVp determines the maximum photon energy ($E_{\text{max}} = eV$ in keV), and thus the penetrating power.

**Block 2: X-Ray Tube (2 Marks)**
Contains a **tungsten cathode** (heated to ~2400 K for thermionic emission) and a **tungsten-rhodium anode** mounted on a rotating disc. Electrons emitted by the cathode are accelerated toward the anode. Upon sudden deceleration, they emit:
- **Bremsstrahlung** ("braking radiation") — continuous spectrum
- **Characteristic radiation** — discrete peaks (Kα, Kβ lines of tungsten)

**Block 3: Filtration Assembly (0.5 Mark)**
Composed of an inherent filter (1 mm Al) and added filter (2.5 mm Al + 0.1 mm Cu). Removes low-energy photons that would only increase skin dose without contributing to image formation (beam hardening).

**Block 4: Collimator (0.5 Mark)**
A set of adjustable lead shutters restricts the beam to the region of interest, reducing patient dose and minimizing scatter.

**Block 5: Patient Attenuation (1 Mark)**
The X-ray beam passes through the body. Attenuation occurs by **photoelectric absorption** (Z³/E³) and **Compton scattering** (Z/E). Bone ($\mu$ high) attenuates more than muscle ($\mu$ lower) than fat ($\mu$ lowest) than air ($\mu$ ≈ 0).

**Block 6: Image Detector (1 Mark)**
- *Film-Screen:* Phosphor screen (e.g., Gd₂O₂S:Tb) converts X-rays to visible light, exposing silver halide film.
- *Digital Radiography (DR):* Flat-panel detector with CsI scintillator coupled to amorphous silicon TFT photodiode array.
- *Computed Radiography (CR):* Photostimulable phosphor (PSP) plate read by a laser scanner.

**Block 7: Image Processing and Display (1 Mark)**
The digital image undergoes preprocessing (flat-field correction, dead pixel correction), post-processing (window/level, edge enhancement), and is displayed on a calibrated DICOM-compliant monitor. The image is archived in **PACS** (Picture Archiving and Communication System).

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Derivation (4 Marks):**

**Step 1:** Consider a thin slab of tissue of thickness $dx$. The number of photons removed is proportional to both the incident number $N$ and the thickness:

$$dN = -\mu N dx$$

**Step 2:** Rearranging and integrating:

$$\int_{N_0}^{N} \frac{dN'}{N'} = -\mu \int_0^x dx'$$

**Step 3:** Evaluating:

$$\ln(N) - \ln(N_0) = -\mu x$$

**Step 4:** Exponentiating:

$$N = N_0 e^{-\mu x} \quad \Rightarrow \quad I = I_0 e^{-\mu x}$$

**Numerical Problem (3 Marks):**

**Given:**
- $I_0 = 1000$ photons/cm²·s
- Muscle: $x_1 = 5$ cm, $\mu_1 = 0.2$ cm⁻¹
- Bone: $x_2 = 2$ cm, $\mu_2 = 0.8$ cm⁻¹

**Step 1 — Apply Beer-Lambert for muscle layer:**

$$I_1 = I_0 \cdot e^{-\mu_1 x_1} = 1000 \cdot e^{-(0.2)(5)}$$

$$I_1 = 1000 \cdot e^{-1} = 1000 \cdot 0.3679 = 367.9 \text{ photons/cm}^2\text{/s}$$

**Step 2 — Apply Beer-Lambert for bone layer (using $I_1$ as new incident intensity):**

$$I_2 = I_1 \cdot e^{-\mu_2 x_2} = 367.9 \cdot e^{-(0.8)(2)}$$

$$I_2 = 367.9 \cdot e^{-1.6} = 367.9 \cdot 0.2019 = 74.27 \text{ photons/cm}^2\text{/s}$$

**Step 3 — Single combined formula:**

$$I = I_0 \cdot e^{-(\mu_1 x_1 + \mu_2 x_2)} = 1000 \cdot e^{-(1.0 + 1.6)} = 1000 \cdot e^{-2.6}$$

$$I = 1000 \cdot 0.0743 = 74.3 \text{ photons/cm}^2\text{/s}$$

**Final Answer:** $\boxed{I_{\text{transmitted}} \approx 74.3 \text{ photons/cm}^2\text{/s}}$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Students frequently make these mistakes in the derivation:
> 1. **Missing the negative sign** in $dN = -\mu N dx$ (loses 1 mark).
> 2. **Forgetting to square or properly integrate** the limits (loses 1 mark).
> 3. **Numerical error:** For the two-layer problem, you MUST apply the equation **sequentially** (muscle first → bone) or use the **sum of $\mu x$** in the exponent. A common error is to multiply the exponents incorrectly.
> 4. **Units:** Always include units in the final answer. Bare numbers without "photons/cm²·s" cost marks.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]**

**(a)** Explain the **principle of Computed Tomography (CT)**. With a neat block diagram, describe the **third-generation CT scanner architecture**. How does CT differ from a plain radiograph in terms of image formation? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Define **Half-Value Layer (HVL)**. An X-ray beam has an HVL of 2.5 mm in aluminum. Calculate the thickness of aluminum required to reduce the beam intensity to 1% of its original value. If the same beam is passed through 10 mm of tissue ($\mu = 0.15$ mm⁻¹), what is the percentage transmission? **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Principle of CT (2 Marks):**
Computed Tomography produces a **cross-sectional (tomographic) still picture** of the body by:
1. Rotating an X-ray source and detector around the patient.
2. Acquiring attenuation measurements from **hundreds of angular projections** (typically 1000+ per slice).
3. Reconstructing the 2D slice using **filtered back-projection (FBP)** or **iterative reconstruction** algorithms.
4. Each pixel in the reconstructed image is assigned a **Hounsfield Unit (HU)** value representing the average linear attenuation coefficient within that voxel.

**Third-Generation CT Architecture (3 Marks):**
- The X-ray tube and a **curved detector array** (800–1500 elements) are mounted on a common frame that **rotates continuously** around the patient (1–3 rotations per second).
- The X-ray beam is **fan-shaped** (10°–60° fan angle), covering the entire body width in a single rotation.
- The patient couch translates through the gantry (helical/spiral acquisition) or remains stationary (axial acquisition).
- The detector output is digitized and fed to a high-speed reconstruction computer.

**Key Difference from Plain Radiograph (2 Marks):**

| Feature | Plain Radiograph | CT Scan |
|---|---|---|
| **Dimension** | 2D projection (superimposed) | Cross-sectional slice |
| **Overlap** | All structures overlap | No superimposition |
| **Contrast Resolution** | Limited (~5–10% difference) | Excellent (~0.5% difference) |
| **Quantitative** | No (qualitative gray) | Yes (Hounsfield Units) |
| **Acquisition** | Single exposure | Multiple angular projections |
| **Reconstruction** | Not required | FBP / Iterative algorithm |
| **Dose** | Low | Higher (5–20 mGy) |

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Definition of HVL (1 Mark):**
The **Half-Value Layer (HVL)** is the thickness of a specified material that reduces the intensity of an X-ray beam to **one-half (50%)** of its original value. It is the standard descriptor of beam quality/hardness.

**Mathematical relation:**

$$\text{HVL} = \frac{0.693}{\mu}$$

**Part 1: Thickness for 1% transmission (3 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Set up the attenuation equation with $I / I_0 = 0.01$.**

$$\frac{I}{I_0} = 0.01 = e^{-\mu x}$$

**Step 2 — Take natural log of both sides.**

$$\ln(0.01) = -\mu x$$

$$-4.6052 = -\mu x$$

**Step 3 — Express $\mu$ in terms of HVL.**

From the HVL definition, $\mu = 0.693 / \text{HVL} = 0.693 / 2.5 = 0.2772$ mm⁻¹.

**Step 4 — Solve for $x$.**

$$x = \frac{4.6052}{0.2772} = 16.61 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 5 — Alternative method using HVL directly.**

If $n$ half-value layers reduce the intensity to $(1/2)^n$ of the original, then:

$$\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^n = 0.01$$

Taking log:

$$-n \cdot \ln(2) = \ln(0.01) = -4.6052$$

$$n = \frac{4.6052}{0.6931} = 6.644$$

$$x = n \cdot \text{HVL} = 6.644 \cdot 2.5 = 16.61 \text{ mm}$$

**Final Answer:** $\boxed{x \approx 16.61 \text{ mm of aluminum}}$

**Part 2: Percentage transmission through 10 mm tissue (3 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Apply Beer-Lambert with $\mu = 0.15$ mm⁻¹ and $x = 10$ mm.**

$$\frac{I}{I_0} = e^{-\mu x} = e^{-(0.15)(10)} = e^{-1.5}$$

**Step 2 — Evaluate.**

$$\frac{I}{I_0} = e^{-1.5} = 0.2231$$

**Step 3 — Convert to percentage.**

$$\text{Percentage Transmission} = 22.31\%$$

**Final Answer:** $\boxed{22.31\% \text{ of the original intensity is transmitted.}}$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning — Part B (b):**
> 1. **Forgetting to take natural log** when solving exponential equations — students often try to "divide by $e$" incorrectly.
> 2. **Unit mismatch:** $\mu$ must be in **inverse mm** if $x$ is in **mm**. Always verify units first.
> 3. **Confusing $I/I_0$ with $I_0/I$:** If you get a number > 1, you have inverted the ratio.
> 4. **For the HVL problem:** The "$n$-th HVL" shortcut $(1/2)^n$ is faster than the $\ln/\mu$ route, but students often miscompute $n$. Show both methods to maximize partial marks.
> 5. **Definition marks:** Always write the *definition* of HVL (1 mark) **before** starting the calculation. Examiners are strict about this.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 3: Diagnostic Still Picture**

- **Beer-Lambert Law:** $I = I_0 e^{-\mu x}$ — the **single most important equation** in the entire module. Master it.

- **HVL Formula:** $\text{HVL} = 0.693 / \mu$ — and the shortcut $(1/2)^n$ for $n$ half-value layers.

- **Hounsfield Unit:** $\text{HU} = 1000 \cdot (\mu_{\text{tissue}} - \mu_{\text{water}}) / \mu_{\text{water}}$; remember water = 0, air = −1000, bone = +1000.

- **Photoelectric effect** dominates at **low energies** and is **Z³ dependent** — this is the primary mechanism of bone-soft tissue contrast.

- **Compton scattering** dominates at **higher energies**, contributes to image noise (scatter), and is the reason **antiscatter grids** are used.

- **Diagnostic X-ray range:** **30–150 keV** (mammography uses **20–35 keV** for high soft-tissue contrast).

- **Five components of the imaging chain:** Source → Filtration/Collimation → Patient → Detector → Display/PACS.

- **CT vs. Radiograph:** CT gives a *cross-sectional* (tomographic) image, while plain radiography gives a *2D projection* with superimposition.

- **Dose-Quality Trade-off:** To **double SNR**, you need **4× the dose** (because $\text{SNR} = \sqrt{N}$).

- **Geometric blur formula:** $\text{Blur} = f \cdot (M - 1)$, where $M = \text{SID}/\text{SOD}$.

- **Bremsstrahlung + Characteristic radiation** are the two mechanisms of X-ray production in a tube.

- **Effective atomic numbers to remember:** Bone ≈ **13.8**, Muscle ≈ **7.4**, Fat ≈ **6.0**, Air ≈ **7.6**, Water = **7.4**.

- **Image matrix and bit depth:** Standard digital radiograph is **2048×2048** with **12–16 bit** depth (4096 to 65,536 gray levels).

- **Spatial resolution unit:** **Line pairs per millimeter (lp/mm)** — radiography achieves 2.5–5 lp/mm; mammography achieves 8–20 lp/mm.

- **PACS** = Picture Archiving and Communication System; **DICOM** = Digital Imaging and Communications in Medicine standard.

- **Three generations of CT:** First (translate-rotate), Second (translate-rotate with fan beam), Third (rotate-rotate with fan beam) — **third generation is the modern standard**.

- **ALARA Principle:** "As Low As Reasonably Achievable" — govern all patient dose considerations in KTU answers.

- **Always include units** in your final numerical answers — this is a frequent deduction in KTU valuation.

- **Always show the derivation steps explicitly** — even if you know the final answer, the steps carry the marks.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fluoroscopy
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT412 - BIOMEDICAL INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Fluoroscopy

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fluoroscopy** is a real-time X-ray imaging modality in which a continuous or pulsed X-ray beam, generated by an X-ray tube, passes through the patient and is intercepted by an **Image Intensifier (II)** tube or a **Flat-Panel Detector (FPD)**. The transmitted X-ray intensity pattern is converted into a visible, electronically amplified image that is displayed on a monitor at video frame rates (typically **25–30 frames per second**), thereby enabling dynamic visualization of internal anatomical structures, physiological motion, and the progress of interventional procedures.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus, fluoroscopy is treated under *Module 3: Diagnostic Imaging — X-Ray Based Systems*, which emphasizes the **Image Intensifier tube**, the **C-arm geometry**, and **Automatic Brightness Control (ABC)** as the three essential pillars of the topic.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are standing behind a frosted glass screen, and on the other side someone is holding a powerful flashlight pointing at you. You cannot see the bulb, but you can see the *shadow* of a hand moving in front of the light. If the hand moves continuously, the shadow changes continuously — you are watching motion in real time.

> **Fluoroscopy is essentially "shadow-graphy in motion."**

- The **flashlight** = the X-ray tube (an invisible, ionizing light source).
- The **frosted glass** = the **Image Intensifier**, which catches the *weakened X-ray shadow* and converts it into a *bright visible-light image* on its output phosphor.
- The **observer** = a **video camera + monitor** that records and displays the moving image.

Without the image intensifier, the X-ray pattern hitting a normal fluorescent screen would be far too dim to view in a lit room. The image intensifier acts as an **"amplifying night-vision scope for X-rays,"** boosting brightness by a factor of **5,000 to 20,000 times** so that a clinician can see the image comfortably.

## 1.3 Key Standard Metrics (Bolded)

| Parameter | Typical Value |
|---|---|
| X-ray tube voltage (kVp) | **50 – 120 kVp** |
| Tube current | **0.5 – 5 mA** (much lower than radiography) |
| Input phosphor diameter | **15 – 40 cm (6" – 16")** |
| Output phosphor diameter | **2.0 – 3.5 cm** |
| Minification gain | **15 – 100** |
| Total brightness gain | **5,000 – 20,000** |
| Frame rate | **25 / 30 fps** (interlaced) or **pulsed 4 – 8 fps** |
| Spatial resolution | **1.0 – 2.5 line pairs/mm** |
| Patient dose rate | **0.01 – 0.05 Gy/min** at the entrance |

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 Module 3):** The student is expected to explain the construction and working of the **Image Intensifier tube**, derive the **brightness gain equation**, and describe the **C-arm assembly** with its associated **Automatic Brightness Control (ABC)** feedback loop.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principle — Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown

Fluoroscopic imaging relies on the same physical principle as plain radiography (differential attenuation of X-rays by tissues), but it differs in **how the image is captured, amplified, and displayed continuously**. The operational chain consists of six sequential stages:

1. **X-ray Generation** — The X-ray tube emits a *low-intensity, continuous (or pulsed)* beam. Fluoroscopy uses **mA values 10–100 times lower** than radiography because the image is being viewed in real time and the cumulative exposure must be kept within ALARA limits.

2. **Patient Attenuation** — The beam is differentially absorbed by tissues, governed by the Beer–Lambert law:
$$I = I_0 \cdot e^{-\mu x}$$
where $I$ is the transmitted intensity, $I_0$ is the incident intensity, $\mu$ is the linear attenuation coefficient, and $x$ is the tissue thickness. The 2-D spatial distribution of $I$ forms the *X-ray shadow image*.

3. **X-ray → Light Conversion (Input Phosphor)** — The transmitted X-ray pattern strikes the **input phosphor**, typically a layer of **Cesium Iodide (CsI) doped with Thallium**, deposited directly on the photocathode. CsI is preferred because it can be grown as *needle-like crystals* that channel the light with minimal lateral scatter, preserving spatial resolution.

4. **Light → Electron Conversion (Photocathode)** — The visible-light photons from the CsI strike a **photocathode** (e.g., Sb–Cs or multi-alkali S-20), which emits **photoelectrons** in direct proportion to the local light intensity. Each X-ray photon absorbed in CsI yields ~1,000–2,000 visible photons, and each visible photon yields ~0.1–0.2 photoelectrons — establishing the *first stage of amplification*.

5. **Electron Acceleration & Focusing (Electron Optics)** — The photoelectrons are accelerated by a high potential difference (**25 – 35 kV**) between the photocathode and the output phosphor, and focused by **electrostatic lenses** onto a small output phosphor. The acceleration multiplies the energy of each electron, and the geometric demagnification concentrates their energy onto a smaller area — this is the *second and largest stage of amplification*.

6. **Electron → Light Conversion (Output Phosphor)** — The high-energy electrons strike the **output phosphor** (typically P-20, ZnCdS:Ag, emitting at **550 nm** — yellow-green), producing a bright, minified visible-light image. A **video camera (CCD/CMOS)** or a **direct digital FPD** then captures this image and routes it to a display.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why two amplifying stages?** Because the dose rate in fluoroscopy is deliberately kept very low (to protect the patient and clinician), the raw X-ray signal at the input is extremely weak. The cascaded amplification (light → electrons → high-energy electrons → bright light) is what makes the image *visible at all* under typical room illumination.

## 2.2 The Image Intensifier (II) Tube — Construction Details

The Image Intensifier is the heart of a modern fluoroscopic system. Its cross-section, from input to output, contains:

- **Input Window** — Thin aluminum or titanium (low X-ray attenuation).
- **Input Phosphor (CsI:Tl)** — Curved (to match the photocathode curvature) and approximately **0.1 – 0.2 mm thick**.
- **Photocathode** — A thin Sb-Cs layer bonded to the inside of the input window.
- **Electrostatic Focusing Electrodes** — Set at progressively increasing potentials.
- **Anode** — A small aperture near the output phosphor.
- **Output Phosphor (P-20, ZnCdS:Ag)** — Deposited on a fiber-optic output window.
- **Output Window** — Fiber-optic plate for direct optical coupling to the camera.

## 2.3 The "Why" Behind the Curved Input

The input phosphor is **slightly curved (spherical)** rather than flat. This curvature is *not* cosmetic — it ensures that all photoelectrons emitted from the photocathode travel **equal optical path lengths** to the output phosphor. Without this correction, electrons from the periphery would travel longer and more curved paths, producing image distortion and non-uniform resolution across the field. The electron optics are designed to invert the image electronically so that the output appears anatomically correct.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All symbols and boundary conditions below are **frequently tested** in KTU End Semester Examinations. Memorize the units and the geometric reasoning.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Description / Boundary Conditions |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Linear attenuation (Beer–Lambert)** | $I = I_0 \cdot e^{-\mu x}$ | $I_0$ = incident intensity, $\mu$ in $\text{cm}^{-1}$, $x$ in cm |
| 2 | **Minification Gain** $G_m$ | $G_m = \left(\dfrac{D_{in}}{D_{out}}\right)^{2}$ | $D_{in}$, $D_{out}$ = input and output phosphor diameters. Unitless. Typical: 15 – 100 |
| 3 | **Flux Gain** $G_f$ | $G_f = \dfrac{\text{No. of light photons at output}}{\text{No. of X-ray photons at input}}$ | Typical: 50 – 100. Energy ratio of accelerated electrons |
| 4 | **Total Brightness Gain** $G_B$ | $G_B = G_m \times G_f$ | Typical: 5,000 – 20,000. Unitless ratio |
| 5 | **Conversion Factor (CF)** | $CF = \dfrac{B \times A}{\dot{X}}$ | $B$ = brightness (cd/m²), $A$ = output area, $\dot{X}$ = input dose rate. Typical: 100 – 300 $\text{cd}\cdot\text{m}^{-2}\cdot\text{mGy}^{-1}\cdot\text{s}$ |
| 6 | **Veiling Glare (VGR)** | $VGR = \dfrac{S}{S+L}$ | $S$ = scatter + glare luminance, $L$ = primary image luminance. Lower is better; typical < 0.1 |
| 7 | **Contrast Ratio** | $CR = \dfrac{L_{max}}{L_{min}}$ | Typical modern II: 30:1 – 100:1 |
| 8 | **Quantum Detection Efficiency (QDE)** | $QDE = \eta_X \cdot \eta_C \cdot \eta_E$ | Product of X-ray absorption, conversion, and electron-optical efficiencies |
| 9 | **ABC Loop Equation** | $V_{out} = K_p \cdot (B_{ref} - B_{actual})$ | $K_p$ = proportional gain, drives kVp/mA adjustments |
| 10 | **Magnification Mode Factor** | $M = \dfrac{D_{in, eff}}{D_{in, full}}$ | e.g., 6" mode from 9" full field gives $M = 6/9 = 0.67$ |
| 11 | **Patient Entrance Dose Rate** | $\dot{D}_{ent} = \dfrac{\text{ESD}}{\text{Examination time}}$ | Expressed in mGy/min; KTU limit typically 25 mGy/min for normal mode |
| 12 | **Dose-Area Product (DAP)** | $DAP = \dot{D}_{ent} \times A_{field}$ | Units: Gy·cm²; cumulative exposure indicator |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Fluoroscopy is the *workhorse imaging modality* of:

- **Interventional Cardiology** — Cardiac catheterization, angioplasty, stent placement, pacemaker lead insertion.
- **Gastroenterology** — Barium swallow studies, ERCP (Endoscopic Retrograde Cholangiopancreatography).
- **Orthopedic Surgery** — Real-time guidance of fracture reduction, joint replacement, and spinal hardware placement.
- **Vascular Surgery** — Angiography and embolization procedures.
- **Pain Management** — Image-guided nerve blocks and epidural injections.

> The **C-arm** is the dominant mechanical architecture: an X-ray source on one end and the image intensifier on the other, both mounted on a *C-shaped gantry* that can be rotated freely around the patient, providing anteroposterior, lateral, and oblique views without moving the patient.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Minification Gain of the Image Intensifier

The image intensifier demagnifies the input image from a *large* input phosphor to a *small* output phosphor. Because the *same number of light photons* are concentrated onto a smaller area, the **brightness** (luminance per unit area) increases as the *inverse ratio of the areas*.

**Step 1:** Write the geometric areas of input and output phosphors.

$$A_{in} = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot D_{in}^{2} \qquad A_{out} = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot D_{out}^{2}$$

**Step 2:** Assume the number of light photons is conserved (no photons are added or destroyed in the geometric transfer; only their spatial density changes).

$$N_{photons, in} \approx N_{photons, out} = N$$

**Step 3:** Express luminance (brightness) as photons per unit area.

$$L_{in} = \frac{N}{A_{in}} = \frac{4N}{\pi D_{in}^{2}} \qquad L_{out} = \frac{N}{A_{out}} = \frac{4N}{\pi D_{out}^{2}}$$

**Step 4:** Form the ratio.

$$G_m \;=\; \frac{L_{out}}{L_{in}} \;=\; \frac{A_{in}}{A_{out}} \;=\; \frac{\dfrac{\pi}{4} D_{in}^{2}}{\dfrac{\pi}{4} D_{out}^{2}} \;=\; \left(\frac{D_{in}}{D_{out}}\right)^{2}$$

**Final Result:**

$$\boxed{\,G_m \;=\; \left(\frac{D_{in}}{D_{out}}\right)^{2}\,}$$

**Numerical Example (KTU-style):**
For a 9-inch input phosphor and a 1-inch output phosphor:

$$G_m = \left(\frac{9}{1}\right)^{2} = 81$$

This means the output image is **81 times brighter** than it would be if the output phosphor were the same size as the input — purely from geometric concentration of light.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Total Brightness Gain

The total brightness gain is the product of two independent amplification mechanisms:

**Step 1:** Define the *flux gain* $G_f$ as the energy ratio of an accelerated output electron to an input photoelectron (typically 50–100).

$$G_f = \frac{\text{Energy gained by electron in 25–35 kV field}}{\text{Initial photoelectron energy}} \approx 50 - 100$$

**Step 2:** Define the minification gain $G_m$ as derived above.

$$G_m = \left(\frac{D_{in}}{D_{out}}\right)^{2}$$

**Step 3:** The two gains are independent (one is geometric, the other is energetic), so they multiply.

$$G_B = G_m \times G_f$$

**Final Result:**

$$\boxed{\,G_B \;=\; \left(\frac{D_{in}}{D_{out}}\right)^{2} \cdot G_f\,}$$

**Numerical Example:**
A 9"-input, 1"-output II with $G_f = 75$ yields:

$$G_B = 81 \times 75 = 6{,}075 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{Total brightness gain} \approx 6{,}075$$

This is the figure KTU examiners love — a system that delivers a *6,000-fold* luminance amplification from a low-dose X-ray input.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Conversion Factor (Image Quality Metric)

The **Conversion Factor (CF)** is the *figure of merit* for an image intensifier — it expresses how efficiently the device converts an *input X-ray dose rate* into a *visible output brightness*.

**Step 1:** Let $\dot{X}_{in}$ be the entrance dose rate to the II input screen (in mGy/s), $A_{out}$ the output phosphor area (in m²), and $L_{out}$ the output luminance (in cd/m²).

**Step 2:** The conversion factor is defined as:

$$CF = \frac{L_{out} \cdot A_{out}}{\dot{X}_{in}}$$

**Step 3:** Since $L_{out} \cdot A_{out}$ has units of $\text{cd} \cdot \text{m}^{2}\cdot\text{m}^{-2} = \text{cd}$, and $\dot{X}_{in}$ has units of mGy/s, the conversion factor has units of:

$$[CF] = \frac{\text{cd} \cdot \text{s}}{\text{mGy}}$$

**Final Result:**

$$\boxed{\,CF \;=\; \frac{L_{out} \cdot A_{out}}{\dot{X}_{in}} \quad [\text{cd}\cdot\text{s}\cdot\text{mGy}^{-1}]\,}$$

**Typical value:** 100 – 300 $\text{cd}\cdot\text{m}^{-2}\cdot\text{s}\cdot\text{mGy}^{-1}$ for a modern image intensifier.

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Veiling Glare Ratio

Veiling glare is the unwanted spread of light from bright regions into dark regions, caused by *electron backscatter* inside the II tube and *optical scattering* in the output phosphor.

**Step 1:** Define $L$ as the *primary image luminance* (signal) and $S$ as the *stray luminance* from scattering and glare.

**Step 2:** Define the Veiling Glare Ratio:

$$VGR = \frac{S}{S + L}$$

**Step 3:** For a perfectly glare-free system, $S = 0$ and $VGR = 0$. For a heavily degraded system, $L \to 0$ and $VGR \to 1$.

**Final Result:**

$$\boxed{\,VGR \;=\; \frac{S}{S + L}\,}$$

A *lower* VGR indicates a *better* image intensifier. Modern CsI-based systems achieve VGR < 0.1.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Image Intensifier Parameters vs. Image Quality

| Parameter | Effect on Image | KTU Tip |
|---|---|---|
| Larger $D_{in}$ | Wider field of view, lower spatial resolution | "9-inch mode" has lower resolution than "6-inch mode" |
| Smaller $D_{out}$ | Higher minification gain, higher brightness | Trade-off: higher veiling glare |
| Higher kVp on photocathode | Higher flux gain, higher brightness | Increases veiling glare and reduces contrast |
| Thicker CsI input | Higher X-ray absorption, higher QDE | Reduces input phosphor resolution if too thick |
| Pulsed (vs. continuous) X-ray | Lower patient dose, sharper frames | "Last-image-hold" feature uses pulsed mode |
| Higher mA | Higher brightness, more patient dose | Compensated by ABC feedback |
| Higher kVp | Greater beam penetration, less contrast | Used in ABC for thick patients |

## 3.6 Worked Numerical Problem (KTU Examination Style)

**Problem:** A fluoroscopic image intensifier has an input phosphor diameter of **23 cm** and an output phosphor diameter of **2.5 cm**. The flux gain of the tube is **60**. Calculate (a) the minification gain, (b) the total brightness gain, and (c) the effective amplification of an input X-ray exposure of **1 µGy/s** to an output luminance in **cd/m²**, given that the output phosphor area is $\frac{\pi}{4}(2.5)^2 \approx 4.91\;\text{cm}^{2}$.

**Solution:**

**Part (a) — Minification Gain:**

$$G_m = \left(\frac{23}{2.5}\right)^{2} = (9.2)^{2} = 84.64 \approx 84.6$$

**Part (b) — Total Brightness Gain:**

$$G_B = G_m \times G_f = 84.64 \times 60 = 5{,}078.4 \approx 5{,}078$$

**Part (c) — Output Luminance:**
Using $CF = 200\;\text{cd}\cdot\text{m}^{-2}\cdot\text{s}\cdot\text{mGy}^{-1}$ as a typical conversion factor:

$$L_{out} = \frac{CF \cdot \dot{X}_{in}}{A_{out}} = \frac{200 \times 1 \times 10^{-3}}{4.91 \times 10^{-4}} \approx 407.3\;\text{cd/m}^{2}$$

> This luminance is comfortably viewable in a dimly lit fluoroscopy suite (typical monitor brightness: 200–500 cd/m²).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Complete Fluoroscopic Imaging Chain

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[HV Generator 50 to 120 kVp] --> B[X-Ray Tube]
    B --> C[Collimator and Filtration]
    C --> D[Patient Attenuation]
    D --> E[Input Phosphor CsI Tl]
    E --> F[Photocathode Sb Cs]
    F --> G[Electrostatic Focusing and Acceleration 25 to 35 kV]
    G --> H[Output Phosphor P20 ZnCdS]
    H --> I[Optical Coupling]
    I --> J[Video Camera CCD or CMOS]
    J --> K[Image Processor DSP]
    K --> L[Display Monitor]
    K --> M[Automatic Brightness Control ABC Feedback]
    M --> A
    K --> N[Last Image Hold LIH Memory]
    K --> O[Digital Recording PACS]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Cross-Section of the Image Intensifier Tube

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P1[Input Window Al or Ti Low Attenuation] --> P2[Input Phosphor CsI Tl Curved]
    P2 --> P3[Photocathode Sb Cs Multi Alkali]
    P3 --> P4[Focusing Electrode 1 Intermediate Potential]
    P4 --> P5[Focusing Electrode 2 Higher Potential]
    P5 --> P6[Anode Aperture near Output]
    P6 --> P7[Output Phosphor P20 ZnCdS Ag 550 nm]
    P7 --> P8[Output Window Fiber Optic Plate]
    subgraph Vacuum_Envelope
        P2
        P3
        P4
        P5
        P6
        P7
    end
```

## 4.3 Mermaid C-Arm Geometry and Operational Modes

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    CA[Patient on Table] --> CB[X-Ray Tube on C Arm Lower End]
    CA --> CC[Image Intensifier on C Arm Upper End]
    CC --> CD[Camera and Display Chain]
    CD --> CE[Continuous Mode 30 fps]
    CD --> CF[Pulsed Mode 4 to 8 fps Lower Dose]
    CD --> CG[Magnification Modes 9 inch 6 inch 4.5 inch]
    CD --> CH[Last Image Hold LIH]
    CD --> CI[Road Mapping for Vascular Procedures]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Automatic Brightness Control (ABC) Feedback Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Reference Brightness Setpoint B ref] --> A3[Summing Junction]
    A2[Actual Output Brightness from II B act] --> A3
    A3 --> A4[Error Amplifier Kp]
    A4 --> A5[Control Logic]
    A5 --> A6{Adjust kVp or mA or Both}
    A6 --> A7[HV Generator]
    A7 --> A8[X-Ray Tube]

    A9 --> A10[Image Intensifier]
    A10 --> A2
```

```mermaid

    M0[Fluoroscopy Operating Modes] --> M1[Continuous Fluoroscopy]
    M0 --> M2[Pulsed Fluoroscopy]

```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Mermaid Block Diagrams instead of physical schematics?**
> KTU 2024 module evaluation does not require artistic line-drawings. A *block-level functional architecture* clearly demonstrates the engineering signal flow and the control loops that examiners reward. The five diagrams above cover (1) the complete imaging chain, (2) the II tube cross-section, (3) C-arm geometry, (4) ABC feedback, and (5) the operating-mode taxonomy — together forming a complete *system-level* picture.

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Define fluoroscopy. List its main clinical applications.*

**Model Answer:**
Fluoroscopy is a real-time X-ray imaging technique in which a continuous or pulsed X-ray beam is passed through the patient and the attenuated radiation is converted into a visible, electronically amplified image using an image intensifier tube or flat-panel detector, displayed on a monitor at video frame rates (typically 25–30 fps).

**Main clinical applications:**
1. Cardiac catheterization and interventional cardiology (angioplasty, stent placement).
2. Gastrointestinal studies (barium swallow, ERCP).
3. Orthopedic surgery guidance (fracture reduction, joint replacement).
4. Vascular angiography and embolization.
5. Pain management (image-guided nerve blocks).

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*What is the role of the photocathode in an image intensifier?*

**Model Answer:**
The photocathode, typically made of a multi-alkali (Sb-Cs) compound, is bonded to the input phosphor and serves the **light-to-electron conversion** function. When visible-light photons from the CsI input phosphor strike the photocathode, they liberate **photoelectrons** via the photoelectric effect. The number of photoelectrons emitted at each point is proportional to the local X-ray intensity, thereby preserving the spatial image information. These photoelectrons are then accelerated and focused by the electrostatic lens system onto the output phosphor, providing the **first stage of signal amplification** in the II tube.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Note:** As per KTU 2024 ESE regulation, every Part B question in a module carries an **internal choice**. Two completely independent alternatives are provided below.

### Question A (14 Marks) — Image Intensifier Tube

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the construction and working of an **image intensifier tube** used in a fluoroscopic system. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Derive the expressions for **minification gain** and **total brightness gain** of an image intensifier. A 9-inch input phosphor and 1-inch output phosphor image intensifier has a flux gain of 80. Calculate the total brightness gain. *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Construction and Working:**

The image intensifier tube is an evacuated glass envelope containing the following components in sequence (input to output):

1. **Input Window** — A thin (1–2 mm) curved aluminum or titanium window that admits X-rays with minimal attenuation.

2. **Input Phosphor (CsI:Tl)** — A curved layer of cesium iodide doped with thallium, approximately 0.1–0.2 mm thick, deposited directly onto the photocathode. CsI is preferred because it can be grown as *needle-like crystals* that act as light pipes, minimizing lateral scatter and preserving spatial resolution. It has high X-ray absorption efficiency and emits in the blue-green spectrum (~550 nm), well matched to the photocathode response.

3. **Photocathode** — A thin multi-alkali (Sb-Cs) layer that converts each visible photon into a photoelectron. Quantum efficiency is ~10–20%.

4. **Electrostatic Focusing Electrodes** — A series of cylindrical electrodes held at progressively increasing positive potentials (from ~ −300 V at the photocathode to +25 to +35 kV at the anode). These electrodes (a) accelerate the photoelectrons to high energy and (b) focus them onto the small output phosphor.

5. **Anode** — A small aperture electrode near the output phosphor through which the accelerated electrons pass.

6. **Output Phosphor (P-20, ZnCdS:Ag)** — A thin (~5–10 µm) layer of zinc cadmium sulfide activated with silver, emitting at 550 nm (yellow-green, well matched to the human eye's photopic sensitivity and to CCD/CMOS camera response). Because the electron energy is very high (~25–35 keV) and the area is small, the luminance produced is very high.

7. **Output Window** — A fiber-optic plate that couples the output image directly to the video camera (or, in older systems, to a lens-and-mirror optical distributor).

**Working principle:** X-rays transmitted through the patient strike the input phosphor → emit visible light → photocathode emits photoelectrons → electrostatic lenses accelerate and focus them → output phosphor converts the high-energy electrons back into a bright, minified, inverted (but electronically re-inverted) visible image → camera captures the image → monitor displays it in real time.

> **Image Inversion:** Because the electrostatic lens inverts the image, a final image inversion (electronic or optical) is required to display it anatomically correctly.

---

**(b) Derivation of Gains:**

**Minification Gain (Gm):**

Let $D_{in}$ and $D_{out}$ be the input and output phosphor diameters. Their areas are:

$$A_{in} = \frac{\pi}{4} D_{in}^{2}, \qquad A_{out} = \frac{\pi}{4} D_{out}^{2}$$

Assuming the same number of light photons $N$ is concentrated from $A_{in}$ to $A_{out}$:

$$G_m = \frac{L_{out}}{L_{in}} = \frac{N/A_{out}}{N/A_{in}} = \frac{A_{in}}{A_{out}} = \left(\frac{D_{in}}{D_{out}}\right)^{2}$$

**Total Brightness Gain (GB):**

$$G_B = G_m \times G_f = \left(\frac{D_{in}}{D_{out}}\right)^{2} \times G_f$$

**Numerical Calculation:**

$$G_m = \left(\frac{9}{1}\right)^{2} = 81$$

$$G_B = 81 \times 80 = \boxed{6{,}480}$$

The II provides a brightness amplification of approximately **6,480 times**, making the image visible at low dose rates.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — C-Arm and Automatic Brightness Control

**[KTU University Exam — May 2023]**

**(a)** Describe the **C-arm fluoroscopic system** with a block diagram. Explain its clinical advantages over conventional radiographic imaging. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Explain the **Automatic Brightness Control (ABC)** system used in fluoroscopy with a block diagram. How does it maintain constant image brightness when the patient thickness changes? *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) C-Arm Fluoroscopic System:**

The **C-arm** is a *C-shaped mechanical gantry* on which an **X-ray tube** is mounted at one end and an **image intensifier (II)** (with a TV camera and monitor chain) at the other end. The "C" allows the source and detector to be rotated freely around the patient's body, providing **multi-angle real-time imaging** without moving the patient.

**Block Diagram:**

```
X-Ray Tube → Collimator → Patient → Image Intensifier → Optical Coupling → TV Camera → Image Processor → Monitor
                                                                                       ↓
                                                                              Automatic Brightness Control (ABC) feedback
                                                                                       ↓
                                                                              High-Voltage Generator
                                                                                       ↑
                                                                              (kVp, mA adjustments)
```

**Major Sub-Systems:**

1. **X-ray Generator** — Typically a 3.5–100 kW high-frequency inverter providing 40–120 kVp at 0.5–5 mA.
2. **X-ray Tube** — High heat-capacity rotating-anode tube (1–3 MHU).
3. **Collimator and Filtration** — Adjusts the X-ray field to the region of interest.
4. **Image Intensifier** — Converts the X-ray shadow to a bright visible image.
5. **TV Camera and Display Chain** — CCD/CMOS camera, image processor, and one or more monitors.
6. **Mechanical C-arm** — Motorized or manually pivoted on multiple axes (angulation, rotation, orbital movement, translation).
7. **Last-Image-Hold (LIH)** — Freezes the last frame for study without additional radiation.
8. **Digital Recording and Road Mapping** — Stores and overlays images for vascular interventions.

**Clinical Advantages Over Radiography:**

| Feature | C-Arm Fluoroscopy | Conventional Radiography |
|---|---|---|
| Real-time imaging | ✅ Yes (25–30 fps) | ❌ Single static image |
| Multi-angle views | ✅ Free rotation of C-arm | ❌ Patient must be repositioned |
| Interventional guidance | ✅ Ideal (catheters, screws) | ❌ Not suitable |
| Image storage | ✅ Digital (PACS) | Film or digital |
| Patient dose per procedure | Higher (longer exposure) | Lower (single short exposure) |

---

**(b) Automatic Brightness Control (ABC):**

**Principle:** In fluoroscopy, the image brightness must remain *constant* for varying patient thickness and density. ABC is a **closed-loop negative-feedback system** that monitors the output luminance of the II and automatically adjusts **kVp, mA, or both** to compensate.

**Block Diagram (closed-loop):**

```
[Reference Brightness B_ref]
        ↓
       (+)  ← (Comparator)  ← [Actual Brightness B_act from II]
        ↓
   [Error e = B_ref − B_act]
        ↓
   [Error Amplifier / Proportional Controller Kp]
        ↓
   [Control Logic]  →  [HV Generator]  →  [X-Ray Tube]  →  [Patient]  →  [Image Intensifier]  →  [Camera]  →  [Monitor]
                                                                                                                                                                                                              ↓
                                                                                                                                                                                                       B_act (feedback)
```

**How it maintains constant brightness when patient thickness increases:**

1. Patient thickness increases → more X-ray attenuation → fewer X-rays reach the II input.
2. II output luminance $B_{act}$ drops below the reference $B_{ref}$.
3. The comparator produces a *negative error* $(B_{ref} - B_{act}) > 0$.
4. The control logic increases **kVp** (to harden the beam and increase penetration) and/or **mA** (to increase X-ray flux).
5. Increased penetration restores $B_{act}$ to $B_{ref}$, and the loop stabilizes.
6. Conversely, for a thin patient, kVp/mA are *reduced* to avoid overexposure.

> **Modern ABC strategies:**
> - *kV-only control* (preferred in pediatric fluoroscopy — minimizes mA variation).
> - *mA-only control* (keeps beam quality constant).
> - *Combined kV + mA* (fastest response, used in vascular and cardiac work).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse ABC with AEC** (Automatic Exposure Control used in *radiography*). AEC terminates the exposure after a set dose; ABC *continuously modulates* parameters in real time.
> 2. Many students write that ABC varies **only mA** — this is incomplete. Modern systems can vary **kVp, mA, or both**, and you must mention at least two of these for full marks.
> 3. The II does *not* amplify brightness; the **flux gain × minification gain** does. Avoid vague phrases like "the II makes the image bright."
> 4. Always state the **typical brightness gain value** (5,000 – 20,000) — without a number, your answer is incomplete.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Fluoroscopy (Module 3)**

- **Fluoroscopy** = real-time X-ray imaging (25–30 fps), uses **low mA** (0.5–5 mA) to minimize patient dose.
- **Image Intensifier (II)** is the core component, providing 5,000–20,000-fold brightness amplification.
- **II Construction order** (input → output): Input Window → **CsI:Tl** Input Phosphor → **Sb-Cs** Photocathode → **Electrostatic Focusing Electrodes** → Anode → **P-20 (ZnCdS:Ag)** Output Phosphor → Fiber-Optic Output Window.
- **CsI:Tl** input phosphor is preferred because its needle-like crystals minimize lateral light scatter and preserve resolution.
- **P-20** output phosphor emits at **550 nm** (yellow-green), well matched to human photopic vision and CCD/CMOS response.
- **Minification Gain**: $G_m = (D_{in}/D_{out})^{2}$ — purely geometric.
- **Total Brightness Gain**: $G_B = G_m \times G_f$ — geometric × energetic (flux) gain.
- **Flux Gain** is set by the photocathode-to-anode accelerating voltage (typically 25–35 kV), typical value 50–100.
- **Conversion Factor (CF)** = (Output Luminance × Output Area) / (Input Dose Rate), typical 100–300 $\text{cd}\cdot\text{m}^{-2}\cdot\text{s}\cdot\text{mGy}^{-1}$.
- **Veiling Glare Ratio (VGR)** = $S/(S+L)$ — lower is better, modern II < 0.1.
- **C-arm** is the standard mechanical assembly for fluoroscopy; allows multi-angle rotation around the patient.
- **ABC (Automatic Brightness Control)** is a *closed-loop negative feedback* system that adjusts kVp, mA, or both to maintain constant output luminance.
- **Pulsed fluoroscopy** (4–8 fps) significantly reduces patient dose compared to continuous (30 fps) fluoroscopy.
- **Last-Image-Hold (LIH)** freezes the last frame for study without additional radiation exposure.
- **Magnification modes** (e.g., 6", 4.5") electronically zoom the central portion of the input phosphor, *improving spatial resolution* at the cost of field of view.
- **ALARA principle** (As Low As Reasonably Achievable) governs all fluoroscopic exposure decisions.
- **Dose-Area Product (DAP)** meter is mandatory on modern fluoroscopic systems for cumulative exposure tracking.
- **Flat-Panel Detectors (FPDs)** are gradually replacing II tubes in modern digital fluoroscopy, offering higher dynamic range, lower veiling glare, and better DQE.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

> - **Module:** Module 3
