\alpha \approx \frac{\sigma}{2} \sqrt{\dfrac{\mu_0}{\varepsilon}} \quad \text{and} \quad \beta \approx \omega \sqrt{\mu_0 \varepsilon}
\end{aligned}$$

The electric field decays exponentially as $E(z) = E_0 e^{-\alpha z}$.

The **depth of penetration** $D$ is defined as the depth at which the field amplitude falls to $\dfrac{1}{e} \approx 37\%$:

$$\begin{aligned}
D = \frac{1}{\alpha} = \frac{2}{\sigma} \sqrt{\dfrac{\varepsilon}{\mu_0}}
\end{aligned}$$

**Numerical Application to MWD in Muscle:**

Given $f = 2450 \text{ MHz}$, $\varepsilon_r = 49$:

$$\begin{aligned}
v_{tissue} &= \frac{c}{\sqrt{\varepsilon_r}} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{\sqrt{49}} = 4.286 \times 10^7 \text{ m/s} \\
\lambda_{tissue} &= \frac{v_{tissue}}{f} = \frac{4.286 \times 10^7}{2.45 \times 10^9} \approx 0.0175 \text{ m} = 1.75 \text{ cm} \\
D &= \frac{\lambda_{tissue}}{2\pi} = \frac{0.0175}{6.283} \approx 2.79 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\boxed{D \approx 2.79 \text{ cm}}$$

This confirms that $2450 \text{ MHz}$ MWD has shallow penetration, suitable only for superficial muscle heating.

---

### **Question B — 14 Marks (Option B — Internal Choice)**

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 3]**
> **(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, describe the principle and operation of a **microwave diathermy** unit. Discuss the role of the magnetron, waveguide, and circulator. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** Explain **monopolar** and **bipolar** surgical diathermy with their waveforms and clinical applications. Compare their safety profiles. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Microwave Diathermy (7 marks)**

A microwave diathermy unit generates continuous-wave EM radiation at $2450 \text{ MHz}$ and applies it to the patient's body via a directional antenna (refer to Section 4.2). The main blocks are:

1. **High-Voltage DC Power Supply (~4 kV):** Required to energise the cavity magnetron.
2. **Cavity Magnetron:** A high-power oscillator with a ring-shaped copper anode containing resonant cavities and a central heated cathode. When DC voltage is applied, electrons spiral past the cavities, exciting electromagnetic oscillations at $2450 \text{ MHz}$. Output power is typically 100–250 W.
3. **Waveguide (WR-430):** A hollow rectangular metal tube that transmits the $2450 \text{ MHz}$ microwave with minimal loss. Cut-off frequency prevents lower-frequency leakage.
4. **Ferrite Circulator (Isolator):** A non-reciprocal three-port device that channels reflected power (from a mismatched patient load) into a dummy water-load, protecting the magnetron from overheating and failure.
5. **Reflected Power Monitor:** Measures the SWR (standing wave ratio) to alert the clinician to improper applicator contact.
6. **Directional Applicator:** A hemispherical or rectangular horn antenna that concentrates the beam onto the treatment zone. A water bolus (or felt spacer) of $\lambda/4$ thickness is placed between applicator and skin to provide impedance matching and prevent surface overheating.

**Heat Generation:** Water dipoles in tissue attempt to align with the rapidly reversing E-field at $2450 \text{ MHz}$. Dielectric friction generates deep heat.

---

**Part (b) — Monopolar vs Bipolar Surgical Diathermy (7 marks)**

**Monopolar Electrosurgery:**
- Current path: Active electrode → patient body → dispersive (return) plate → generator.
- Active electrode is a small-area surgical pencil; high current density at the tip vaporises intracellular water → clean CUT (continuous sine wave) or COAG (damped bursts).
- Used for: general surgery, laparoscopy, large-vessel coagulation.
- Requires good return-plate contact; risk of alternate-site burns if plate detaches.

**Bipolar Electrosurgery:**
- Current path: One tip of bipolar forceps → tissue gripped between jaws → other tip. **No return plate required**.
- Only the tissue between the jaws is heated — extremely localised.
- Waveform is typically lower voltage, continuous sine wave for coagulation (e.g., 50–80 W).
- Used for: neurosurgery, ophthalmic surgery, microsurgery, tubal ligation.

**Waveform Comparison:**
- **Cut mode:** Continuous high-frequency sine wave, low crest factor (~1.5), rapid vaporisation.
- **Coag mode:** Damped bursts with high peak voltage, high crest factor (~6–8), slower desiccation.

| Safety Aspect | Monopolar | Bipolar |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Return electrode needed | Yes | No |
| Alternate-site burn risk | Present (if plate loose) | Absent |
| Suitable for patients with pacemaker | Contra-indicated | Safer (with caution) |
| Current through body | Whole torso | Only between jaws |
| Power setting | Higher (up to 300 W) | Lower (≤ 80 W) |

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students LOSE marks on Diathermy questions:**
> 1. **Confusing SWD and MWD frequencies** — Memorize: SWD = $27.12 \text{ MHz}$ (radio band); MWD = $2450 \text{ MHz}$ (microwave band). Writing "shortwave uses microwaves" costs 1 full mark.
> 2. **Forgetting the units in numerical derivations** — Always state the SI unit after each numerical result (e.g., "$D \approx 2.8 \text{ cm}$"). KTU examiners deduct marks for unit omission.
> 3. **Skipping the resonance/LC matching concept** — A complete SWD answer must mention that the patient forms one plate of a capacitor and the tank circuit is tuned to resonate at $27.12 \text{ MHz}$.
> 4. **Mixing up surgical vs therapeutic diathermy** — Surgical diathermy CUTS tissue; therapeutic diathermy HEALS tissue. Examiners award the CO (Course Outcome) mapping mark only if this distinction is explicit.
> 5. **Ignoring safety/contra-indications** — A 7-mark question on surgical diathermy without mention of the dispersive plate, isolation transformer, or pacemaker hazard is automatically capped at 5 marks.
> 6. **No block diagram** — A 7-mark "explain with block diagram" question scored without a diagram will lose a minimum of 2 marks as per KTU valuation scheme.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-density rapid-revision checklist for Diathermy (Module 3):**
> - **Diathermy** = "heating through" using HF current (0.3 MHz–2450 MHz).
> - **Three therapeutic modalities:** Longwave, Shortwave ($27.12 \text{ MHz}$), Microwave ($2450 \text{ MHz}$); plus Ultrasound ($1$–$3 \text{ MHz}$).
> - **SWD methods:** Capacitive (E-field, plates) vs Inductive (B-field, coil); $P_{eddy} \propto \sigma f^2 B^2 r^2$.
> - **MWD:** Uses **cavity magnetron** + **waveguide** + **ferrite circulator**; dipole rotation heating; penetration $\approx 2.8 \text{ cm}$ in muscle.
> - **Surgical diathermy:** Monopolar (with dispersive plate) vs Bipolar (no plate, forceps-only). Frequencies $\sim 500 \text{ kHz}$.
> - **Cut waveform:** Continuous sine wave → vaporisation → incision.
> - **Coag waveform:** Damped bursts → desiccation → haemostasis.
> - **Standard constants to memorize:** $f_{SWD} = 27.12 \text{ MHz}$; $f_{MWD} = 2450 \text{ MHz}$; $c = 3 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}$; $\varepsilon_{r,\text{muscle}} \approx 49$.
> - **Key formula:** $\lambda = c/f$; $D = \lambda_{tissue}/(2\pi)$.
> - **Safety:** Pacemakers, metallic implants, pregnancy, malignancy, testes, lens of eye are CONTRAINDICATED for diathermy.
> - **Engineering trade-off:** Higher frequency = more heating but less penetration. Clinicians must balance these.
> - **KTU-expected CO mapping:** This topic maps primarily to **CO3 — Therapeutic and Surgical Instrumentation**, Bloom levels *Understand* (L2), *Apply* (L3), and *Analyse* (L4).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Basic Principle of Computed Tomography (CT)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT412 - BIOMEDICAL INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Basic Principle of Computed Tomography (CT)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Computed Tomography (CT)** is a non-invasive diagnostic imaging modality that uses computer-processed combinations of multiple X-ray projections taken from different angles to produce cross-sectional (tomographic) images of specific areas of a scanned object, allowing the user to see inside the object without cutting it open.

The term *Tomography* originates from the Greek words *tomos* (slice) and *graphein* (to write). CT, therefore, literally means "writing slices."

In the context of **KTU 2024 Scheme (PEMRT412 - Biomedical Instrumentation)**, CT is studied as a radiation-based imaging system where the basic principle relies on measuring the **linear attenuation coefficient (μ)** of tissues along multiple linear paths and reconstructing a 2D map of μ(x,y) using mathematical algorithms.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Loaf of Bread" Analogy**
>
> Imagine a loaf of baked bread with a hidden raisin inside. If you look at the loaf from the front, all you see is the outer crust — you cannot tell the size, shape, or position of the raisin. This is what a conventional **X-ray radiograph** does: it compresses a 3D structure into a 2D shadow, and overlapping tissues become superimposed.
>
> Now, imagine slicing the loaf horizontally into thin pieces. By looking at each slice individually, you can clearly identify the raisin, measure its size, and pinpoint its exact position. **CT scanning does exactly this for the human body** — it virtually "slices" the body into cross-sectional images using mathematics, without ever physically cutting the patient.

## 1.3 Why CT? — Limitations of Conventional Radiography

Conventional X-ray radiography suffers from several critical limitations that CT was designed to overcome:

| Limitation of X-ray | How CT Overcomes It |
|---|---|
| **Superimposition** of anatomical structures (3D collapsed into 2D) | Produces **cross-sectional slices**, eliminating overlap |
| **Poor soft-tissue contrast** | Provides quantitative **CT numbers (Hounsfield Units)** |
| **Inability to distinguish small density differences** | Achieves contrast resolution of ~**0.5%** density difference |
| **No depth information** | Provides **3D volumetric** reconstruction |

## 1.4 Key Physical Quantities in CT

- **Linear Attenuation Coefficient (μ)**: Measured in **cm⁻¹**. Represents the fraction of X-ray photons absorbed/scattered per unit thickness of tissue.
- **CT Number / Hounsfield Unit (HU)**: A dimensionless normalized quantity used to standardize attenuation values across scanners. Water is defined as **0 HU** and air as **−1000 HU**.
- **Pixel (Picture Element)**: The smallest 2D element in a CT image.
- **Voxel (Volume Element)**: The 3D representation of a pixel with slice thickness.

> [!TIP]
> Remember: μ depends on the **X-ray photon energy (keV)** and the **physical density & atomic number** of the tissue. Bone has a high μ; air has a very low μ; soft tissue and water are intermediate.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Physical Principle — X-ray Attenuation

When a monochromatic X-ray beam of intensity $I_0$ passes through a homogeneous tissue of thickness $x$, the transmitted intensity $I$ is governed by the **Beer-Lambert Law**:

$$I = I_0 \, e^{-\mu x}$$

where:
- $I_0$ = incident X-ray intensity
- $I$ = transmitted X-ray intensity
- $\mu$ = linear attenuation coefficient (cm⁻¹)
- $x$ = thickness of the absorbing material (cm)

### For a Non-Homogeneous Medium

In reality, the human body is composed of tissues with varying μ along the beam path. For a ray passing through N different tissues:

$$I = I_0 \, \exp\left(-\sum_{i=1}^{N} \mu_i \, \Delta x_i\right)$$

In the continuous (integral) form:

$$I = I_0 \, \exp\left(-\int_L \mu(x,y) \, ds\right)$$

where the integral is taken along the line $L$ of the X-ray path, and $\mu(x,y)$ is the spatially varying attenuation coefficient we wish to reconstruct.

## 2.2 The CT Reconstruction Problem

The **fundamental challenge of CT** is the inverse problem: given measurements of $I/I_0$ along many lines $L$ at many angles $\theta$, reconstruct the function $\mu(x,y)$ over the entire 2D slice.

The **projection** $P(\theta, s)$ at angle $\theta$ and detector position $s$ is defined as:

$$P(\theta, s) = \ln\left(\frac{I_0}{I}\right) = \int_L \mu(x,y) \, ds$$

This is the **Radon Transform** of $\mu(x,y)$, named after the mathematician Johann Radon (1917).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Why" behind CT:** A single X-ray gives one line integral. A 2D image needs 2D data. By rotating the source-detector system around the patient and acquiring projections at many angles (typically 360° at ~1° increments → ~**360 projections** for a full scan), we collect enough 1D line integrals to reconstruct the 2D attenuation map.

## 2.3 CT Number (Hounsfield Unit)

Once $\mu(x,y)$ is reconstructed, each pixel is assigned a **CT number**:

$$HU = 1000 \times \frac{\mu_{\text{tissue}} - \mu_{\text{water}}}{\mu_{\text{water}}}$$

| Tissue / Material | Typical CT Number (HU) |
|---|---|
| Air | **−1000** |
| Lung | −600 to −400 |
| Fat | −100 to −50 |
| Water | **0** |
| CSF / Urine | +15 |
| Kidney | +30 |
| Blood (clotted) | +60 to +100 |
| Muscle | +35 to +55 |
| Bone (cortical) | +700 to +3000 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** CT numbers enable **tissue characterization** (e.g., distinguishing hemorrhage from ischemic stroke), **quantitative measurements** (e.g., bone mineral density), and **windowing** (displaying only a subset of HU values for optimal contrast).

## 2.4 Generations of CT Scanners (Historical & Operational)

| Generation | Geometry | Beam Shape | Detectors | Motion | Time per Slice |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **1st** | Translate-Rotate | Pencil beam | 1 | Linear + Rotational | **~5 min** |
| **2nd** | Translate-Rotate | Narrow fan | 5–30 | Linear + Rotational | **~20 s** |
| **3rd** | Rotate-Rotate | Wide fan beam | 600–1200 | Rotational only | **~1 s** |
| **4th** | Rotate-stationary | Wide fan beam | 1000+ (ring) | X-ray tube rotates | **~0.5 s** |
| **5th (Electron-beam)** | Stationary | Fan | Ring | Electron beam steered | **~50 ms** |
| **Helical (Spiral)** | Continuous rotation + patient translation | Fan | Array | Sliding gantry | **sub-second** |
| **Multi-slice (MDCT)** | Continuous rotation | Cone beam | Thousands | Sliding gantry | **<0.3 s, 320+ slices** |

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-YIELD FORMULAS — MEMORIZE THESE FOR KTU EXAMS**

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $I = I_0 e^{-\mu x}$ | Beer-Lambert Law (monoenergetic) | W/m², cm⁻¹, cm |
| 2 | $P = \int_L \mu(x,y) \, ds$ | Radon Transform (Projection) | dimensionless |
| 3 | $P = \ln(I_0 / I)$ | Measured projection from intensities | dimensionless |
| 4 | $HU = 1000 \cdot (\mu_t - \mu_w)/\mu_w$ | Hounsfield Unit (CT Number) | dimensionless (HU) |
| 5 | $\Delta x = d \cos\theta + s \sin\theta$ | Coordinate of voxel in projection line | cm |
| 6 | $\text{Spatial Resolution} \approx 1 \cdot \text{lp/mm}$ | Modern MDCT: ~ **0.4–0.5 mm** | mm |
| 7 | $\text{Noise} \propto \sqrt{N_{\text{photons}}}$ | Quantum (Poisson) noise | — |
| 8 | $D = \sum_i N_i \cdot HT$ | Dose-length product concept | mGy·cm |

**Note on table syntax:** All absolute-value or line-integral notations use $\int_L$ instead of vertical pipes to preserve markdown table integrity.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Radiation Therapy Planning:** CT provides the electron density map essential for calculating dose distributions in **linear accelerators (LINACs)**.
- **Image-Guided Interventions:** Real-time CT (CT-fluoroscopy) guides biopsies, drainages, and ablations.
- **Industrial Non-Destructive Testing (NDT):** Same principle used to inspect turbine blades, rocket motors, and pipeline welds.
- **Cardiac CT Angiography:** Sub-millimeter resolution of coronary arteries using **64-slice MDCT** with ECG gating.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Attenuation of a Monoenergetic X-ray Beam

**Step 1: Consider an infinitesimally thin slice of tissue $dx$.**

The probability that a single X-ray photon interacts with the tissue in this slice is proportional to $\mu$ and $dx$:

$$dI = -\mu \, I \, dx$$

The negative sign indicates intensity **decreases** with increasing depth (absorption dominates).

**Step 2: Rearrange the differential equation.**

$$\frac{dI}{I} = -\mu \, dx$$

**Step 3: Integrate both sides from 0 to $x$, with intensity going from $I_0$ to $I$.**

$$\int_{I_0}^{I} \frac{dI'}{I'} = -\mu \int_{0}^{x} dx'$$

**Step 4: Evaluate the integrals.**

$$\ln(I) - \ln(I_0) = -\mu x$$

$$\ln\left(\frac{I}{I_0}\right) = -\mu x$$

**Step 5: Exponentiate both sides to obtain the Beer-Lambert Law.**

$$I = I_0 \, e^{-\mu x} \qquad \blacksquare$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Projection for an Inhomogeneous Medium

**Step 1: Divide the beam path into $N$ small segments** of thickness $\Delta x_i$, each with attenuation $\mu_i$.

For segment $i$ alone: $I_i = I_{i-1} \, e^{-\mu_i \Delta x_i}$

**Step 2: Apply sequentially through all segments** (telescoping product).

$$I_N = I_0 \cdot e^{-\mu_1 \Delta x_1} \cdot e^{-\mu_2 \Delta x_2} \cdots e^{-\mu_N \Delta x_N}$$

$$I_N = I_0 \cdot \exp\left(-\sum_{i=1}^{N} \mu_i \, \Delta x_i\right)$$

**Step 3: Take the limit as $\Delta x_i \to 0$ to obtain the continuous form.**

$$I = I_0 \cdot \exp\left(-\int_L \mu(s) \, ds\right)$$

**Step 4: Define the projection $P$ as the line integral of attenuation.**

$$P = \int_L \mu(s) \, ds = \ln\left(\frac{I_0}{I}\right)$$

This is the **fundamental measured quantity** in a CT scanner. $\blacksquare$

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Hounsfield Unit (CT Number)

**Step 1:** Define the reference material as water, with $\mu_{\text{water}} = \mu_w$.

**Step 2:** Define a normalized, scaled quantity such that:
- Air (with $\mu \approx 0$) gives $HU = -1000$
- Water ($\mu = \mu_w$) gives $HU = 0$
- Dense bone ($\mu \gg \mu_w$) gives $HU \approx +1000$ to $+3000$

**Step 3:** Construct the linear normalization.

$$HU = k \cdot \frac{\mu_{\text{tissue}} - \mu_w}{\mu_w}$$

**Step 4:** Solve for $k$ using the air boundary condition** $\mu_{\text{air}} \approx 0$:**

$$-1000 = k \cdot \frac{0 - \mu_w}{\mu_w} = k \cdot (-1)$$

$$\therefore k = 1000$$

**Step 5:** Final form:

$$HU = 1000 \cdot \frac{\mu_{\text{tissue}} - \mu_w}{\mu_w} \qquad \blacksquare$$

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Simple Back-Projection Concept

**Step 1:** Consider a single point of high attenuation (e.g., bone) at location $(x_0, y_0)$ with value $\mu_0$.

**Step 2:** Its projection at angle $\theta$ and detector position $s$ is:

$$P(\theta, s) = \mu_0 \cdot \delta(s - s_0(\theta))$$

where $s_0(\theta) = x_0 \cos\theta + y_0 \sin\theta$ is the projection of the point onto the detector.

**Step 3:** Back-project: "smear" each projection back across the image plane at the corresponding angle.

$$\mu_{\text{back}}(x,y) = \int_{0}^{\pi} P(\theta, x\cos\theta + y\sin\theta) \, d\theta$$

**Step 4:** For a single point source, this produces a **star-shaped (1/r) blur** pattern — known as the **back-projection point spread function**.

**Step 5:** To remove this blur, **filtered back-projection (FBP)** is used: each projection is first convolved with a high-pass filter (e.g., Ram-Lak, Shepp-Logan) before back-projection. This sharpens the reconstructed image and is the standard reconstruction algorithm in clinical CT. $\blacksquare$

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Reconstruction Methods

| Method | Mathematical Basis | Speed | Image Quality | Modern Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Simple Back-Projection | $\int P(\theta,s) \, d\theta$ | Fast | Poor (blurred) | Educational only |
| Filtered Back-Projection (FBP) | $\int [P * h] \, d\theta$ | Fast | Good (sharp) | **Clinical standard** |
| Iterative (ART, SIRT) | Algebraic minimization | Slow | Very good | Specialized (low-dose) |
| Statistical Iterative (MBIR) | Poisson likelihood model | Very slow | Excellent | Emerging clinical |
| Deep Learning (AI-based) | Neural network prior | Fast | Excellent | **State-of-the-art (2024)** |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — CT Scanner Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    HV[High Voltage Generator 80 to 140 kV] --> XRAY[X ray Tube Rotating Anode]
    XRAY --> COLL[Collimator Shapes fan or cone beam]
    COLL --> PAT[Patient on Sliding Couch]
    PAT --> ATT[Attenuated X ray Beam]
    ATT --> DET[Detector Array Scintillator plus Photodiode]
    DET --> DAS[Data Acquisition System DAS]
    DAS --> REC[Reconstruction Computer FBP or Iterative]
    REC --> HU[CT Number Hounsfield Unit Map]
    HU --> DICOM[DICOM Image Storage]
    DICOM --> WS[Radiologist Workstation 3D Visualization]
    GANT[Gantry Rotation Motor Continuous Slip Ring] --> XRAY
    GANT --> DET
    CON[Console Operator Interface] --> HV
    CON --> GANT
    CON --> REC
```

## 4.2 Data Acquisition Flow — Projection to Image

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Single X ray Projection P theta s] --> B[Logarithmic Conversion ln I0 over I]
    B --> C[Apply High Pass Filter Ram Lak]
    C --> D[Back Project onto Image Matrix 512 by 512]
    D --> E[Sum all angles 0 to 180 degrees]
    E --> F[Final CT Image mu x y Map]
    F --> G[Calculate HU per Pixel]
    G --> H[Display Window Level Width Adjusted]
```

## 4.3 Generations of CT — Evolution Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    G1[Gen 1 Pencil Beam 1 Detector Translate Rotate] --> G2[Gen 2 Narrow Fan 30 Detectors]
    G2 --> G3[Gen 3 Wide Fan 600 plus Detectors Rotate Rotate]
    G3 --> G4[Gen 4 Stationary Detector Ring]
    G4 --> G5[Gen 5 Electron Beam Cardiology]
    G5 --> HEL[Helical Spiral Continuous Translation]
    HEL --> MDCT[Multi Detector CT MDCT 64 to 320 Slices]
    MDCT --> DECT[Dual Energy CT Spectral]
    DECT --> PCCT[Photon Counting CT Latest 2024]
```

## 4.4 Signal Path — From Photons to Pixels

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Acquisition
        P1[Photon Hits Scintillator] --> P2[Visible Light Burst]
        P2 --> P3[Photodiode Converts to Charge]
        P3 --> P4[Integrating Amplifier]
        P4 --> P5[Analog to Digital Converter 16 to 24 bit]
    end
    subgraph Processing
        P5 --> Q1[Logarithmic Amplifier]
        Q1 --> Q2[Beam Hardening Correction]
        Q2 --> Q3[Ring Artifact Removal]
        Q3 --> Q4[Filtered Back Projection FBP]
    end
    subgraph Display
        Q4 --> R1[512 by 512 Pixel Matrix]
        R1 --> R2[Window Level and Width Applied]
        R2 --> R3[Monitor Display 8 bit Gray Scale]
    end
```

> [!TIP]
> **Examination Tip:** When asked to "draw the block diagram of a CT system," always include the **X-ray source**, **gantry/rotation system**, **detector array**, **DAS (Data Acquisition System)**, **reconstruction computer**, and **display**. Skipping the reconstruction block is a common 2-mark deduction in KTU valuation.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024] Define Hounsfield Unit. Why is water assigned a value of 0 HU?**

> [!NOTE]
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> The **Hounsfield Unit (HU)**, or CT number, is a normalized quantitative scale of X-ray attenuation used in CT imaging. It is defined as:
>
> $$HU = 1000 \times \frac{\mu_{\text{tissue}} - \mu_{\text{water}}}{\mu_{\text{water}}}$$
>
> **Water is assigned 0 HU** because it is the chosen reference medium (most abundant biological substance) and gives a convenient midpoint for the scale. Air is defined as **−1000 HU** and dense cortical bone as **+1000 to +3000 HU**. This standardization allows comparison of scans across different machines and institutions.
>
> **(1 mark – definition, 1 mark – formula, 1 mark – justification of water = 0)**

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] State the Beer-Lambert Law as applied to X-ray attenuation. Why must CT use a polyenergetic spectrum correction?**

> [!NOTE]
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> The Beer-Lambert Law for a monoenergetic X-ray beam passing through a tissue of thickness $x$ with linear attenuation coefficient $\mu$ is:
>
> $$I = I_0 \, e^{-\mu x}$$
>
> For an inhomogeneous medium:
>
> $$I = I_0 \, \exp\left(-\int_L \mu(x,y) \, ds\right)$$
>
> **Real X-ray tubes produce a polyenergetic (broad-spectrum) beam.** As the beam traverses tissue, lower-energy photons are preferentially absorbed ("beam hardening"), causing μ to **decrease with depth**. This violates the assumption of constant μ. **Corrections** (water-bowtie filters, beam-hardening software algorithms) must therefore be applied during reconstruction to maintain the validity of the projection integral.
>
> **(1 mark – law, 1 mark – inhomogeneous form, 1 mark – polyenergetic correction need)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Question — Module 3 Internal Choice (14 Marks)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – July 2024]* — Set 1

> **Q3(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the basic principle and components of a Computed Tomography (CT) system. Discuss the role of the gantry, detector array, and reconstruction computer. **(7 marks)**
>
> **Q3(b)** Derive the relationship between Hounsfield Unit (HU) and linear attenuation coefficient. List the typical HU values for air, water, fat, muscle, and bone. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution — Q3(a) [7 marks]**

**Principle:** CT uses a **rotating fan-beam X-ray source and detector array** that revolves around the patient, acquiring **projections** of X-ray attenuation at hundreds of angles. From these projections, a 2D map of the linear attenuation coefficient $\mu(x,y)$ is reconstructed using the **Radon transform inversion (Filtered Back-Projection)**.

**Key Components:**

| Component | Function | Marks |
|---|---|---|
| **High-Voltage Generator** | Produces stable 80–140 kVp to drive the X-ray tube | 1 |
| **X-ray Tube** | Rotating-anode tube produces pulsed fan or cone beam | 1 |
| **Gantry** | Rotates the source-detector pair around the patient; uses **slip-ring** for continuous rotation in helical CT | 1 |
| **Collimator** | Shapes the beam to control slice thickness and patient dose | 0.5 |
| **Detector Array** | Scintillator (Gd₂O₂S or CsI) + photodiode converts X-rays to electrical signals; modern arrays have 600–100,000+ elements | 1.5 |
| **Data Acquisition System (DAS)** | Digitizes detector signals, performs logarithmic conversion, applies corrections | 1 |
| **Reconstruction Computer** | Performs FBP / iterative reconstruction, generates HU map | 1 |

**Working:**
1. Patient lies on a sliding couch inside the gantry bore.
2. The X-ray tube emits a thin fan/cone beam that passes through the body.
3. Attenuated photons are detected; the DAS computes the projection $P = \ln(I_0/I)$.
4. As the gantry rotates (typically 0.3–0.5 s per rotation), 1000+ projections per second are collected.
5. The reconstruction computer applies **filtered back-projection** to compute $\mu(x,y)$ for every voxel.
6. The HU image is displayed on a monitor with adjustable window/level.

**(Block diagram carries 2 marks; explanatory components 5 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Q3(b) [7 marks]**

**Derivation:**

**Step 1:** Take water as the reference material with attenuation $\mu_w$. Define the CT number $HU$ as a linear function of the deviation of tissue attenuation from water:

$$HU = k \cdot \left(\mu_t - \mu_w\right)$$

**Step 2:** Normalize by $\mu_w$ so the quantity is dimensionless and reproducible:

$$HU = k \cdot \frac{\mu_t - \mu_w}{\mu_w}$$

**Step 3:** Apply the boundary condition for **air** ($\mu \approx 0$): by convention $HU_{\text{air}} = -1000$.

$$-1000 = k \cdot \frac{0 - \mu_w}{\mu_w} = -k \implies k = 1000$$

**Step 4:** Final relationship:

$$HU = 1000 \cdot \frac{\mu_t - \mu_w}{\mu_w}$$

**Typical HU Values (Table – 3 marks):**

| Tissue | HU Value |
|---|---|
| Air | **−1000** |
| Fat | **−100 to −50** |
| Water | **0** |
| Muscle | **+35 to +55** |
| Cortical Bone | **+700 to +3000** |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> - Students often **omit the normalization by $\mu_w$**, giving $HU = 1000(\mu_t - \mu_w)$. This is **wrong** — must include the denominator.
> - Students confuse the **sign** of the air condition, leading to $k = -1000$. Always re-check using air first.
> - Do **not** state typical HU values without units or context; examiners deduct 0.5 marks per missing entry in the table.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – July 2024]* — Alternative Choice

> **Q4(a)** Explain the principle of image reconstruction in CT. With suitable diagrams, describe the **filtered back-projection (FBP)** method. **(7 marks)**
>
> **Q4(b)** A CT scanner measures a transmitted intensity of $I = 0.05 I_0$ through a 10 cm thick tissue. If the linear attenuation coefficient of the tissue at the operating kVp is $\mu = 0.2 \text{ cm}^{-1}$, verify the measurement and calculate the **Hounsfield Unit** assuming $\mu_{\text{water}} = 0.18 \text{ cm}^{-1}$. Also state whether the tissue is more likely to be **fat, water, or bone**. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution — Q4(a) [7 marks]**

CT reconstruction aims to recover $\mu(x,y)$ from projections $P(\theta, s) = \int \mu \, ds$.

**Back-Projection:** The simplest method "smears" each projection back across the image plane at the corresponding angle $\theta$:

$$\mu_{\text{back}}(x,y) = \int_{0}^{\pi} P(\theta, x\cos\theta + y\sin\theta) \, d\theta$$

**Problem:** This produces a $1/r$ blurred image (star artifact).

**Filtered Back-Projection (FBP) — Steps:**

1. **Acquire** projections $P(\theta, s)$ at all angles.
2. **Take the 1D Fourier transform** of each projection with respect to $s$: $\mathcal{F}\{P\}(θ, ω)$.
3. **Multiply by a ramp filter** $H(\omega) = \vert \omega \vert$ (or a windowed variant such as Ram-Lak, Shepp-Logan, Cosine, Hamming) in the frequency domain.
4. **Take the inverse Fourier transform** to obtain the filtered projection: $P_f = \mathcal{F}^{-1}\{H \cdot \mathcal{F}\{P\}\}$.
5. **Back-project** the filtered projection across the image.
6. **Result:** A sharp, artifact-free reconstruction of $\mu(x,y)$.

**Mathematically:**

$$\mu(x,y) = \int_{0}^{\pi} \left[ P(\theta, s) * h(s) \right]_{s = x\cos\theta + y\sin\theta} d\theta$$

where $h(s)$ is the inverse Fourier transform of the ramp filter, and $*$ denotes 1D convolution.

**(Diagram of FBP pipeline with 3 marks; explanation with 4 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Q4(b) [7 marks]**

**Step 1: Apply the Beer-Lambert Law to verify the measurement.**

$$I = I_0 \, e^{-\mu x}$$

$$I = I_0 \cdot e^{-(0.2)(10)} = I_0 \cdot e^{-2} = I_0 \cdot 0.1353$$

$$I / I_0 = 0.1353$$

**Verification:** The measured value is $I/I_0 = 0.05$, which is **lower** than the predicted $0.1353$. This discrepancy occurs because the actual beam is **polyenergetic** and the true μ is energy-dependent, or because the tissue is not perfectly homogeneous. *(If students assume the question implies a homogeneous tissue and directly use the given μ, they proceed to step 2.)* — For this problem, we use the **given μ = 0.2 cm⁻¹** as the effective monoenergetic value for HU calculation.

**Step 2: Calculate the Hounsfield Unit.**

$$HU = 1000 \cdot \frac{\mu_t - \mu_w}{\mu_w} = 1000 \cdot \frac{0.20 - 0.18}{0.18}$$

$$HU = 1000 \cdot \frac{0.02}{0.18} = 1000 \cdot 0.1111$$

$$\boxed{HU \approx +111}$$

**Step 3: Identify the tissue.**

From the standard table:
- Fat: −100 to −50 HU ❌
- Water: 0 HU ❌
- Muscle: +35 to +55 HU ❌
- Cortical bone: +700 to +3000 HU ❌

An HU of **+111** corresponds to **clotted blood / dense tissue / fresh hemorrhage**. It is **NOT** fat (which would be negative) and is **denser than water and muscle** but **less dense than cortical bone**.

**Answer:** The tissue is most likely **clotted blood (hematoma) or dense soft tissue**, with $HU \approx +111$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Q4(b):**
> - **Sign mistakes:** $(0.20 - 0.18)$ is positive — do not write $(0.18 - 0.20)$.
> - **Forgetting to divide by μ_w:** This is a 2-mark deduction if missed.
> - **Wrong tissue identification:** Many students say "bone" — but bone is +700 to +3000, not +111. Read the table carefully.
> - **Unit omission:** HU is **dimensionless**; do not write "HU units" redundantly.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — MODULE 3: BASIC PRINCIPLE OF CT**

- [x] **CT = "Computed Tomography"** = X-ray-based cross-sectional imaging using Radon transform inversion.
- [x] **Beer-Lambert Law:** $I = I_0 e^{-\mu x}$ — foundation of all projection measurements.
- [x] **Projection:** $P = \ln(I_0/I) = \int_L \mu(x,y) \, ds$ — the actual quantity reconstructed.
- [x] **CT Number / Hounsfield Unit:** $HU = 1000(\mu_t - \mu_w)/\mu_w$; water = 0, air = −1000.
- [x] **HU Reference values:** Air = −1000, Fat ≈ −100, Water = 0, Muscle ≈ +50, Bone ≈ +1000.
- [x] **Rotating source-detector** around patient provides multiple angular projections.
- [x] **Filtered Back-Projection (FBP)** = ramp filter in Fourier domain + back-projection = standard reconstruction.
- [x] **Generations:** 1st (pencil) → 2nd (narrow fan) → 3rd (wide fan, rotate-rotate) → 4th (ring detectors) → Helical → MDCT → Photon-Counting.
- [x] **Spatial resolution** of modern MDCT: ~ **0.4–0.5 mm**.
- [x] **Reconstruction algorithms:** FBP (fast, clinical standard), Iterative (slower, better for low-dose), AI-based (state-of-the-art 2024).
- [x] **Beam hardening correction** is essential because real X-ray tubes are polyenergetic.
- [x] **Window/Level** display lets the radiologist visualize specific HU ranges (e.g., lung window, bone window, brain window).

> [!TIP]
> **Final KTU Exam Strategy:** When asked to "explain the basic principle of CT," always structure your answer in **three layers**:
> 1. **Physical principle** (X-ray attenuation + Beer-Lambert).
> 2. **Data acquisition** (rotating source + multi-angle projections).
> 3. **Reconstruction** (Radon transform + FBP → HU image).
>
> This 3-layer structure is exactly what KTU examiners expect for full 7-mark answers. Skipping any layer costs 2–3 marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Magnetic Resonance Imaging (MRI) System and Nuclear Medicine Imaging System (PET, SPECT)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT412 - BIOMEDICAL INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Magnetic Resonance Imaging (MRI) System and Nuclear Medicine Imaging System (PET, SPECT)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# MODULE 3 — MRI, PET & SPECT IMAGING SYSTEMS

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Magnetic Resonance Imaging (MRI)

**Formal KTU Definition:**
> Magnetic Resonance Imaging (MRI) is a non-invasive, non-ionizing diagnostic imaging modality that exploits the principles of **Nuclear Magnetic Resonance (NMR)** to generate high-resolution, multi-planar tomographic images of the human body. The technique involves placing the patient inside a strong static magnetic field (typically **0.5 T to 7 T**), perturbing hydrogen proton spins using a Radio-Frequency (RF) pulse tuned to the **Larmor frequency**, and spatially encoding the resulting relaxation signals using switched gradient magnetic fields.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** MRI does NOT use ionizing radiation (unlike X-ray, CT, or PET). It is the gold standard for imaging **soft tissues** such as the brain, spinal cord, muscles, ligaments, and cartilage.

**Intuitive Analogy — "The Spinning Compass in a Magnetic Field"**

Imagine billions of tiny spinning tops (hydrogen protons in water and fat inside your body). Normally, they spin in random directions — their magnetic axes point everywhere, so the net magnetic signal cancels out to zero. Now place them inside a giant, uniform **Earth-like magnetic field (B₀)**. Just like a compass needle aligns with the Earth's magnetic field, these tiny protons **precess and align either with or against the field**. More align *with* it (lower energy state), producing a small but measurable net longitudinal magnetization (M₀).

Next, you "ping" them with a **radio-frequency pulse at exactly the right frequency (Larmor frequency)** — analogous to pushing a swing at its resonant frequency. The protons absorb this energy and tip away from alignment. When you stop pinging, they "relax" back, releasing energy as a faint RF signal. By varying the magnetic field slightly across the body (using gradient coils), you can map *where* each signal came from, ultimately reconstructing an anatomical image.

**Physical Constants and Key Metrics:**

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value |
|---|---|---|
| Gyromagnetic ratio of hydrogen | γ | 2.675 × 10⁸ rad·s⁻¹·T⁻¹ |
| γ/2π for ¹H | — | **42.58 MHz/T** |
| Larmor frequency at 1.5 T | ω₀ | 63.87 MHz |
| Larmor frequency at 3 T | ω₀ | 127.74 MHz |
| Standard clinical field strength | B₀ | 1.5 T or 3 T |
| High-resolution/research field | B₀ | 7 T |
| Energy of 511 keV photon | Eγ | **511 keV** (PET) |
| Tc-99m gamma energy | Eγ | **140 keV** (SPECT) |
| Speed of light | c | 3 × 10⁸ m/s |

---

### 1.2 Positron Emission Tomography (PET)

**Formal KTU Definition:**
> PET is a **functional nuclear medicine imaging modality** that visualizes *physiological and metabolic processes* in vivo by detecting pairs of **511 keV annihilation photons** emitted indirectly by a positron-emitting radiopharmaceutical (most commonly **¹⁸F-Fluorodeoxyglucose / FDG**). The coincident detection of the two 180°-opposed photons by a ring of scintillation detectors allows reconstruction of the spatial distribution of the tracer.

> [!NOTE]
> PET shows **function** (metabolism, receptor binding), not anatomy. It is the clinical gold standard for **oncology** (tumor staging), **neurology** (Alzheimer's, epilepsy), and **cardiology** (myocardial viability).

**Intuitive Analogy — "Glow-in-the-Dark Sugar Hunt"**

Think of a tumor cell as a hungry, fast-eating child. Inject a tiny amount of radioactive sugar (FDG) into the body. Healthy cells eat sugar slowly, but tumor cells gobble it up greedily and trap it inside. The radioactive sugar atoms then undergo **beta-plus decay** — emitting a **positron** (an anti-electron). This positron travels a few millimeters in tissue, meets a regular electron, and the two **annihilate** (E = mc² → energy), producing **two gamma rays** that shoot off in **exactly opposite directions** (180° apart), like a perfectly symmetric fireworks burst.

The PET scanner is essentially a giant ring of light sensors watching for these "firework pairs." When two sensors fire at *exactly the same instant (in coincidence)*, the scanner draws a line between them. With millions of such lines, it triangulates the exact 3-D location of the "hungry child" — revealing the tumor.

---

### 1.3 Single Photon Emission Computed Tomography (SPECT)

**Formal KTU Definition:**
> SPECT is a **functional nuclear imaging technique** that uses **single gamma-emitting radiopharmaceuticals** (e.g., **Tc-99m HMPAO**, **Tl-201**, **I-123**) and a rotating gamma camera (typically one, two, or three detector heads) equipped with a **parallel-hole collimator** to acquire multiple 2-D projections from various angles around the patient. These projections are then computationally reconstructed (filtered back-projection or iterative methods) into 3-D tomographic images of tracer distribution.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Unlike PET, SPECT does **NOT** rely on coincidence detection — it uses a **physical collimator** to determine photon direction, which dramatically reduces sensitivity compared to PET.

**Intuitive Analogy — "The Pinhole Camera in a Dark Room"**

Imagine shining a flashlight in a completely dark room — the light scatters everywhere, and you cannot tell where it came from. To form an image, you place a sheet of cardboard with one small hole (a pinhole). Now each point in the room projects through the hole to a *unique* point on the wall — forming an inverted image. SPECT's **parallel-hole collimator** plays the same role: it is a thick lead plate with thousands of parallel holes, allowing only photons traveling along specific directions to reach the detector. By rotating the gamma camera head 360° around the patient, the system acquires many such "pinhole" images, and the computer fuses them into a 3-D map of tracer uptake.

> [!NOTE]
> **Clinical use:** Myocardial perfusion imaging (blood flow to the heart), bone scintigraphy, thyroid imaging, and brain perfusion studies (e.g., dopamine transporter imaging in Parkinson's).

---

### 1.4 Side-by-Side Comparison (Module 3 Summary)

| Feature | MRI | PET | SPECT |
|---|---|---|---|
| Radiation type | RF (non-ionizing) | 511 keV γ (ionizing) | 140 keV γ (ionizing) |
| Source signal | Hydrogen proton relaxation | Positron annihilation | Direct gamma emission |
| Tracer | None (endogenous water/fat) | ¹⁸F-FDG, ¹¹C, ¹³N, ¹⁵O | Tc-99m, Tl-201, I-123 |
| Spatial resolution | **~0.5–1 mm** | ~4–5 mm | ~7–10 mm |
| Imaging type | Anatomical + functional | Functional/metabolic | Functional/perfusion |
| Typical scan time | 20–60 min | 15–30 min | 15–30 min |
| Cost | Very high | Very high | Moderate |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 MRI — Operational Principle (The Bloch Equation Framework)

The complete classical description of magnetization behaviour in MRI is given by the **Bloch equations** (1946). For a spin-½ nucleus (e.g., ¹H) placed in a static field B₀ along the z-axis:

### Step 1 — Equilibrium Magnetization (No RF)

When a tissue sample is placed in B₀, the proton magnetic moments **precess** around the field axis. The net magnetization vector **M** has two components:
- **Longitudinal (M_z):** aligned with B₀
- **Transverse (M_xy):** randomly phased, averages to zero

At thermal equilibrium:

$$M_{z,eq} = M_0 = \frac{N \gamma^2 \hbar^2 B_0}{4 k_B T}$$

where **N** = proton density, **ħ** = reduced Planck's constant, **k_B** = Boltzmann constant, **T** = absolute temperature.

> **Intuition:** More protons + stronger magnet + colder sample = bigger signal. That is why cryogenically cooled high-field magnets (7 T) and tissues with high water content (CSF, tumors) produce strong MRI signals.

### Step 2 — RF Excitation (Resonance Condition)

An RF pulse oscillating at the **Larmor frequency** (B₁ field, perpendicular to B₀) tips the magnetization by a flip angle α. The condition for resonance is the **Larmor equation**:

$$\omega_0 = \gamma B_0$$

This is the single most important equation in MRI. The RF pulse rotates M away from the z-axis into the transverse (xy) plane, generating detectable signal in a receive coil (Faraday's law).

### Step 3 — Free Precession and Relaxation

After the RF pulse, M returns to equilibrium via two independent, simultaneous exponential processes:

**Longitudinal (T1) Recovery** — energy exchange with the lattice (spin–lattice):

$$M_z(t) = M_0 \left(1 - e^{-t/T_1}\right)$$

**Transverse (T2) Decay** — dephasing of spin ensemble (spin–spin):

$$M_{xy}(t) = M_0 \, e^{-t/T_2} \, \sin(\alpha)$$

> **Intuition:** T1 = "how fast the magnet recharges its battery along z." T2 = "how fast the spinning fans de-phase in the xy plane."

| Tissue | T1 (ms) at 1.5 T | T2 (ms) at 1.5 T |
|---|---|---|
| Fat | ~250 | ~70 |
| White matter | ~780 | ~90 |
| Gray matter | ~920 | ~100 |
| CSF (water) | ~4000 | ~2000 |
| Muscle | ~870 | ~47 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Image contrast** in MRI is governed by three parameters: **Proton density (ρ), T1, and T2**. By choosing the pulse sequence parameters (TR = repetition time, TE = echo time), the radiologist controls *which* property dominates the image — this is the **T1-weighted, T2-weighted, or PD-weighted** contrast mechanism that KTU examiners love to ask about.

### Step 4 — Spatial Encoding (Three Gradients)

The scanner uses three orthogonal **gradient coils** (X, Y, Z) that superimpose a linearly varying field on B₀:

$$B_z(x,y,z,t) = B_0 + G_x x + G_y y + G_z z$$

- **Slice-select gradient (G_ss):** Selects the 2-D slice to be imaged.
- **Frequency-encoding gradient (G_fe):** During signal readout, makes Larmor frequency a linear function of position along one axis (k-space traversal in x).
- **Phase-encoding gradient (G_pe):** Imparts a position-dependent phase shift before readout, encoded row-by-row in k-space along the other axis.

The acquired signal populates the **k-space** (spatial frequency domain), and the image is recovered via the **2-D Inverse Fourier Transform**:

$$I(x,y) = \iint S(k_x, k_y) \, e^{i 2\pi (k_x x + k_y y)} \, dk_x \, dk_y$$

### Step 5 — Subsystems of an MRI Scanner

| Subsystem | Function |
|---|---|
| **Main magnet** (superconducting, He-cooled) | Generates B₀ (1.5 T, 3 T, 7 T) |
| **Gradient coils** (X, Y, Z) | Spatial encoding via linear field variations |
| **RF transmitter coil** | Delivers B₁ pulse at ω₀ |
| **RF receiver coil** | Detects precessing magnetization (FID, echo) |
| **Shim coils** | Homogenize B₀ (correct field inhomogeneities) |
| **Computer & reconstruction system** | k-space → image, controls pulse sequence |
| **Patient table & bore** | Mechanical support, RF shielding (Faraday cage) |

### Common Pulse Sequences (KTU-favorite topic)

- **Spin Echo (SE):** 90° pulse → wait TE/2 → 180° pulse → echo at TE. T2-weighted contrast. T₂* compensated.
- **Gradient Echo (GRE):** Variable flip angle α < 90°. Faster than SE, but sensitive to T₂*. Used for 3-D imaging, angiography.
- **Inversion Recovery (IR):** 180° → 90° → echo. Suppresses specific tissues (e.g., **STIR** = fat suppression, **FLAIR** = CSF suppression).
- **Echo Planar Imaging (EPI):** Single-shot k-space traversal. Foundation of **functional MRI (fMRI)** via BOLD effect.

---

## 2.2 PET — Operational Principle

### Step 1 — Radiotracer Production

PET uses **positron (β⁺) emitters**, typically produced in a **cyclotron**:
- **¹⁸F** (t½ = 109.8 min) → FDG (fluorodeoxyglucose)
- **¹¹C** (t½ = 20.4 min)
- **¹³N** (t½ = 9.96 min)
- **¹⁵O** (t½ = 2.03 min)
- **⁶⁸Ga** (t½ = 68 min, generator-produced)

### Step 2 — Positron Emission and Annihilation

A proton-rich radionuclide undergoes β⁺ decay:

$$p \rightarrow n + e^+ + \nu_e$$

The emitted positron **thermalizes** in tissue over a short distance (1–3 mm, the **positron range**) and then annihilates with a nearby electron:

$$e^+ + e^- \rightarrow 2\gamma \quad (511 \text{ keV each, } 180° \text{ apart})$$

By **conservation of energy and momentum**, the two 511 keV photons fly off in **exactly opposite directions**.

> [!NOTE]
> The 511 keV energy arises from the rest mass of an electron/positron: E = m_e c² = 511 keV.

### Step 3 — Coincidence Detection

A ring of **scintillation detectors** (typically **Bismuth Germanate, BGO** or **Lutetium Oxyorthosilicate, LSO/LYSO**) surrounds the patient. When two detectors fire within a narrow **coincidence time window** (~6–12 ns), the event is recorded as a **"line of response" (LOR)** along which the annihilation occurred.

Three types of coincidences:
- **True coincidences:** Both photons from the same annihilation (the useful events).
- **Scattered coincidences:** One or both photons Compton-scattered — assigned to wrong LOR.
- **Random coincidences:** Two unrelated photons detected simultaneously.

### Step 4 — Image Reconstruction

After correction for **attenuation, scatter, randoms, dead time, and normalization**, the LORs are reconstructed into 3-D images using:
- **2-D/3-D Filtered Back-Projection (FBP)**, or
- **Iterative reconstruction** (e.g., OSEM — Ordered Subsets Expectation Maximization).

The final output is a parametric map of **Standardized Uptake Value (SUV)**:

$$SUV = \frac{\text{Tracer activity concentration (kBq/mL)}}{\text{Injected dose (MBq) / Body weight (kg)}}$$

---

## 2.3 SPECT — Operational Principle

### Step 1 — Radiopharmaceutical

SPECT uses **single-photon gamma emitters**, predominantly **Tc-99m** (t½ = 6 hours, γ = 140 keV), eluted from a **⁹⁹Mo/⁹⁹ᵐTc generator**.

### Step 2 — Gamma Camera Components

A **gamma camera (Anger camera)** has four main components:
1. **Collimator** — Lead plate with parallel holes (or pinhole, fan-beam). Defines photon direction. **Most photons (>99%) are absorbed here**, which is why SPECT sensitivity is so low.
2. **Scintillator** — NaI(Tl) crystal (~10 mm thick) that converts each 140 keV photon into a flash of visible light (~300–400 photons per event).
3. **Photomultiplier Tubes (PMTs)** — Hexagonal array of 30–100 PMTs that convert scintillation light into proportional electrical pulses.
4. **Position-computing circuitry (Anger logic)** — Weighted centroid calculation from PMT signals determines the (x, y) position of the event and the total energy (z = pulse-height analyzer).

### Step 3 — Tomographic Acquisition

The gamma camera head rotates 360° around the patient in step-and-shoot or continuous-rotation mode, acquiring 60–120 projections. Each projection is a 2-D planar scintigram. After back-projection through filtered (e.g., **Ramp + Butterworth**) sinograms, a 3-D volume is reconstructed.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **SPECT/CT hybrid scanners** now dominate the market — the CT provides **attenuation correction** and anatomical co-registration, dramatically improving diagnostic accuracy.

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description / Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\omega_0 = \gamma B_0$ | **Larmor equation** — resonance condition |
| 2 | $f_0 = \gamma B_0 / 2\pi$ | Larmor frequency in Hz |
| 3 | $M_z(t) = M_0 (1 - e^{-t/T_1})$ | Longitudinal (T1) recovery |
| 4 | $M_{xy}(t) = M_0 \, e^{-t/T_2} \sin\alpha$ | Transverse (T2) decay |
| 5 | $SNR \propto B_0^{1.5 \text{ to } 2}$ | SNR scales supra-linearly with field |
| 6 | $E = h\nu = \hbar \omega_0 = \gamma \hbar B_0$ | Energy of RF photon |
| 7 | $E_{511} = m_e c^2 = 511 \text{ keV}$ | PET annihilation photon energy |
| 8 | $E_{Tc\text{-}99m} = 140 \text{ keV}$ | SPECT gamma photon energy |
| 9 | $\lambda = hc / E$ | Photon wavelength (λ₁H@1.5T ≈ 4.7 m) |
| 10 | $T_2^* = \left(\frac{1}{T_2} + \frac{1}{T_2'}\right)^{-1}$ | Effective transverse relaxation (with inhomogeneity) |
| 11 | $SUV = A_{voxel} / (D_{inj}/W_{body})$ | Standardized Uptake Value (PET) |
| 12 | $k_B T_1 = $ (Zeeman energy fluctuation timescale) | Sets baseline T1 order of magnitude |
| 13 | $G_{max} \approx 40 \text{ mT/m}$ | Modern gradient strength limit |
| 14 | $S/N \propto \sqrt{N_{acq}}$ | SNR improvement with N averages |

> **Rule of thumb (for deriving γ/2π):** If the examiner gives you γ = 2.675 × 10⁸ rad·s⁻¹·T⁻¹, then dividing by 2π gives **42.58 MHz/T** — a must-memorize number for KTU MRI problems.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Significance

- **MRI engineering challenges:** Magnet homogeneity (≤10 ppm over 50 cm DSV), gradient slew rate (≥150 T/m/s for EPI), RF coil Q-factor, SAR (Specific Absorption Rate ≤ 4 W/kg head), cryogenic He consumption.
- **PET engineering challenges:** Time-of-Flight (TOF) coincidence resolution (~300 ps for LSO:Ce), depth-of-interaction (DOI) correction, attenuation correction (using CT), scatter correction algorithms.
- **SPECT engineering challenges:** Collimator design (HEGP vs. LEHR — high-energy vs. low-energy), dead-time correction, partial volume effect, motion correction.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Larmor Frequency (Classical Treatment)

### Background

A proton has a magnetic moment **μ** proportional to its spin angular momentum **S**:

$$\vec{\mu} = \gamma \vec{S}$$

When placed in an external magnetic field **B₀ = B₀ ẑ**, the magnetic moment experiences a torque:

$$\vec{\tau} = \vec{\mu} \times \vec{B_0}$$

By Newton's second law for rotational dynamics, **dS/dt = τ**, hence:

$$\frac{d\vec{\mu}}{dt} = \gamma \, \vec{\mu} \times \vec{B_0}$$

### Step-by-Step Expansion

Write out the three Cartesian components with B₀ = (0, 0, B₀):

$$\frac{d\mu_x}{dt} = \gamma (\mu_y B_0 - \mu_z \cdot 0) = \gamma \mu_y B_0$$

$$\frac{d\mu_y}{dt} = \gamma (\mu_z B_0 - \mu_x \cdot 0) \cdot (-1) = -\gamma \mu_x B_0$$

$$\frac{d\mu_z}{dt} = \gamma (\mu_x \cdot 0 - \mu_y \cdot 0) = 0$$

The last equation shows **μ_z is conserved** — i.e., the magnitude along the field is constant. To solve the first two, differentiate the first again:

$$\frac{d^2 \mu_x}{dt^2} = \gamma B_0 \frac{d\mu_y}{dt} = \gamma B_0 \cdot (-\gamma \mu_x B_0) = -\gamma^2 B_0^2 \, \mu_x$$

This is the simple harmonic oscillator equation:

$$\frac{d^2 \mu_x}{dt^2} + (\gamma B_0)^2 \, \mu_x = 0$$

The angular frequency of oscillation is therefore:

$$\boxed{\omega_0 = \gamma B_0}$$

The general solution is:

$$\mu_x(t) = \mu_\perp \cos(\omega_0 t + \phi), \quad \mu_y(t) = \mu_\perp \sin(\omega_0 t + \phi)$$

This represents **circular precession** of the magnetic moment about B₀ at the **Larmor frequency** ω₀.

> **Key insight:** This derivation uses only classical mechanics. It is valid because in a strong B₀, the quantum spin-½ expectation values behave classically (Ehrenfest's theorem).

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the NMR Signal — Rotating Frame and FID

In the laboratory frame, the precessing transverse magnetization induces an oscillating voltage in a receive coil (Faraday's law, by Lenz's law). The signal is:

$$S(t) \propto M_0 \, e^{-t/T_2} \, e^{i \omega_0 t}$$

This is the **Free Induction Decay (FID)**. The envelope decays at T₂; the carrier oscillates at ω₀.

To simplify analysis, transform into the **rotating frame** rotating at ω₀ about z. In this frame, the effective field is **B_eff = B₀ - ω₀/γ = 0**, so magnetization appears stationary. The Bloch equations reduce to:

$$\frac{dM'_x}{dt} = 0, \quad \frac{dM'_y}{dt} = 0, \quad \frac{dM'_z}{dt} = -\frac{(M'_z - M_0)}{T_1}$$

And transverse relaxation:

$$\frac{dM'_{xy}}{dt} = -\frac{M'_{xy}}{T_2}$$

with solutions (for a 90° pulse tipping M from z into y'):

$$M'_y(t) = M_0 \, e^{-t/T_2}, \quad M'_z(t) = M_0 (1 - e^{-t/T_1})$$

This is the **classical foundation of all MRI signal equations**.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Derivation: Larmor Frequency Calculation (Worked Example)

**Question:** Calculate the Larmor frequency of hydrogen protons in a 1.5 T MRI scanner. Use γ = 2.675 × 10⁸ rad·s⁻¹·T⁻¹.

**Step 1 — Identify given values:**

$$\gamma = 2.675 \times 10^8 \text{ rad·s}^{-1}\text{·T}^{-1}, \quad B_0 = 1.5 \text{ T}$$

**Step 2 — Apply Larmor equation:**

$$\omega_0 = \gamma B_0 = (2.675 \times 10^8) \times (1.5)$$

$$\omega_0 = 4.0125 \times 10^8 \text{ rad·s}^{-1}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to Hz (divide by 2π):**

$$f_0 = \frac{\omega_0}{2\pi} = \frac{4.0125 \times 10^8}{2\pi} = \frac{4.0125 \times 10^8}{6.2832}$$

$$f_0 \approx 6.387 \times 10^7 \text{ Hz} = 63.87 \text{ MHz}$$

**Step 4 — Cross-check with the rule of thumb 42.58 MHz/T:**

$$f_0 = 42.58 \text{ MHz/T} \times 1.5 \text{ T} = 63.87 \text{ MHz} \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 5 — Wavelength of RF photon in vacuum (λ = c/f):**

$$\lambda = \frac{3 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}}{6.387 \times 10^7 \text{ Hz}} \approx 4.70 \text{ m}$$

> **Practical implication:** The RF wavelength (~5 m) is much larger than the human body. This is why **birdcage coils** and **uniform B₁ fields** are essential, and why ultra-high-field MRI (7 T, f₀ ≈ 300 MHz, λ ≈ 1 m) introduces **standing wave and dielectric effects**.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: PET Annihilation Energy

**Step 1 — Rest mass energy of an electron (or positron):**

$$m_e = 9.109 \times 10^{-31} \text{ kg}$$

**Step 2 — Apply E = mc²:**

$$E = (9.109 \times 10^{-31}) \times (3 \times 10^8)^2$$

$$E = 9.109 \times 10^{-31} \times 9 \times 10^{16} = 8.198 \times 10^{-14} \text{ J}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to eV (1 eV = 1.602 × 10⁻¹⁹ J):**

$$E = \frac{8.198 \times 10^{-14}}{1.602 \times 10^{-19}} \text{ eV} = 5.11 \times 10^5 \text{ eV} = \mathbf{511 \text{ keV}}$$

**Step 4 — By conservation of energy and momentum at annihilation, the energy is shared equally between the two photons traveling in opposite directions:**

$$E_{\gamma,1} = E_{\gamma,2} = m_e c^2 = 511 \text{ keV}$$

> **KTU pitfall:** Students often confuse the 511 keV energy with the input energy of the cyclotron. The 511 keV is the **rest-mass energy of the electron-positron pair** — it is fixed by fundamental physics, independent of the tracer.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Contrast Mechanism in MRI

| Weighting | TR (ms) | TE (ms) | Contrast Dominated By | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **T1-weighted** | Short (300–600) | Short (10–20) | T1 differences | Anatomy, fat bright, CSF dark |
| **T2-weighted** | Long (2000–4000) | Long (80–120) | T2 differences | Pathology (edema bright), CSF bright |
| **Proton density (PD)** | Long | Short | ρ (proton density) | High-resolution anatomy |
| **FLAIR** (IR-based) | Long | Long | T2 with CSF nulled | MS lesions, periventricular pathology |
| **STIR** (IR-based) | Short | Short | T1 with fat nulled | Bone marrow edema |

> **TR/TE rule of thumb:** Short TR → T1 weighting. Long TE → T2 weighting. Short TR + short TE = PD-like. Long TR + long TE = T2.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->
