# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define the term "Larmor frequency" and state its significance in MRI. Calculate the Larmor frequency for ¹H in a 1.5 T scanner.** *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

The Larmor frequency (ω₀) is the angular frequency at which the net nuclear magnetic moment **precesses** about an external static magnetic field B₀. It is given by the Larmor equation:

$$\omega_0 = \gamma B_0 \quad \text{or} \quad f_0 = \frac{\gamma B_0}{2\pi}$$

It is significant because the RF excitation pulse must be transmitted at **exactly** this frequency to achieve resonance and tip the magnetization vector into the transverse plane (Bloch condition). The signal induced in the receive coil also precesses at ω₀.

For ¹H at 1.5 T:

$$f_0 = 42.58 \text{ MHz/T} \times 1.5 \text{ T} = 63.87 \text{ MHz}$$

$$\boxed{f_0 \approx 63.87 \text{ MHz}}$$

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**List the three main components of a gamma camera used in SPECT and state the function of each.** *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

1. **Collimator (parallel-hole lead plate):** Defines the direction of incoming gamma photons by physically absorbing photons not traveling parallel to the holes. It establishes the geometric correspondence between source and image — analogous to a lens in optics.
2. **Scintillator (NaI(Tl) crystal):** Converts each 140 keV gamma photon into a brief flash of visible light (~300–400 photons/event).
3. **Photomultiplier Tube (PMT) Array + Anger Logic:** Converts the scintillation light into proportional electrical pulses; the position-computing circuit determines the (x, y) location and total energy (z) of each event.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often mention only "PMT" and forget the **Anger position-computing logic** — this is a separate subsystem and is worth marks.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]**

**(a) With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a Magnetic Resonance Imaging (MRI) system. Discuss the role of the main magnet, gradient coils, and RF coils.** *(7 marks)*

**(b) Explain the T1 and T2 relaxation processes in MRI. How are they used to generate T1-weighted and T2-weighted images?** *(7 marks)*

### Model Solution — (a)

The MRI system consists of the following main subsystems (block diagram shown in Section 4.1):

**1. Main Superconducting Magnet:**
- Produces the static B₀ field (1.5 T or 3 T clinical).
- Wires of Nb-Ti alloy are superconducting at 4 K, immersed in liquid He.
- Aligns proton magnetic moments and produces net longitudinal magnetization M₀.

**2. Gradient Coils (X, Y, Z):**
- Three sets of coils producing linearly varying fields G_x, G_y, G_z superimposed on B₀.
- **Slice-select:** Excites a thin slice.
- **Frequency-encoding:** Larmor frequency becomes position-dependent along one axis during readout.
- **Phase-encoding:** Stepped phase shifts before readout encode the perpendicular axis.
- Together, they fill **k-space** row-by-row, allowing image reconstruction.

**3. RF Coils (Transmitter + Receiver):**
- Transmitter coil produces B₁ field at ω₀, tipping magnetization by flip angle α.
- Receiver coil (often separate surface coil) detects the FID via Faraday induction.
- Phased-array coils improve SNR (parallel imaging, e.g., SENSE, GRAPPA).

**4. Computer and Reconstruction:**
- Performs 2D Inverse Fourier Transform on k-space data.
- Generates the final image for display.

The complete workflow: **Patient in B₀ → M_z builds → RF pulse tips M to xy plane → precession induces FID in receive coil → gradients encode position → 2D IFFT → image displayed.**

*(Award 1 mark for each subsystem explained; 1 mark for the workflow integration.)*

---

### Model Solution — (b)

**T1 (Longitudinal / Spin–Lattice) Relaxation:**

After a 90° RF pulse, M_z = 0. As the excited protons transfer energy back to the surrounding molecular lattice (thermal reservoir), M_z recovers exponentially:

$$M_z(t) = M_0 \left(1 - e^{-t/T_1}\right)$$

- **T1** is the time for M_z to recover to 63% of M₀.
- Tissues with **short T1** (fat) appear **bright** on T1-weighted images.
- Tissues with **long T1** (CSF, water) appear **dark** on T1-weighted images.

**T2 (Transverse / Spin–Spin) Relaxation:**

Immediately after the 90° pulse, all proton spins precess **in phase**, giving maximum M_xy. Random local field fluctuations from neighbouring spins cause **dephasing**:

$$M_{xy}(t) = M_0 \, e^{-t/T_2}$$

- **T2** is the time for M_xy to decay to 37% of M₀.
- Tissues with **long T2** (CSF, edema) appear **bright** on T2-weighted images.
- Tissues with **short T2** (muscle, liver) appear **dark** on T2-weighted images.

**Pulse-Parameter Trade-off for Contrast:**

| Weighting | TR | TE | Looks bright |
|---|---|---|---|
| **T1-weighted** | **Short** | **Short** | Fat, subacute blood, melanin, gadolinium |
| **T2-weighted** | **Long** | **Long** | Water, edema, inflammation, tumors, CSF |

- **TR (Repetition Time):** Time between successive 90° pulses. Short TR → incomplete T1 recovery → T1 contrast.
- **TE (Echo Time):** Time from 90° pulse to echo peak. Long TE → more T2 decay → T2 contrast.

> **Clinical example:** A **glioma** is dark on T1-W and bright on T2-W due to high water content — KTU examiners may ask for such examples.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]**

**(a) Explain the principle of Positron Emission Tomography (PET). With a block diagram, describe the working of a PET scanner.** *(7 marks)*

**(b) Explain the working of SPECT imaging. Compare PET and SPECT with respect to tracer, detection principle, spatial resolution, sensitivity, and clinical applications.** *(7 marks)*

### Model Solution — (a)

**Principle:**
A positron-emitting radiopharmaceutical (e.g., **¹⁸F-FDG**) is injected into the patient. The emitted positron travels a short distance (1–3 mm) in tissue, then **annihilates** with an electron, producing **two 511 keV gamma photons** that travel in **opposite directions (180° apart)** — by conservation of energy and momentum. A ring of scintillation detectors surrounding the patient detects these photon pairs in **coincidence**, defining a **Line of Response (LOR)** along which the annihilation occurred.

**Block Diagram — PET Scanner:**

*(See Section 4.2 for full Mermaid block diagram; key elements listed below)*

1. **Patient with injected ¹⁸F-FDG tracer**
2. **Detector Ring** (multiple rings of BGO / LSO / LYSO crystals coupled to SiPMs or PMTs)
3. **Coincidence Processor** — accepts only events within ~6–12 ns window
4. **Attenuation, Scatter, Random Correction**
5. **Image Reconstruction** (3D OSEM or FBP)
6. **Display and Quantification** (SUV map)

**Step-by-step Workflow:**
1. Cyclotron produces ¹⁸F (t½ = 109.8 min).
2. Automated synthesis module produces ¹⁸F-FDG.
3. Patient is injected and waits 45–60 min for tracer uptake.
4. Patient is positioned in the PET bore.
5. Detectors record millions of coincident events.
6. Computer sorts LORs into sinograms.
7. Iterative reconstruction yields 3-D image of FDG distribution.
8. SUV is computed voxel-by-voxel for quantification.

---

### Model Solution — (b)

**SPECT Working:**

1. A **single-photon-emitting radiopharmaceutical** (e.g., Tc-99m, 140 keV) is administered.
2. The patient is positioned in front of a **rotating gamma camera (Anger camera)**.
3. Emitted gamma photons pass through a **parallel-hole lead collimator** (which selects direction).
4. They strike a **NaI(Tl) scintillator**, producing a flash of light.
5. **PMTs + Anger logic** determine position and energy.
6. The head rotates 360° around the patient, acquiring 60–120 projections.
7. **Filtered back-projection or OSEM** reconstructs a 3-D image.
8. **SPECT/CT hybrid** provides attenuation correction and anatomical fusion.

**Comparison Table — PET vs SPECT:**

| Parameter | PET | SPECT |
|---|---|---|
| Tracer | β⁺ emitter (¹⁸F, ¹¹C, ¹³N, ¹⁵O) | γ emitter (Tc-99m, Tl-201, I-123) |
| Photon energy | **511 keV** | **140 keV** |
| Detection principle | **Coincidence** (electronic collimation) | **Physical collimator** |
| Sensitivity | **~100× higher** than SPECT | Low (collimator absorbs >99%) |
| Spatial resolution | **4–5 mm** | **7–10 mm** |
| Quantification | True SUV (counts → Bq/mL) | Semi-quantitative |
| Cost | Higher (needs cyclotron nearby) | Lower (uses generator) |
| Half-life of tracer | Very short (² to 110 min) | Longer (6 h for Tc-99m) |
| Clinical uses | Oncology, neurology, cardiology | Cardiac perfusion, bone, thyroid |
| Attenuation correction | Excellent (TOF + CT) | Needs SPECT/CT |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall #1:** Students often write "PET uses X-rays" or "SPECT uses positrons" — completely wrong. Memorize: **PET → Positron → 511 keV**; **SPECT → Single photon → 140 keV**.
> **Pitfall #2:** Forgetting that **PET's coincidence detection is electronic collimation**, while **SPECT's collimator is physical** — this is the #1 reason for the sensitivity gap.
> **Pitfall #3:** In 14-mark answers, students sometimes skip the **attenuation correction** discussion. Examiners explicitly look for this in PET/SPECT questions.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

### **MRI Essentials**
- ⭐ **Larmor equation:** $\omega_0 = \gamma B_0$, with $\gamma/2\pi = 42.58$ MHz/T for ¹H.
- ⭐ **At 1.5 T, f₀ = 63.87 MHz; at 3 T, f₀ = 127.74 MHz.**
- ⭐ **Three relaxation parameters:** ρ (proton density), T1 (longitudinal, ms–s), T2 (transverse, ms).
- ⭐ **Three contrast weightings:** T1-W (short TR, short TE), T2-W (long TR, long TE), PD-W (long TR, short TE).
- ⭐ **Three gradients for spatial encoding:** slice-select, frequency-encode, phase-encode → k-space.
- ⭐ **Image = 2D Inverse Fourier Transform of k-space.**
- ⭐ **Key sequences:** Spin Echo, Gradient Echo, Inversion Recovery (FLAIR, STIR), EPI (fMRI).
- ⭐ **No ionizing radiation** — uses RF at Larmor frequency.
- ⭐ **Main magnet is superconducting** (Nb-Ti at 4 K in liquid He).

### **PET Essentials**
- ⭐ **Mechanism:** β⁺ decay → positron → annihilates with electron → 2 × **511 keV photons at 180°**.
- ⭐ **Coincidence detection** (electronic collimation, ~10 ns window).
- ⭐ **Common tracer:** ¹⁸F-FDG (t½ = 109.8 min).
- ⭐ **Quantification:** SUV = activity / (injected dose / body weight).
- ⭐ **Corrections:** attenuation, scatter, randoms, dead-time, normalization.
- ⭐ **Reconstruction:** 2D/3D FBP or iterative OSEM.
- ⭐ **Resolution ~4–5 mm, sensitivity ~100× SPECT.**

### **SPECT Essentials**
- ⭐ **Tracer:** ⁹⁹ᵐTc (140 keV) from ⁹⁹Mo/⁹⁹ᵐTc generator; t½ = 6 h.
- ⭐ **Gamma camera components:** Collimator → NaI(Tl) scintillator → PMT array → Anger position logic.
- ⭐ **Collimator absorbs >99% of photons** (the limiting factor for sensitivity).
- ⭐ **Acquisition:** rotate head 360° in 60–120 projections.
- ⭐ **Reconstruction:** Filtered back-projection (FBP) or OSEM.
- ⭐ **SPECT/CT hybrid** standard for attenuation correction.
- ⭐ **Resolution ~7–10 mm**; lower than PET.

### **Cross-Cutting "Examiner Traps"**
- ⭐ **"PET has higher sensitivity than SPECT"** — TRUE, because of coincidence (no collimator loss).
- ⭐ **"MRI is best for bone"** — FALSE. CT is best for bone; MRI is best for soft tissue.
- ⭐ **"FDG uptake is high in all tumors"** — mostly true for metabolically active tumors; prostate and some renal cancers are FDG-poor.
- ⭐ **"T2*-weighted imaging" is different from T2-weighted** — T2* includes static field inhomogeneity, important in BOLD fMRI and hemorrhage imaging.
- ⭐ **Larmor equation appears in ~40% of KTU MRI numericals** — memorize γ for ¹H.

### **Quick-Reference Numbers for Numerical Problems**
- γ (¹H) = **2.675 × 10⁸ rad·s⁻¹·T⁻¹** = **42.58 MHz/T**
- m_e c² = **511 keV** (PET photon energy)
- Tc-99m γ = **140 keV** (SPECT photon energy)
- c = **3 × 10⁸ m/s**
- ħ = **1.055 × 10⁻³⁴ J·s**
- Clinical B₀ = **1.5 T or 3 T**; research = **7 T**
- f₀(1.5 T) = **63.87 MHz**; f₀(3 T) = **127.74 MHz**; f₀(7 T) = **298.06 MHz**

> **Final KTU Tip:** Always draw a **labelled block diagram** in 7-mark MRI/PET/SPECT questions. Even if your explanation is imperfect, the diagram can earn you 1–2 marks. Use the diagrams in Section 4 as your model.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Radiation Therapy
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT412 - BIOMEDICAL INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Radiation Therapy

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Radiation Therapy (Radiotherapy):** A clinical modality of controlled medical intervention that uses precisely measured **ionizing radiation** (photons, electrons, protons, or heavier ions) to deliver a curative, palliative, or adjuvant **absorbed dose** to a defined target volume (tumour) while sparing the surrounding healthy tissue within tolerance limits prescribed by the radiation oncologist.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, radiation therapy is classified under **Therapeutic Biomedical Instrumentation** as a *high-energy, externally controlled or internally implanted radiation delivery system* that operates on the principle of **DNA double-strand break induction** in malignant cells.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "Surgical Sunlight":**
> Imagine a magnifying glass that focuses ordinary sunlight to burn a single ant on a sheet of paper, leaving the paper mostly intact. **Radiation therapy works in the same way**: a beam of high-energy photons (or particles) is geometrically shaped, oriented, and modulated so that its destructive energy converges on a tumour mass. The challenge, like in the analogy, is that the "paper" (healthy tissue) must survive the exposure. This is achieved through *beam shaping, fractionation, IMRT, and IGRT*.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Radiation Quantities (SI Units)**
> - **Absorbed Dose (D):** measured in **Gray (Gy)** = 1 J/kg. (Legacy unit: **rad**, where 1 Gy = 100 rad.)
> - **Equivalent Dose (H):** measured in **Sievert (Sv)** = H = D × W_R (radiation weighting factor).
> - **Exposure (X):** measured in **Coulomb/kg** (C/kg). Legacy unit: Roentgen (R), where 1 R = 2.58 × 10⁻⁴ C/kg.
> - **Activity (A):** measured in **Becquerel (Bq)** = 1 disintegration/sec. Legacy unit: Curie (Ci), where 1 Ci = 3.7 × 10¹⁰ Bq.
> - **Linear Energy Transfer (LET):** measured in **keV/µm**.
> - **Mass Energy Absorption Coefficient (µ_en/ρ):** measured in **m²/kg**.
> - **Mass Attenuation Coefficient (µ/ρ):** measured in **m²/kg**.

### 1.4 Two Broad Clinical Categories

| Category | Location of Source | KTU Subtopic |
|----------|-------------------|--------------|
| **Teletherapy** (External Beam) | Source is *outside* the patient | Cobalt-60, LINAC, CyberKnife |
| **Brachytherapy** (Internal) | Source is *placed inside/near* the tumour | LDR, HDR, Intracavitary, Interstitial |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Physics of Interaction — Why Radiation Kills Cells

The biological effect of ionizing radiation is governed by the **interaction cross-section** between the incident radiation and tissue constituents (primarily water, which constitutes ~80% of soft tissue). The principal photon interactions are:

1. **Photoelectric Effect** — dominant at low energies (< 50 keV); strongly dependent on **Z³** of the absorber.
2. **Compton Scattering** — dominant in the **clinical energy range (100 keV – 10 MeV)**; the workhorse of megavoltage therapy.
3. **Pair Production** — dominant at high energies (> 1.022 MeV); produces an electron-positron pair in the Coulomb field of a nucleus.
4. **Photodisintegration** — very high energy (> 10 MeV); rarely used clinically.

For electrons: dominant interactions are **ionization, bremsstrahlung, and elastic scattering**.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Operational Logic of a Teletherapy Unit

> [!NOTE]
> The operational chain is: **Source → Collimation → Beam Modification → Patient Alignment → Dose Delivery → Verification**.

- **Step 1 — Source Generation:** Photons are produced either by a radioactive isotope (⁶⁰Co) or by electron–target interaction in a LINAC.
- **Step 2 — Primary Collimation:** Defines the maximum circular field (~ 35–50 cm diameter at SSD = 80 cm).
- **Step 3 — Secondary Collimation:** Movable jaws (independent X and Y jaws) shape a rectangular field.
- **Step 4 — Beam Modification:** Wedges, compensators, MLC, and bolus alter dose distribution.
- **Step 5 — Isocentric Mount:** The gantry rotates about a fixed point (isocentre), with the tumour placed exactly at the isocentre.
- **Step 6 — Dose Monitoring:** Transmission ionization chambers (monitor chambers) measure **Monitor Units (MU)**.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity / Law | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Exposure Rate Constant (Γ)** | $\dot{X} = \Gamma \cdot \dfrac{A}{d^{2}}$ | R·cm²/(mCi·h) | For ⁶⁰Co: $\Gamma \approx 0.965$ R·cm²/(mCi·h) |
| 2 | **Inverse Square Law** | $I \propto \dfrac{1}{d^{2}}$ | — | Applies to point source; **most-tested formula** |
| 3 | **Exponential Attenuation** | $I = I_{0} \, e^{-\mu x}$ | — | Linear attenuation coefficient $\mu$ in m⁻¹ |
| 4 | **Mass Attenuation Coefficient** | $\dfrac{\mu}{\rho}$ | m²/kg | Used in heterogeneous media |
| 5 | **Absorbed Dose from Exposure** | $D = X \cdot \left(\dfrac{W}{e}\right) \cdot \left(\dfrac{\mu_{en}}{\rho}\right)_{tissue}^{air}$ | Gy | $W/e = 33.97$ J/C |
| 6 | **Equivalent Dose** | $H = D \cdot W_{R}$ | Sv | Photons: $W_R = 1$; Electrons: $W_R = 1$; $\alpha$: $W_R = 20$ |
| 7 | **TMR (Tissue–Max Ratio)** | $TMR(d, S) = \dfrac{D(d, S)}{D(d_{max}, S)}$ | dimensionless | Used in isocentric dose calc |
| 8 | **PDD (Percentage Depth Dose)** | $PDD(d) = \dfrac{D(d)}{D(d_{max})} \times 100\,\%$ | % | Peak at $d_{max}$ (1.5 cm for ⁶⁰Co) |
| 9 | **SSD vs SAD Setup** | $PDD = \dfrac{(f+d_{max})^{2}}{(f+d)^{2}} \cdot TMR \cdot 100$ | % | $f$ = SAD (typically 80 or 100 cm) |
| 10 | **Half-Life (Radioactive)** | $A = A_{0}\, e^{-\lambda t}$, $\lambda = \dfrac{\ln 2}{T_{1/2}}$ | Bq | ⁶⁰Co: $T_{1/2} = 5.27$ yr |
| 11 | **SSD to SAD Conversion (Mayneord F)** | $F = \left(\dfrac{SSD + d_{max}}{SSD + d}\right)^{2}$ | — | Empirical correction |
| 12 | **Brachytherapy Cumulative Dose (Manchester System)** | $D = \dfrac{\text{Total implant strength}}{\text{Treated volume}} \cdot \text{Radial dose function}$ | Gy | Used in interstitial implants |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** These formulas are the backbone of **Treatment Planning Systems (TPS)** such as Eclipse (Varian), Pinnacle (Philips), and RayStation. Every clinical dose calculation in modern radiotherapy reduces to a discrete solution of these equations over a 3D voxel grid (typically 2–3 mm resolution).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Inverse Square Law from Point-Source Geometry

Consider an isotropic point source emitting $N_0$ photons per second uniformly into $4\pi$ steradians. Let $n$ = number of photons crossing a spherical surface of radius $d$.

**Step 1: Flux per unit area**

$$
\text{Flux } \Phi(d) = \frac{N_0}{4\pi d^{2}}
$$

**Step 2: Intensity (dose rate) is proportional to flux**

$$
\dot{D}(d) = k \cdot \Phi(d) = \frac{k \cdot N_0}{4\pi d^{2}}
$$

**Step 3: Ratio at two distances $d_1$ and $d_2$**

$$
\frac{\dot{D}(d_1)}{\dot{D}(d_2)} = \left(\frac{d_2}{d_1}\right)^{2}
$$

> **Logic:** This is why doubling the SSD from 80 cm to 160 cm reduces the dose rate by a factor of **4** — the most fundamental principle tested in KTU problems.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Calculation of Absorbed Dose Rate for a ⁶⁰Co Source

**Given:** A ⁶⁰Co source has activity $A$ (in Bq) and emits two γ-photons of **1.17 MeV and 1.33 MeV** per disintegration.

**Step 1: Average photon energy per disintegration**

$$
E_{avg} = \frac{1.17 + 1.33}{2} = 1.25\ \text{MeV}
$$

**Step 2: Energy released per second at distance $d$ (m)**

$$
\dot{E}(d) = A \cdot E_{avg} \cdot 1.602 \times 10^{-13}\ \text{J/MeV}
$$

**Step 3: Energy fluence rate at distance $d$**

$$
\Psi(d) = \frac{A \cdot E_{avg} \cdot 1.602 \times 10^{-13}}{4\pi d^{2}}\ \text{J/(m²·s)}
$$

**Step 4: Absorbed dose rate in tissue**

$$
\dot{D}_{tissue}(d) = \Psi(d) \cdot \left(\frac{\mu_{en}}{\rho}\right)_{tissue}
$$

**Step 5: Inserting numerical values for soft tissue** at 1.25 MeV: $(\mu_{en}/\rho)_{tissue} \approx 0.0296\ \text{m}^2/\text{kg}$.

$$
\dot{D}_{tissue}(d) = \frac{A \cdot 1.25 \cdot 1.602 \times 10^{-13} \cdot 0.0296}{4\pi d^{2}}
$$

**Step 6: Simplifying with $A$ in Bq and $d$ in metres, dose rate in Gy/s:**

$$
\dot{D}_{tissue}(d) \approx \frac{6.0 \times 10^{-16} \cdot A}{d^{2}}\ \text{Gy/s}
$$

> **Logic:** This compact form lets us compute dose at any distance by substitution. For example, a 5000 Ci source at 80 cm SSD gives $\dot{D} \approx 1.6$ Gy/min — a typical clinical ⁶⁰Co output.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — SSD → SAD Conversion of PDD (Isocentric Setup)

In **SAD setup**, the tumour is placed at the isocentre (typically 100 cm from the source). The PDD formula must be referenced to $d_{max}$ at the isocentre.

**Step 1: Geometric relationship between SSD and SAD**

$$
f_{SAD} = SSD + d
$$

**Step 2: Effective depth at the isocentre**

$$
d_{iso} = SSD_{ref} - SSD_{new} + d_{max,ref}
$$

**Step 3: Mayneord F-factor correction**

$$
F = \left(\frac{f_{SAD} + d_{max}}{f_{SAD} + d}\right)^{2}
$$

**Step 4: Final PDD conversion**

$$
PDD_{SAD}(d) = \frac{PDD_{SSD}(d) \cdot F}{100}
$$

> **Logic:** KTU frequently tests numerical problems asking to convert SSD-based PDD tables to SAD-based ones, or vice versa. The F-factor accounts for the change in inverse-square contribution.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Major Radiation Therapy Modalities

| Parameter | ⁶⁰Co Teletherapy | LINAC (6 MV) | LINAC (15 MV) | HDR Brachy (¹⁹²Ir) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Source type | Radioisotope | Electron beam on W target | Electron beam on W target | Radioisotope (afterloader) |
| Energy | 1.17 + 1.33 MeV γ | 6 MeV X-ray (peak) | 15 MeV X-ray (peak) | 0.38 MeV (avg) γ |
| $d_{max}$ (depth) | 0.5 cm | 1.5 cm | 3.0 cm | N/A (volumetric) |
| Dose rate at isocentre | ~ 1.5–2.5 Gy/min | Up to 6 Gy/min (FFF: 24) | Up to 6 Gy/min | > 12 Gy/hr (HDR) |
| Beam profile | Flat (but penumbra) | Flat, sharp penumbra | Flat, sharp penumbra | Spherical / line source |
| Cost | Low | High | Very High | Moderate |
| Maintenance | Source replacement every 5.27 yr | Klystron/magnetron, dosimetry QA | Same as 6 MV | Source change every ~3 months |
| Skin sparing | Good | Excellent | Excellent | Not applicable |
| Penumbra width | ~ 1.5–2.0 cm | ~ 0.5 cm | ~ 0.5 cm | N/A |

### 3.5 Derivation 4 — Brachytherapy Manchester System Dose Rate

For an **interstitial implant** with total source strength $S_k$ (in U, where 1 U = 1 µg-Ra-equivalent):

**Step 1: Air kerma strength definition**

$$
S_k = \dot{K}_{\delta}(d_{ref}) \cdot d_{ref}^{2}
$$

**Step 2: Dose rate at distance $r$ from a line source**

$$
\dot{D}(r) = \frac{\Lambda \cdot S_k}{r^{2}} \cdot g(r) \cdot \phi(r)
$$

where $\Lambda$ is the dose-rate constant (cGy·h⁻¹·U⁻¹), $g(r)$ is the radial dose function, and $\phi(r)$ is the anisotropy function.

**Step 3: Total dose over implant time $T$**

$$
D_{total} = \dot{D}(r) \cdot T
$$

> **Logic:** The Manchester system prescribes 70 Gy in 6–7 days for LDR ¹³⁷Cs or ¹⁹²Ir implants of head/neck cancers, and HDR delivers equivalent BED in minutes using ¹⁹²Ir stepping sources.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Block Diagram of a Linear Accelerator (LINAC)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph A1["High Voltage Generation"]
        HV["HT Power Supply (10-50 kV)"]
        MOD["Modulator (PFN)"]
        KLY["Klystron / Magnetron RF Source (3 GHz)"]
    end

    subgraph A2["Electron Injection"]
        EC["Electron Gun (Heated Cathode)"]
        AC1["Accelerator Waveguide"]
        AC2["Bending Magnet 270 deg"]
    end

    subgraph A3["Beam Delivery"]
        TGT["X-ray Target (W, 1-2 cm thick)"]
        FC["Primary Foil / Flattening Filter"]
        IC1["Monitor Chamber 1 (Transmission)"]
        IC2["Monitor Chamber 2 (Backup)"]
        MLC["Multi-Leaf Collimator (80-120 leaves)"]
        JAW["Secondary Jaws X and Y"]
        WDG["Wedge / Compensator"]
        PAT["Patient on Couch"]
    end

    HV --> MOD --> KLY --> AC1
    EC --> AC1 --> AC2
    AC2 -->|Electron mode| PAT
    AC2 -->|Photon mode| TGT
    TGT --> FC --> IC1 --> IC2 --> MLC --> JAW --> WDG --> PAT
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The flow goes from **High Voltage Generation** → **Electron Injection** → **Beam Delivery**. Photon mode requires the X-ray target; electron mode bypasses the target and uses a scattering foil instead.

### 4.2 Functional Architecture of a Cobalt-60 Teletherapy Unit

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SRC["Source Assembly"]
        S1["Pencil Source 60Co (5-15 cm3)"]
        S2["Source Drawer (ON/OFF Pneumatic)"]
        S3["Source Head Shielding (Pb + W)"]
    end

    subgraph COL["Collimation System"]
        C1["Primary Collimator (fixed)"]
        C2["Secondary Collimator (variable)"]
        C3["Tertiary Collimator (blocks/MLC)"]
    end

    subgraph MECH["Mechanical Assembly"]
        M1["Gantry (rotates 360 deg)"]
        M2["Collimator Head"]
        M3["Treatment Couch (6 DOF)"]
        M4["Isocentre (intersection of axes)"]
    end

    subgraph CTRL["Control & Safety"]
        K1["Control Console"]
        K2["Door Interlocks"]
        K3["Dosimetry Timer"]
        K4["Treatment Record & Verify (R and V)"]
    end

    S1 --> S2 --> S3 --> C1 --> C2 --> C3
    M1 --> M2
    M3 --> M4
    K1 --> S2
    K1 --> M1
    K1 --> M3
    K2 --> S2
    K3 --> S2
    K4 --> K1
```

> **Functional Note:** The *isocentre* is the mechanical pivot where gantry, collimator, and couch axes intersect — all calibrated within a **1 mm radius sphere** (per IEC 60976).

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Brachytherapy Remote Afterloading System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SAFE["Source Safe (Storage)"]
        SH["Shielded Tungsten Safe"]
    end

    subgraph MOT["Drive Mechanism"]
        ST["Stepper Motor (1 mm stepping)"]
        CAB["Drive Cable (steel)"]
        CK1["Check Source (dummy)"]
    end

    subgraph PAT["Patient Applicators"]
        AP1["Intracavitary (gynecological)"]
        AP2["Interstitial (needles/catheters)"]
        AP3["Surface Mold"]
    end

    subgraph PLAN["Treatment Planning"]
        TPS["TPS (Plato / Oncentra)"]
        DOS["Dwell Position and Time"]
    end

    CTRL["Console with DICOM Interface"]
    SAFE --> MOT
    MOT --> PAT
    TPS --> CTRL
    CTRL --> MOT
    CK1 --> MOT
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Treatment plan from **TPS** is fed to the **Console**, which drives the **Stepper Motor** to push the source from the **Safe** through the **Cable** into patient **Applicators** for prescribed dwell times at each position.

### 4.4 Dose Distribution Comparison — Photon vs Electron Beam

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph P1["6 MV Photon Beam PDD"]
        P1a["Surface Dose ~ 15 percent"]
        P1b["dmax = 1.5 cm (buildup)"]
        P1c["Gradual fall-off beyond dmax"]
        P1d["Exit dose 30-50 percent"]
    end

    subgraph E1["12 MeV Electron Beam PDD"]
        E1a["Surface dose ~ 85 percent"]
        E1b["dmax = 2.5 cm (shallow)"]
        E1c["Rapid fall-off after Rp"]
        E1d["Rp approx 5 cm in tissue"]
        E1e["Practical range R90 percent"]
    end

    P1a --> P1b --> P1c --> P1d
    E1a --> E1b --> E1c --> E1d --> E1e
```

> **Clinical Use:** Photons are preferred for **deep-seated tumours**; electrons are used for **superficial lesions** (skin, chest wall boost) because of their sharp distal fall-off.
